title: The Emergence of Life on Earth
author: Iris Fry
description: Scientific history of how life began on earth
memories: Analogy to SETI: what if we heard the exact sequence of the human genome?!; Really technical, hardly readable… basically a textbook; intro to the idea of metabolism vs. replicator; decent philosophy chapter at end; awkward chapter about life on Mars after that;

title: Origins of Life
author: Freeman Dyson
description: Theory of double origin of life (DNA Last)
memories: Reproduce vs. replicate; Weird model at the end; decent overview at beginning; fairly unmemorable but ambitious

title: The Origin of Life
author: Paul Davies
description: All scientific and related issues to life  origin
memories: Really great writing; comprehensive; error catastrophe; chirality matches in DNA and protein; life under sea

title: Consciousness: A Very Short Introduction
author: Susan Blackmore
description: Overview of consciousness debate
memories: Super unfair to most ideas; believes that consciousness isn't real; objective vs. subjective consciousness; higher-order thought = consciousness?; impossibility of objective study

title: Conversations on Consciousness
author: Susan Blackmore
description: Overview of consciousness debate
memories: Vilayanur Ramachandran; Interview  based, but good; the author is annoying but interviewed good people; staircase with loop at top as idea; Higher-Order Thought; Consciousness is the fundamental thing in the universe; quantum soul; "epistemic asymmetry" because we can only observe selves

author: George Orwell
description: Big Brother
memories: Scary idea of future, almost seems possible; doublethink, crimestop, panopticon; complete breakdown of a person

title: This Explains Everything
author: Various
description: Deep, beautiful, elegant scientific ideas
memories: Tons of cool ideas; above planck density,clocks are destroyed before they can measure time and no measurement; stretching a rubber band makes it heavier

title: Physics in Mind
author: Werner B. Loewenstein
description: Brain physics
memories: Quantum stuff at the end is halfway decent, otherwise unreadable; quantum computers are cool

title: On Writing
author: Stephen King
description: Stephen King's autobiography/fiction tips
memories: Impossible to put down; great stories, awesome writing; inspiration, I want to write a novel now; door closed vs. open; Ideal Reader;

title: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
author: Lewis Carroll
description: Trippy journey through kid's imagination
memories: Different than the story I remember; commentary on language and ambiguity; lots of wordplay; jokes kids wouldn't get

title: Consciousness
author: Eva Herr
description: Pseudoscience; quantum consciousness
memories: Horrible book - one sensible person interviewed and he was super confused by the author; only useful thing was awareness -> cognition -> consciousness

title: The Elements of Style
author: E.B. White
description: Writing well
memories: Awesome; super good tips; need to internalize all; "flammable" is a fake word

title: Invisible Monsters
author: Chuck Palahniuk
description: Beauty becomes beast
memories: Everybody is transgender; really awesome, fucked up book; jumping around in time; "Give me… flash"; About making intentional mistakes to counter human drive

title: 10% Happier
author: Dan Harris
description: "I thought of a catchy meditation slogan"
memories: 90% autobiography, 5% meditation, 5% douchebaggery; but his life is actually pretty awesome, so his stories are cool. He's just kinda a douche

title: Fully Present
author: Smalley & Winston
description: Science, art, practice of meditation
memories: Good techniques; tension in stomach; pay attention to whole body; question emotions behind thoughts;

title: A More Beautiful Question
author: Warren Berger
description: Questioning: how, why
memories: Why, What If, How; Make more broad or narrow to improve and ID assumptions; five whys;  questions as key to disagreement and debate; sit down with people, empathise and understand

title: The Rosie Project
author: Graeme Simsion
description: Aspergers professor finds love
memories: Learns to dance without music; eating schedule/efficiency; "deleting" the shower from his workout schedule

title: Talking Diabetes with Owls
author: David Sedaris
description: Hilarious short stories
memories: Sedaris writes down everything that happens in his life and did a bunch of drugs apparently; so witty and observant

title: The Fault in Our Stars
author: John Green
description: Cancer girl meets cancer boy; sadness
memories: So sad; makes you love the characters; they are totally themselves the whole time and it is awesome; they egg the blind kid's ex; movie very true to book; even achieving dreams won't be enough because you'll think it could have been done better; sometimes the universe wants to be noticed

title: The (Honest) Truth About Dishonesty
author: Dan Ariely
description: Everybody lies, but just a little
memories: Everybody lies, but only enough so they can reap some benefits AND keep the impression that they're a good person; "fudge factor" approximately 20%; money and probability of getting caught have little to do with it; creativity and lying go hand in hand because they're better at justifying fibs; depleted people lie; watching others do things makes you more likely to do them

title: Born to Run
author: Christopher McDougall
description: Running barefoot with Tarahumara
memories: Such gripping storytelling; I need to buy thinner running shoes; Caballo Blanco sounds like he figured something out; crazy ultra races sound like a super fun subculture; effect of love on running and physical performance; Kasparov intentionally loses a queen, playing intentionally from behind to get creative and psychologically ahead; easy, light, smooth, and fast; 19=64 for running

title: Breakfast of Champions
author: Kurt Vonnegut
description: America sucks, and so on
memories: Interesting satirical critique of America; and so on; describes things really bluntly and without context; story not all that interesting, maybe if I read more Vonnegut; inserts himself as a character, blows the mind of his main character; bird gets three wishes: open cage, open window... put me back in cage.; Roses are red, and ready for plucking, you're sixteen, and ready for high school; questions why creator of the universe made rattlesnakes so deadly; people do stupid shit because it's what they read and watch movies about - I wonder how media portrayal has affected public behavior, probably impossible to know; Vonnegut doesn't think the world is meant to be reasonable - maybe the worldis, humans definitely aren't; machines are evolving faster than humans

title: American Gods
author: Neil Gaiman
description: Gods still exist, and they're gullible
memories: Cool idea, all gods that have ever existed are still alive; can be different in different places (Odin vs. Wednesday); Shadow is a badass; interesting straddle of real and fake, especially with the sidebars on other gods; liked the alternate God dimension; wife character really interesting, so were the Vorya sisters; people only fight over imaginary things; religions are metaphors

title: An Astronaut's Guide to Life on Earth
author: Col. Chris Hadfield
description: Being an astronaut would be tough, fun
memories: Sweat the small stuff; what can kill me next; power of preparation; cool space things - weighted treadmills, dome where you can see everything, "EVA"; weird astronaut rituals like peeing on the tire, signing the door; I want to fly; luck is super important in making it huge like that; how is he already retired; son is the unsung hero, making him go viral; aim to be a zero

title: The Happiness Project
author: Gretchen Rubin
description: (Most) moms can be happy too
memories: I was clearly not the audience for this book; some interesting ideas; applaud the dedication; she is very honest about who she is; the acknowledgements in the book that the book was a bad idea was weird; she REALLY reads a lot; shows that pure dedication + popular idea + pre-existing fame precludes the need for actually good ideas or writing; action follows feeling, we can control action but not feeling;

title: The Mind of God
author: Paul Davies
description: Why can we understand math and physics?
memories: Fun book, constantly mind-blowing; laws are inductive, not fact; all religions basically agree that the universe can't make itself; SUN RISING IS FAITH; randomness and uncertainty seem to cause free will; no system of understanding is complete, thanks Godel; does God have free will? If yes, could have chosen a worse world. But if not, then limited too, so he's either not perfectly rational or not perfectly good; synthetic propositions are in the world, analytic are outside, can't derive synthetic from only analytic; "God is subtle, not malicious" - Einstein

title: Doctor Sleep
author: Stephen King
description: Sequel to The Shining; people still shine
memories: First Stephen King I've ever read, super long but totally worth it; couldn't put it down, finished at 3:00AM; spoons at birthday party to ceiling so abra could be like the magician; ghosties; AA; "canny" aka cocaine; secrets are worse in than out; telling truths about the world to make the crazy stuff seem more real; boxes to store bad memories (or ghosts to kill your rivals); true knot drinks steam; "cycling" after knot dies; abra's temper; find your center outside is wheel; when the student is ready the teacher will arrive

title: Against the Gods
author: Peter L. Bernstein
description: Risk and stats evolved with people
memories: A little too technical to be fun and easy to read; cool story in the end; all about rationality breakdown; stats started with gambling, figure out how to divide winnings if unfinished games; uncertainty = no one right answer; rosy philosophy and economics and stuff were SEVERELY affected by the first world war: art, literature, music, Einstein, Freud; ambiguity aversion; personal side to acceptable risk; nearly reasonable world

title: The Martian
author: Andy Weir
description: Stranded astronaut grows potatoes on Mars
memories: Awesomely sciencey; realistic even with a crazy premise; hab explosion; growing potatoes; fixing things; tricking out his rover; crazy ending with the flyby rescue; NASA seems really cool; In space, nobody can hear you scream like a little girl

title: Miracles
author: Eric Metaxas
description: Miracles are real, Metaxas' friends claim
memories: Super interesting first part about miracles of the earth and such existing; second part talked about lots of different kinds of miracles; the miracle stories were actually kinda scary; the red and blue looking huge angel visions; keys on the middle of the car windshield; "brought back from dead"; how could they have just happened; ridiculous analogies are funny; no choice but to figure out these things

title: The Universe in a Single Atom
author: HH Dalai Lama
description: Being the Dalai Lama would be legit
memories: Pretty in-depth; not very thorough or simple enough explanations; lots of private conversations because who doesn't wanna talk to the Lama himself; lots of parallels between Buddhist thinking and modern physics

title: Emergence
author: Steven Johnson
description: 2001 book predicts emergent tech well
memories: Ant pheromones; brains evolved to be social; theory of mind = consciousness; sim city is emergent; cities are boring but apparently cool as long as they have sidewalks; stupid things can be smart with the right rules; five rules (dope.); minds are mostly pattern recognition.

title: Think Like a Freak
author: Levitt & Dubner
description: How to think good
memories: Accessible writing style; still really intelligent references and obviously smart; short book - huge type; basic ideas were nothing new but things people need to learn;

title: The Phantom Tollbooth
author: Norton Juster
description: Bored boy learns to love life
memories: Phenomenal writing: so clever and interesting; lots of great life lessons and wisdom; personally influential; need to show this to my kids someday; tall/short/fat/skinny man; Mathemagician and King Azaz; so many literal things (Watchdog) and wordplay; people waste time because there's so much of it; the only reason to go from one place to another is to see what's in between

author: Stephen King
description: Time traveler saves JFK, rips time-space
memories: Amazing writing; historically really interesting and crazy research; the past harmonizes; fights back if you try to change it; strings; the love story was amazing, injury reminds me of invisible monsters; dancing the Lindy Hop; talent shows; Of Mice and Men; the past is obdurate; saves the mom and kids the second time with cold-blooded murder of the dad; women's rights interesting, school board won't defend Sadie even after she's mauled by her suicidal and delusional ex-husband

title: Science and Religion in Quest of Truth
author: John Polkinghorne
description: Scientifically believing in God is possible
memories: Interesting mind - very well grounded in the science side but very Bible-y and philosophical about faith issues; science as metaphor/map; circularity between experiment and theory; "background problem" of eliminating experimental noise is a judgment call; models are not total explanations... just models; NOMA is dumb, must be "consonantly related"; is uncertainty in physics a measurement thing, or true about the world? probably true about world; strings are 16 degrees of magnitude smaller than we have observed; personal experience is "profoundly particular" and inscrutible; scripture is often poetry

title: The Design of Everyday Things
author: Donald Norman
description: User error is the designer's fault
memories: Book about common things written before the PC really took off, or the cell phone, lots of desk phone examples; totally different world, I wonder what he would think of "hidden" today; make things visible; feedback; understand what you can do, how to do it; no instructions; easy for the first time; instruction manuals suck, if you do them at all do it before the creation of the product; predicted IoT

title: I Am A Strange Loop
author: Douglas Hofstadter
description: "I" is a strange loop
memories: Love Hofstadter, very playful style; the things most real to us are those most commonly activated; hunekers; small souls; mosquitoes; after rebirth of symbols, origin gone; all concepts interoperable in brain; we are so different that to find what we truly are, we have to look in very unexpected places; stuff about his wife dying in the middle makes it super personal; what matters is the pattern, not the particles; envelopes and marbles; I is an outcome not a starting point; which turns into who

title: Think Big, Start Small, Move Fast
author: Nicholas LaRusso
description: Mayo learned how to innovate, not write
memories: Really poorly written, but lots of knowledge; good story behind Mayo with the dad and bros, first integrated practice; left open for further thought and research; explicit, tacit, latent needs of consumers; first time I've super skimmed a book and it deserved it; finished in like 2-3 hours and still got the gist

title: The Drama of the Gifted Child
author: Alice Miller
description: Author loves her model, hates  her parents
memories: Basic gist is that everybody was abused, and everything bad is a result of early childhood abuse; there's a really great genuine point that if you don't accept your true emotions you'll take them out on someone else, but it's lost in the repetition (boring repetition) and the grandiose claims; it's not words that free people, but experience; I was recommended this book by the Tim Ferriss podcast; makes a good point about accomplishments and how some really driven people are just walking on stilts, and hate that they're not normal but also feel the need to keep getting better and better

title: Descarte's Error
author: Antonio Damasio
description: Emotions guide reason
memories: Really dry and scientific, seems like he was writing a science paper rather than a mass-market book; really interesting point that seems incontrovertibly true - that emotions really determine how reason works; emotions help us narrow down the choices available to us immediately; real rationality (weighing pros and cons) is ridiculous and not how the world actually works; color blind people can't imagine color; the mind is emobidied and embrained; paralyzed people can move their faces automatically when happy; willpower = choosing long term > short term; adaptive irrationality;

title: Modern Romance
author: Aziz Ansari
description: More options, different problems in love
memories: Regular world vs. phone world; companionate to soul mate marriage; so many new possibilities, people used to marry people in their building or on their block; between 2005 and 2012, 1/3 of all marriages met online; 3D -> 2D online; people don't know what they prefer really; marriage used to be a way into adult freedom for women; passionate love fades to companionate; Grave Digger date; Eduardo eating all the donuts; robots in Japan... and eggs; online introduction, not dating, service

title: Belief in God in an Age of Science
author: John Polkinghorne
description: Religious discovery can mirror science
memories: I think this book was too smart for me… just hard to follow; very thought provoking though, and some harvestable examples; emotional moments at finding truth, like literally seeing into another world; "natural theology" is that the religioius model brings insight to our understanding of the world; hold beliefs with convinction but the possibility of correction; impersonal and personal are both ways of understanding; we believe in plenty of things we can't see - like gluons and God; science and religion as assimilation vs. consonance - is one under the other or can they agree and concord; consciousness as "the fall"; Benard instability shows convection between two plates makes hexagons... somehow; all scientific facts are interpreted, theory vs. experiment/observation; background radiation is TV static

title: The Innovators
author: Walter Isaacson
description: Collaboration makes computers
memories: Technology advance was sweet and incremental; oftentimes similar inventions at same time, just who could market them the best and face consumers; Tesla warning; poetical science; overreaction first but undervalue true end state; ripe seeds on fertile ground; "intuition is nothing but the outcome of earlier intellectual experience" - Einstein; innovaton requires articulation; hard to know when to push ahead against haters and when to admit they have something right; hacker = quickly done, inelegant; deals to retain equity are huge, same with intellectual property and licensing; importance of basic, fundamental research; "the best way to predict the future is to invent it" - alan kay; "insert quarter, avoid klingons"; Google founders got BK after first round of funding

title: Way of the Peaceful Warrior
author: Dan Millman
description: Dan probably found zen in a gas station
memories: Guy meets Socrates in a gas station, he has super strength and can jump onto buildings but has a heart problem; Dan falls in love with this girl Joy but then they lose each other then re-find once they both lose their memories of each other; accepting right now is the key; innate perfection of your life unfolding; just do stuff, don't hesitate but know the full consequences; be unreasonably happy; a master dedicates his training to life; the place is here and the time is now

title: Black Holes and Baby Universes
author: Stephen Hawking
description: Stephen Hawking is real smart
memories: Real repetitive; black holes emit particles that travel faster than the speed of light; there might be baby "primordial" black holes as far away as Pluto; Hawking's time limits are all in imaginary time (at right angle to real time), universe had a start in real time with the Big Bang; sum over histories; he didn't want a Theory of Everything to be made (the movie); black holes are a lot like consciousness - increasing gravity like increasing perception until it loops;

title: It
author: Stephen King
description: Imagination makes and kills monsters
memories: A little bit too formulaic, but scary as fuck still; the town of Derry doesn't seem too dystopian, people just turn away the evil like people do everywhere; balloons and clowns forever corrupted; loved the bravery the kids showed; characters rocked; Eddie and his inhaler, and sacrifice at the end; Ben the builder and lover of Bev, was fat but got fit and made the sweet dam and fort and bulidng like the library walkway; Bill the hero; Beverly the lost soul and submissive but strong; Richer and his voices; Mike and the bird; Stan who died; can't think of another book with that strong of character development except Harry Potter; the phone calls; bubbling blood; imagination stuff

author: Jonathan Crary
description: Praxis-ing sleep is natural, also fuck capitalism
memories: Such a pretentious philosophy book; name drops all the cool kids from debate; actually a lot of interesting ideas, just marinated in bullshit; 24/7 shames the human necessity of shutting down and sleeping and getting away from computers; we are better off with a distinction between night and day; manufactured desire for drugs; smart devices make it so there is never unplugged time; computer and TV dispossess of time; purely online interactions only support separatin and isolation, must be based in something real and physical

title: we are all completely beside ourselves
author: Karen Joy Fowler
description: Chimps are people too
memories: Rosie uses big words, love it; fun to see how they loved the chimp as a sister, seems like that would really happen; Lowell's descent into craziness even with good intentions; Harlow is crazy but fun; they don't mention it in their family because they think it would hurt Rosie; Fern (chimp) and Rosie ended up mostly like sisters, being teachers of kids; umwelt = worldview; you end up really caring for the characters

title: Saving the Appearances
author: Owen Barfield
description: People lived metaphors in the past
memories: Figuration = full experience of world, like in a moment all the things that come from sense experience; alpha thinking = thinking about figurations; beta thinking = thinking about thinking; Aristotle was different from Plato - Plato was first and believed in perfection and mathematical orbs for stars, Socrates didn't because he started to separate them from participation; participate in phenomena is your addition in figuration, old cultures always did this and knew they were doing it; on the other side of phenomena are the represented, for past they were the same as "us" so it was identifying and constantly creating things from potentials and creating yourself in the same way; wasn't JUST metaphors, they were the truth; metaphors show how we have separated into metaphoric and literal when those were basically the same in the past; same with internal, external; future is God and final participation, not as keen on that part

title: The Curious Case of the Dog in the Night-time
author: Mark Haddon
description: Autistic boy is good at math
memories: So interesting to see how the guy portrayed the kid feeling emotions but having no idea how to express them; the family is so flawed but so real; kid loves small spaces and white noise and being "negligible" with the stars; this is the play from broadway I saw with Jacob and An; very convincing voice

title: Endzone
author: John U. Bacon
description: Dave Brandon almost broke Michigan
memories: So important not to just change for the sake of change or because you think you're smart - stay open; Hagerup's crazy journey through bad decisions smoking and drinking, missing three strikes but fighting back; amazing support for Harbaugh to come back to Michigan - everybody ever called; Brandon is rich, and a dick; CSG presidents rocked - Dishell and Proppe - mad political skills; Hoke was a good guy just too much oversight from Brandon; Brandon fired everybody; they didn't know Morris had a concussion at the time, just a bum ankle and nobody saw because all medical staff on the field

title: Got to Give the People What They Want
author: Jalen Rose
description: Jalen Rose is a baller
memories: Jalen got some support just not the same kind as other kids - emotional and some financial, but not like Chris Webber who went to private school; Uncle Len saved his life and kicked his ass in video games; Ed Martin wasn’t actually a bad guy, Chris Webber fucked everything up by lying to the grand jury; put the damn banners back up; "Nut Check" to keep egos in check; have a chip on your shoulder, worked for Jalen; what shoe high schools and college wear makes a huge difference, Nike highschools didn't want to lose their free stuff so they'd forward their kids to Nike colleges; black socks of Jalens were dress socks over regular white ones; appreciate your job but plan your promotion; gtgtpwtw; depends where you get drafted, goal is to be a veteran for lots of guys; Fab Five played ball on the Squad court; never let them see you sweat; old dudes with uniforms ("tight pants") in the MLB, gotta cut that out

title: Black Holes & Time Warps
author: Kip Thorne
description: Black holes are probably complicated
memories: Lots of different styles of research: people with huge teams, some that locked themselves away; tons of parallel discoveries, some people shied away and just went to weird fields to find stuff by themselves; iron curtain of Stalin really fucked with progress, made people isolate between russia and usa/uk, no collaboration or visiting each other; black holes were originally thought of as a mistake, then stepwise got it; Chandrasekhar was the first to find the limit of white dwarfs size, above = black hole?; next was the neutron stars limit, then people were like fine they exist; started with really idealized images of black holes (no electromagnetim, perfectly circular stars, no spin) then gradually made it better and better to accomodate all possibilities; still not tons of good evidence, especialyl of what happens within the horizon (singularity almost for sure but tidal gravity and such); special relativity = special because no gravity; down to the arc second in measurements of orbits and such led us to realise the Newtonian gravity laws were bad, .1 arc second in Mercury, 0.01 is a human hair... at 2km; relativity thing with the cannonball, window, and jumping off a cliff; nukes work by chain reactions of releasing neutrons making other neutrons release; black holes have no hair; we know star movement within .1km/second; synchrotron are multiple radio telescopes connected to make an awesome image; black holes seemed WAY to active with radio waves to be real - 10 million suyns of pure energy at 100% efficiency;black holes emit radiation because sponteous +1 and -1 pairs form and one gets sucked into the hole; coffee and donut have same topology because same connections

title: The Bazaar of Bad Dreams
author: Stephen King
description: King turns his real world stories horrific
memories: Car that eats people: big vs. little kids, kids figure it out easier than adults; woman dies from being fat, dog dies with her in a hot car; Alzheimer's dad shanks a guy; THE DUNE: cool story about lawyer and old guy, sees names of dead people on a dune, sick ending; Bad Little Ginger gets shot in the face; murderer (?) gets hung; ghost elephants; woman punches a kid in the face for a priest; afterlife where you get two choices - do it all exactly the same or disappear; ur-reality, books, news, future, and paradox police; drunk mother kills her kids by speeding in a car; man "uses his imagination" to avoid admitting his wife is dead; blockade billy is a dumb catcher who kills an ump; Mister Yummy = gay guy's death, in form of a hot guy; hippie scene moves underground because people died young; green god lives inside a dumb old man, surprises nurse; guy running late for presentation sees a murder in the "other world" of a bus, can't do anything about it; guy has power to kill you by writing your obituary; drunken firework show ends in fire; post-apocalypse, two dudes and a dog trying and failing to cope

title: A Deadly Wandering
author: Matt Richtel
description: Don't text and drive, your brain ain't that good
memories: Kid kills two rocket scientists, ends up torturing him forever; our brains can only process one thing at a time; texting and driving is really bad, everybody knows it, but everybody still does it; cell phones started because people wanted car phones "can your secretary dictate at 60 mph"; first innovation from silicon valley was the fruit cup; bottoms-up appeal is the pings and notifications, top-down is the connection with people, combine to make phones basically impossible to resist; self-disclosure is a primary desire like food and sex; social media works because it's often useless and unfulfilling, intermittent reinforcement like a slot machine; people heal by telling the truth, especially to themselves; void in technology can be helpful to process everything, don't just replace it with something else

title: How I Lost 170 Million Dollars
author: Noah Kagan
description: Guy got fired; probably due to writing style
memories: Pretty terribly written - really short paragraphs and smiley faces, repeated stuff, really just a hack job; interesting enough story though, facebook was a frat; Zuckerberg was totally unforgiving and in complete control, fired tons of people instantly; you can tell what a company values by the percentage of their personnel; six laws of Zuck: people not users, detail matters, use your own product, people don't know what they want, don't suck, site can never be down; stick to guns rather than being hurt by change haters; guy actually went through a hard time, I can relate to having things criticized and feeling the pain

title: Einstein's God
author: Krista Tippett
description: There are scientists that believe
memories: Krista is a really good interviewer - she read all their books and knew them almost better than they did; took forever to read this one, weak reading game; wave/particle shows contradictions can be true; men wonder about tons of stuff but not themselves; if block time is true, your life is still there in its entirety after you die;we have global entertainment, but not global medicine; goal is unmodulated experience of the world; Darwin was really careful to not exclude religion; morphed idea of creationism to imply more about the world at some point

title: Consider the Lobster
author: David Foster Wallace
description: Wallace knows really big words
memories: Most memorable is Wallace's huge vocabulary - there were times when dictionaries didn;'t have definitions for the words he used; porn award coverage was ridic, they happen at CES?!; so many footnotes; Updike criticism; Kafka is funny, really dark and simple humor but lots of exformative associations; Authiritative /dictionary of american Usage - great review of presciptivists (like Wallace) and descriptivists (that say all language use is legit); no personal language because there's no external standard, need one because otherwise there's no consistency; 9/11 with old people; shitty tennis pro's auto-bio that ignored all the crazy parts of her story (young start, fast fall); McCain's campaign, made we want to vote for McCain, showed how he was either a maniacal genius or really sincere and the fact that he was a war criminal made the first more likely - sounds like Bernie; they called GW Bush "the Shrub"; low turnout is good for incumbents; lobster fest and he questions morality the whole time; tourism is silly and destroys the thing you're trying to see; Dostoevsky review of how important his books were  in confronting social stuff, he was free to be much more moralistic because of his culture; we are afraid of being really ideological; John Ziuegler's radio show and how talk radio is biased to conservatives because tolerance isn't interesting but hating OJ simpson and yelling at black guys is; liberals aren't dissatisfied enough with the media to need something like talk radio

title: Accidental Empires
author: Robert X Cringely
description: Bill Gates was a dick; it worked
memories: Funny tone, I like the author; kinda meh and boring at times; no real discussion of internet, too old; predicted Gates would never marry; IBM was too big to be nimble enough to pivot to PCs very well; "waves" of technology metaphor is good, surfers have to enter and exit at right times; really did seem accidental that these companies worked at all; nerds vs. suits; first second third stage leaders; metaprogrammers; fast better than good if you can hit 85% good judgments via Simonyi; "Bill Gates has no style; Steve Jobs has nothing buy style"; FYIF- fuck you i'm fully vested; Gates has annual reading weeks

title: The Laws of Simplicity
author: John Maeda
description: Subtract the obvious, add the meaningful
memories: The world's always been falling apart, so relax; shrink, embody, hide; squint at the world, you'll see more by seeing less; the author is a total nerd and makes fun of Apple Shuffle because it claimed to be completely random; safe, confident, instinctive, repeat yourself for teaching; RTFM, Read The Fucking Manual; simplicity is all about context and how things relate to their environments; be a light bulb sometimes, and a laser beam at others; when there is less, we appreciate everything more, allow white space; relate, translate, surprise for a new thing

title: All The Light We Cannot See
author: Anthony Doerr
description: Nazis were people too
memories: Blind girl and nazi boy have parallel stories, really cool device with alternating chapters; really beautifully written, great descriptions and very scientific view of the world; loved the characters, was cool to see them all at the end however many years later; so sad... werner dies from a landmine; brutally realistic, not a happy ending really; never found out what happened to the dad; the diamond might have actually had a real curse; the models of the city her dad made for her; voice of grandfather was the one that werner and his sister heard (!!); clair de lune started the werner fight out of the collapsed building, saved her life; brain is "one wet kilogram within which spin universes"

title: My Reading Life
author: Pat Conroy
description: Pat Conroy has read 300 pages a day for 300 years
memories: Details are literally everything; connected with his mom through books, allowed him to talk about his abusive air force father; southern guy with deep roots; wants to be friends with everybody he ever meets; has a networking dinner thing that took off at a bookstore, got rowdy; thinks War and Peace is the best book about war or peace - controversial

title: Infinite Jest
author: David Foster Wallace
description: Wallace understands (un)happiness, also words
memories: So many big words, infintely many I had to look up; Enfield Tennis Academy, Hal Orin Avril Himself Mario, Pemulis CT OrthoStice John Wayne, Gately Madame Psychosis Lenz; Dog killing scene as a gradual escalation from Lenz' madness; Hal accidentally dragged their dog behind their car, Mom ridiculously believed it was an "accident" from another car running it over; Assassins de Fateuils Rollent (wheelchair assassins) as Quebecois pissed about continental reconfiguration; the Wraith that was Himself, aka JO Incandenza, came back to haunt the Ortho vs. Hal match and maybe give Hal drugs, etc; total allinear book, end is in the beginning; probably a shoutout to the theme of "annular" which was the nuclear waste recycling loop; giant hamsters in Maine from the recycling thing being too good and making a perfect environment; the super long scene with Steeply and Marathe on the mountain - long german word for distended shadows; pressures of tennis as told by the story of the kid who played with a gun to his head and said he would kill himself if he lost; all of JO's films are hilarious, Medusa (Stone) and the Obalique (ruby) in a mirror fight; Infinite Jest killed people it was such good entertainment; whole plot is AFR wants it to throw a knot in reconfiguration; ennet house is crazy fucked up, gately ends up being shot by canadians whose dog lenz killed; drugz; Orin and his "subjects" who were all married women, he fell in love with Madame Psychosis (who did a weird radio program back in the day) and who was incredibly hot until her mom threw acid on her face (accidentally, aimed for Dad); lots of incestual child rape; beatings where no bruises would show of gately's mom by the MP; Alcoholics Anonymous meetings and the "crocodiles" with "geologic" sober time; does Aubrey deLint have a z coordinate?; year names (and the statue of liberty) are notw sponsored, e.g., "year of the tuck's medicated pad"; "Found Drama" means that someone somewhere is the main character in a drama but doesn't know it;

title: When Breath Becomes Air
author: Paul Kalanithi
description: Doctor faces death himself
memories: End really hit me like a ton of bricks, the beginning wasn't too overtly cool but the last two pages made it worth it; made his death difficult intentionally - "isn't that how it should be"; people used to kill people to donate them as cadavers: "burking"; I can't go on, I'll go on; ambitions are abandoned or achieved, either way they are in the past

title: Surely You're Kidding, Mr. Feynman
author: Richard Feynman
description: Physicist loves strip clubs, brain teasers
memories: Seems like he had a very interesting life, with lots of international travel to Brazil and elsewhere (e.g., Japan); he really cared about getting better at things, e.g., drumming, drawing; went to the strip club 5-6 nights a week and was still married somehow; just "decided" some things and never questioned them again, like his job at CalTech; didn't drink after one urge he got one night; obsessed with practical knowledge, really understanding, would always work through general theories with a specific physical example in mind; experimented with everything, didn't trust experts, like testing if his scent was as good as his dogs; worked on the atomic bomb in the Manhattan Project and cracked locks all day; didn't seem too affected by his wife's death, was around the bomb project instead of her bedside as she was dying; don't average people's reports of how long the emperor's nose is; give all the information for and against your theory

title: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child
author: J.K. & Others Rowling
description: Bellatrix is Voldemort's baby mama; baby is mean
memories: Albus Severus Potter is understandably overshadowed by his name; the Dumbledore portrait seems more than half-Dumbledore; Hermione is Minister of Magic; Time travel to save the world, but they end up totally messing it up; Cedric killed Neville in the alternate universe where he got humiliated in the second task of Triwizard - didn't think he had that side to him; Snape sacrifices himself AGAIN; Ron and Hermione have their souls sucked out together to save the real future; the Augurey = bird that cries when rain (or death) is coming, this girl that posed as Amos Diggory's niece - called herself Delphini or Delphi; some hints of JK Rowling but it's a little hyper-emotional, and Harry seems super troubled; Snape: "Sometimes costs are made to be borne"

title: Look Me in the Eye
author: John Elder Robison
description: Funny, or just Aspergian, stories
memories: Sincerely can't tell if the author is trying to be funny - I think he's a little self-aware but also just trying to convey how he thinks, which is so different from normal that it's funny; he had a long discussion about whether he picked the best sister of the three; he loved machines - trains, cars, electrical circuits, guitars; he was always worried that he wasn't good enough and that his machines would fail, like his lights for KISS's tour; the fire story where his friend's parents are totally unconcerned with a flame the height of their house in their front yard; lived in the woods with some homeless guy for a while; had names for everybody, like Varmint for his brother, Slave and Stupid for his mom and dad, Little Bear for his first wife and Cubby for his son

title: Rita Hayworth and the Shawshank Redemption
author: Stephen King
description: Reading a whole book in Morgan Freeman's voice rocks
memories: Who knew that Stephen King wrote Shawshank? They simplified the plot a lot, only one warden who was a mash-up of the others, and made up Andy's final FU to that warden/his suicide, but otherwise it was pretty exact to the book; "Suds" was in the novel, not invented as product placement for the movie; get busy living, or get busy dying; Red was another invented character, a mixture of the bird guy and the librarian and the old person who didn't want to leave (no suicide in book); guard's wife is so obedient that she could eat her way through a boxcar of Ex-Lax and wouldn;t dare fart unless I gave her the nod

title: Lolita
author: Vladimir Nabokov
description: Pedophiles can be protagonists too
memories: Amazingly well-written, unbelievable that Nabokov wrote this in his THIRD language; makes a pedophile into a protagonist, you can't help but accept his love of "nymphets" to be something he can't control (when he cradled Lolita post-coitally, then was overcome by his desire again); takes a while to realize but definitely messed Lolita up; Lolita goes by Dolly or Dolores; I was really surprised that she ended up getting wifed up by some deaf old guy... just wanted anything that wasn't abusive and terrible; very unclear if the guy Humbert Humbert kills actually raped Lolita or not; description of tennis was amazing; he says "I won't exist if you dont imagine me," shatters the fourth wall constantly; "there is no harm in smiling"; the wife finds his letters and then gets hit by a car, that description was amazing "the ___ and the ____"

title: The Hunt for Red October
author: Tom Clancy
description: Clancy'll you miss the good ol' days of hating Russians
memories: From pg. 100-500 was pretty useless; lots of very technical detail which were #grounding but overall pretty boring; the final battle after the main chase was over was a bit contrived but by far the coolest and most suspenseful part of the book… I mean seriously they rammed another sub; last battle was genuinely surprising but the rest of the book wasn't; they made SUCH A BAD DECISION by turning on the Red October crawl drive thing during that battle; could have done more to set up the spy working inside the USA; pretty cool to tell individual stories of people, see how they handled small pieces but coordinated; seems like the officers were AWESOME at their jobs, almost nobody EVER got a judgment call wrong; the letter was anti-climactic, "I'm going to New York" and he actually planned on going there lols; I liked how the characters had menial thoughts while in high pressure situations; submarines need seatbelts; Ryan is a very relatable hero, he's smart in the way that a lot of people would like to be, and has normal emotional reactions ("sorry" to the guy he just shot, then puking); Russian officers died of brain hemmorages during questioning, "nine-millimeter hemmorages"

title: Charlotte's Web
author: E.B. White
description: Charlotte is the friend you wish you had
memories: Such a pure story - amazing characterization throughout, a compelling arch, I didn't realize that Charlotte was going to die; great lessons; amazing imagery of the farm and the various parts of it : normal day, seasons, river, etc.; cool wondering if Fern could actually understand the animals or if she was just imagining... but clear via charlotte's web that she was actually right; children can understand better; showed cool lessons about what makes people go: dad wanted recognition, fern changed and loved the random ferris wheel boy, avery the brother just loved being raucous, etc.; rat templeton was a good example of a bad friend who still does some goodp;

title: Animal Farm
author: George Orwell
description: Pigs can be Communists too
memories: A good story, and legendary, but not particularly enjoyable… probably more relevant for a generation that lived through Communist worries; Snowball's ousting was interesting, and his demonization was pretty standard; fact that all the animals were dumb is a shame, one smart one would have made the book more interesting; the Napoleon hangover was hilarious (drinking is punishable by death... jk just in excess after the hangover passes); the sheep were an important part, they're like the Trump twitter bots that just echo his bullshit; Major died right away, even though he was the intellectual father of the movement; Squeaker had a huge role as the main propagandist; "all animals are equal, some are just more equal than others"; lots of disinformation needed to placate and get slave labor out of the masses; and old Major knew that they shouldn't come to resemble humans.

title: A Room of One's Own
author: Virginia Woolf
description: Did Virginia take her own medicine for genius? Maybe.
memories: Argues in a series of lectures mostly fictional-focused that women should have $500 euro a year and a room of their own to write well; imagines shakespeare's sister who never had a chance because of the times in which she lived; tons of women's stories not being told; the fictional pieces were really powerful, the first 30 pages of the book are masterful; woman starts at river, gets a good thought but interrupted because she's not allowed to walk on the grass; then she has a great meal with the dudes with great conversation but the women's college food for dinner is such slop that she can't talk or think well after/during; argues that you need total intellectual freedom (even from your gender, you should have a androgynous mind) to write amazingly; some people are too manly, some too womanly, need a balance; she says women have and like "suggestive power" where men are very free because wealthy and powerful, but obsessed with "I"; also says that women have no good role models for great prose to work off of; Jane Austen wrote in a common room and had to hide hrer work;  key to writing is integrity, belief that this is the truth; women are muses and objects of creativity for poems but absent from history; masterpieces arise from many voices behind a single voice; must have fully integrated your words with the truth of reality, not of people or characters or especially yourself

title: The Great Gatsby
author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
description: What did Gastby love: the light, or the girl?
memories: Amazingly written, some of the best and most striking writing, descriptive and lyrical; two scenes that you couldn't stop reading: the confrontation between Gatsby and Tom Buchanon, the final murder scene and aftermath; everybody from the Midwest and Gatsby went to Olaf; the light across the sound was so close and a symbol of his undying but impossible love, but then as soon as Daisy was there it just became a light, kinda like the dust world where Tom's mistress lived and died; eyes of God, actually didn't find much significance in that; Gatsby spent his whole life in pursuit of Daisy, what happens if he actually gets the girl? or if he doesn't get killed? still feel like he can't live in a world without her, and she chose Tom; why did Daisy choose Tom? fever dream of their old love, but once it was close it wasn't the same anymore?; I didn't really care about the other girl character who liked Callaway (narrator), they seemed to not actually like each other, he despised her before she said she liked how steady and good he was; same situation with Callaway and Gatsby, he never liked him but was still the only one (other than owl eyes) at the funeral; nobody showed up at gatsby's funeral even though everyone was at his parties (hollow fame/glory/wealth); theme of the whole book was distance and the images we build up inside, as a concentrated version of reality and chasing the rosy retrospection of the past

title: Eichmann in Jerusalem: A Report on the Banality of Evil
author: Hannah Arendt
description: Eichmann was dumb, and maybe also evil
memories: Chilling that someone so stupid and literally only focused on his career could be a war criminal that killed maybe literally millions; seriously the guy contradicted himself all the time and was generally clueless; also maybe he was smarter but intentionally was the scapegoat; 15,000 jews killed -> 10 years hard labor, lots of ridiculously light punishments; Eichmann got killed like three hours after his appeal was denied; the whole thing was mostly just the report of the trial and lots of menial details of different deportations; best question saved for the epilogue, of whether the jewish court ought to have tried the case or if they needed an international court; lots of jewish artifacts were only saved because nazis made museums DURING the war and holocaust; they called gas chambers "medical matters"; interesting rhetorical tricks like that, and instead of "look at the horrible things I did to those people," "look at the horrible things I was forced to watch as part of my duty"; during the meeting where they proposed the final solution, people basically agreed immediately; labeling and forming the jewish councils were always the first steps; lots of nazis were tempted to do good but "resisted temptation"; the jewish councils made lots more jews die because nazis were undermanned and uncoordinated; Denmark was the only country to resist and it took EVERYONE, citizens and leadership; at Auschwitz, criminals weren't killed, never selected for gas chambers; ironically, Eichmann was only captured illegally-ish because he was stateless... and that's also how he justified killing the jews, because they had no state; the most compelling thing was Arendt's fake ruling from the judges at the end; if you excuse because anybody would have, not far to "why not me, stats say X should die this year"

title: Ender's Game
author: Orson Scott Card
description: Big bro takes over Earth; little bro kills aliens
memories: The world the author made was interesting but hardly believable - pretty unclear why kids are suddenly geniuses that outsmart literally the entire world and an entire alien species; chose Ender because he was really smart (infallibly so?) and vicious, he killed two kids in "self-defense"; I loved the relationship Ender had with his sister Valentine, that and struggling with the deaths of the "buggers" were the most interesting parts of the book; the game sounds really fun, basically laser tag in space; I would love playing that game, and could crush some nerds; basically just sorta unbelievable to the point where I can't connect with the book... unfortunate; also some of the prose was hyped and blown out of proportion/didn't sound like things people actually say, and people basically all spoke in the same voice; the world was cool enough to make itself a good book, but the writing wasn't stellar; would have liked to see more of how Demosthenes and Locke took over the world via internet

title: The Story of My Experiments with Truth
author: Gandhi
description: Gandhi thought a lot about food, apparently
memories: Gandhi was the real deal - incredibly inspiring, great judgment, extremely wise and personally dedicated to self-improvement; but also really boring as a writer; he stole stuff to smoke as a kid, then, bored with his buddy, decided to commit suicide; married super early and had lots of lust for her; was nursing his dad (Gandhi loved nursing) but then left when his brother offered to take over, he went to wake up his wife to sleep with her and then his dad died; reformer can;'t be intimate with whom he tries to reform; infinte striving after God probably needs a perfect teacher, don't put too much faith in imperfect people as Gurus; rifts are rifts, no matter how you repair them; service done for show sucks, but if you have joy in your heart can be great; fasting only works with mind and body, not just body but you fantasize about food (or whatever) all the time; very journal-y and self-reflective and he questions just stopping writing at times; doesn't talk about lots of stuff I want to hear like prison and non-violence in his biggest moments; Miss Schlesin was his best contemporary; didn't teach his kids a literary education, but did focus on moral stuff obvs; threw his friend's binoculars into the sea because he didn't like them and they were a point of contention between them; society was divided about accepting him, so he just withdrew and then even the detractors liked him; stuck to diet even on his deathbed when docs asked him to drink/eat milk; but then he did eventually drink goat's milk because he wanted to live and serve more

title: The Victory Lab
author: Sasha Issenberg
description: Surprising few, politics was data-dumb pre-Obama
memories: All based on the individuals behind the evolution of data science in campaigns; control randomized trial is key; taught me a lot about how things happened and how new the science still is; basically no discussion of Facebook or social media; would have loved to hear how Trump did stuff; started with Getting Out The Vote in 1927, first experiment to up turnout; $100K to survey 1900 people in 1952; voters not "consumers" but "investors" that use heuristics, interpret signs; +15 = "base" for NCEC; in-person is BY FAR the best way to reach people - does this account for cost, though?; 1984, 25% voters ballot-split across parties, in 2000, 7%; Labor '96 increased share of election from 13% to 23%; 1990s was GOP innovation by snatching up radio and tv talk shows; P&G spends more ever year advertising soap than Americans spend in total on elections; descriptive norms work better than prescriptive ones (like "few people litter," Cialdini); 10% of people lie that they voted to the CPS; 8% that got "here are all your neighbors that have voted" mailer turned out anew... one mailer!; Obama in Iowa needed 180K turnout... got 239,000 because of Millennials (voters under 25 TRIPLED), 1/2 caucused for first time; predictive models don't work without precedent... which stinks if you're trying to innovate; one woman in Iowa was contacted 103 times and was still undecided, campaign director thought about asking Obama to call her directly; REGISTRATION, PERSUASION, TURNOUT; Marshall Ganz is all about personal narratives?!; Bradley effect probably had something to do with Trump - people don't want to admit nastiness of racism (but asking if their neighbors are racist works); people have very well-developed sniff tests for ads, so glossy shit doesn't work because people smell the Madison Avenue on it; Obama org. decided in 2008 campaign that polls were broken and microtargeting was the clear future; "vector autoregressions" are used by equities traders to show one variable over time; opportunity for personal over internet that is largely unexplored

title: The Diary of a Young Girl
author: Anne Frank
description: She had to make it one more month! One!!
memories: Very gripping in how normal it was - I could see a ton of myself in Anne, and her struggles of aging and dealing with parents; felt very obviously magnified with no separation from parents ever; watching her develop to love Peter - and her recognition that Peter in the room was similarly idyllic (i.e., an image and not the real person) to the Peter she loved before; so sad that her life was squandered, she was ridiculously empathetic and would have been a great writer (was!); she was very obsessed with boys and understanding what everyone else thought; her letter to her Dad was incredibly cruel, but reminds me of conversations I've had with my Mom... very similar dynamic honestly; the fact that Anne still felt fortunate in that situation is amazing and she is ridiculously well-centered... contrast to Mrs. Van Daan who was NOT; Secret Annexe is a:"little piece of blue heaven" in the eye of a storm; very interesting to watch her realize that she's becoming more self-conscious; she grew to love nature, partially because she was separated from it, and recognized its perfection, said that where there is nature, cannot be unhappy; "there is always some beauty left" in contrast to her mother who wanted only to compare to worse off and say we are better; I wonder if the strawberries hubbub gave them away

title: The Communist Manifesto
author: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels
description: Eff da (bourgeois) police
memories: Super short manifesto, could be read by real revolutionaries and absorbed; pretty compelling in its historical grounded, but I just don't know enough about those movements (or like, serfs) to really care or know if it's right; they do get very broad, which feels counterproductive... step one is ALL OUT REVOLUTION AND NO MORE PRIVATE PROPERTY which feels excessive; more than 1/2 the book dedicated to counter-arguments and pooping on the other existing working-class revolutions; had an interesting parallel to the fake news phenomenon that's all the rage nowadays, in that they say the world is changing faster than people can catch up to it - just as memes are created too quickly to process and give time to any one of them; core thesis is that relations between people arise from modes of production and that only; argued machines are bad because they divorce a person from their own individual physical strength - which is super interesting considering some are now fighting to keep their machine jobs as the computers take over; bourgeois society, past dominates present, in communism, present dominates past; they end up saying "we support ALL movements against the way things are, leggo"

title: Discipline and Punish
author: Michel Foucault
description: Prisons conquered torture, then society
memories: Really amazing writing, super thorough, incredibly persuasive; I want to be MORE taken by its message than I already am but feel like that's somehow wrong/malleable/evidence that I knew nothing about prisons before this; one of the best philosophy books I've read b/c it didn't even feel opinionated or far-stretched, just a laying out of the facts and the conclusions (big ones!) naturally followed; starts with tortue and explaining how it's the body of the king you attack when you breka the law so the body of the king responds with crazy display of force, a "spectacle"; was where people would have to see and be deterred by seeing the brutality of the punishment; but eventually that got out of control because people would stand up to executioners or even fight them; "pain, ritual, spectacle" of punishment helps it deter; was often to extract confessions, but obv people got fake ones or kept talking to try and delay the punishment; chain gangs also around this time but those were super counterproductive b/c prisoners became heroes or at least anti-heroes; society had three options: torture, a punishment system totally proportional and individualized, or prison; society chose prison because it deferred violence, could be easily proportional because more time for worse crime; prison was wrapped in reform the whole time, always being changed but the answer was always "get back to roots" and imprison better/more fundamentally; prison not just punishment but discipline: controls time w/ timetables, has work, education, health, surveillance within and without prison to form whole idea of subject, assessment, ranking, create individual idea of criminal, and can change the punishment in prison; prison effectively controls sentencing because response to punishment can give more or less of it; separation is huge for effective running - panopticon = one person can see all, but they can't see back; shift from punishment of all people seeing the one (king) with its punishment of a person to where one person can see all; this spilled over into society with hospitals, schools, military, factories, rehab centers, poor houses, foster homes, etc - all part of the disciplinary society that ranks and individualizes and objectifies to make reform of the SOUL, not of the body, "possible," but really it's just a form of disciplinary power of the state (?) presumably; the extension into society is also a result and continuing factor in the other misc institutions; prison creates the delinquent person, and is a self fulfilling prophesy because people recidivise and go back into prison; other class of people created that are somehow separate from us good people; we are not a society of spectacle, but surveillance; seven maxims of prison: correction, classification, modulation of penalties, work, education, technical supervision, auxilary institutions

title: Between The World And Me
author: Ta-Nehisi Coates
description: People who think themselves white break black bodies
memories: Most interesting thing is that Ta-Nehisi hates that so many white people are reading this book, wasn't written for me… but it was super moving; he learned to avoid gunfire before he learned his shapes and colors, growing up in West Baltimore; went to Howard, where he met a few amazing women, experienced the diversity of black life (all colors, backgrounds), met his friend Prince who would later be tracked down and shot by a black police officer; said it was like a natural disaster, set in motion and unstoppable and the way things are; incredibly pessimistic, but real and incredibly honest; went and talked to Prince's mom and she was no nonsense, steeled like people from the civil rights riots; whole thing is a letter to his son who was raised in a way better environment but still cried when he heard that the killer of a black boy got off free; his dad was a black panther; a woman pushed his 5 year old son, he flipped out, white guy said "I could have you arrested" for yelling and that was part of "ownership" of his black body; "people who consider themselves white" need blacks as a race to be antagonistic, like irish before but that dissolved which is evidence to Ta-Nehisi that race isn't real... and I think I agree; 60% of all black men who drop out of high school go to jail; guns of the black panthers seemed "honest" because they addressed the "despotic violence" of police intervention and shootings in black areas; redlining = disapproving loans for housing in ghettos; a lot of it was him searching for a "black people are kings" history but then he realized that "Tolstoy was the Tolstoy of the Zulus," don't need to have an equivalent of the Dream for black people; a mountain is not a mountain if there is nothing below

title: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
author: Thomas Kuhn
description: Normal science doesn't apply in paradigm shift
memories: Normal science is problem solving - when anomalies are discovered that don’t fit with existing paradigms, time for a wholesale shift; different paradigms speak different languages and require translation to talk to each other; took people 50 years to agree with Newton, this stuff is slow moving and science doesn't just let go of beliefs; scientific progress doesn't move towards anything like an objective understanding of the world, because there's no value- or paradigm-neutral way to articulate that movement - theories are chosen over others for aesthetic reasons or because they help us solve more problems; communities are formed, science is awesome at mobilizing people at problems that they mutually agree are important in a given paradigm, preserving knowledge across generations, isolating themselves from outside (science's audience = scientists), textbooks to agree on basic stuff; indoctrinated not by the theories, but the questions at the end of the chapter; without a paradigm fact-gathering is random; many fields quickly got too complicated for laypeople and had to be translated into normal words to make sense; physical discoveries and theories come hand-in-hand, they are "mere facts" if discovered before we can actually interpret them; focus on in-paradigm problem-solving is why science seems to progress so fast; novelty arises when you know with precision what to expect but you're proven wrong; if complexity is growing faster than accuracy, you're in trouble; proliferation of versions of a theory = crisis; scientists don't reject theories when they have evidence against them, only when they hop to a new theory that's better; sometimes the shape of the new paradigm is foreshadowed by the structure of the anomaly; young people not seeped in traditon normally bring these things about; new paradigms define both what problems matter and how it's possible to solve them; data themselves change with a paradigm, you can literally get different measurements; textbooks "write history backwards" and only highlight the old experiments that confirm current theories; Planck: scientific truth doesn't win by convincing old people, just lasting until they die off; evolution of science is like the evolution of species, not TOWARDS a pre-defined goal but evolution FROM what we do know

title: The Character of Physical Law
author: Richard Feynman
description: Physicist says all you can say about simple physics
memories: Nothing particularly new: people have to replace theories instead of just rejecting then, nature is simple, there are laws that apply here and there ("long threads"), laws are always sorta there ("if you thought before that science was certain, that's an error on your part"); moon orbits earth once a month; orbits cover the same area in the same amount of time, slices are the same volume, so they swing faster when closer to whatever they're orbiting; science is only useful in telling us about things we haven't seen yet; astronomical history is a little different from physical law, mostly just ignore the bang; relativity means there is no "now" and simultaneity doesn't exist because things happen at different times depending on your movement; physical laws are symmetric in lots of ways (forward/backward in time, rotation) but NOT in reflection because molecules aren't symmetric, same as the left-handed protein thing; nature is 99.99% indistinguishable left and right; "i can safely say that nobody understands quantum mechanics"; search where you can be proven wrong to make progress; when you get it right, more comes out than goes in

title: The Art of War
author: Sun Tzu
description: Sun Tzu says: this is a metaphor for everything
memories: So short, here are all the lessons I liked: no country benefits from prolonged war, so let the object be victory; best leader wins without attacking, making them not want to engage; have to have your team animated by the same spirit; you can defend yourself and be super prepared, but being able to beat your opponent comes from their mistakes; win with ease - make no mistakes - the victorious strategist fights only when the war is already won; good fighter will be "terrible in his onset, and prompt in his decision"; don't stick to same successful tactics, be like water and flow over terrain; gotta know the terrain, use local guides; use "gongs and drums, banners and flags" to signal your troops; always times to just not fight a battle, not obey an order, etc; five faults of a general: recklessness, cowardice, temper, delicate honor, caring too much about troops; " if you know the enemy and know yourself, your victory will not stand in doubt; if you know Heaven and know Earth, you may make your victory complete; when outlook is good, tell your troops, when not say nothing; only way to be better than others is to have foreknowledge, all knowledge comes from other people

title: The Double Helix: A Personal Account of the Discovery of THE STRUCTURE OF DNA
author: James Watson
description: 25 year old chases girls, Nobel prizes
memories: Very approachable and frank book - James Watson seemed like a normal dude, obviously smart b/c got his PhD at 22, but lazy to learn certain stuff and in the right place at the right time; very preoccupied with women, took lots of vacation while developing the solution to DNA; originally had the building blocks all known, but no way to put them together (debated between different #s of strands); originally thought it was three but couldn't make the physical model work, then gave up for a year or so, emboldened by a famous guy's (Louis Pauling's) very woebegotten attempt to solve a three-helical solution, and once they got the B-form xray from Rosalind Franklin, Watson made the big theoretical leaps and was corrected by a colleague on which form of G and T to include (the textbooks were wrong!), and the rest was Watson building models and doing theory and Crick calculating; Crick was way older; everyone hated Rosalind Franklin until they realized that she was super smart, and meant well but was constantly being undermined because she was a woman; Francis was married to a woman who understood nothing about science and thought gravity only went three miles into the sky; they felt that this solution was right before they could prove it - it was so beautiful

title: The Origin of Species
author: Charles Darwin
description: It's the ciiiiiiirrrcle (tree) of life
memories: This book is everything good and bad about science - Darwin spent 20 years researching AFTER he had the idea of natural selection (wow), and then published a really example-heavy and incredibly well researched snoozefest of a book; not fun to read, but enjoyable at times depending on the section; ended up skimming a lot of the examples because who cares; somehow he figured this out without knowing about DNA or how inheritance works; the argument is largely based on something people know - that breeders can artificially select for certain traits; no hard and fast rule for species vs. variety; more diverse the species -> wider reaching -> more variation -> better chances of survival; anticipates every major challenge (fossil record, features that can't be formed by small gradations) and crushes them; fossil record chapter was by far the best; can't select for the good of another species; book makes tons of predictions and factual statements that could be proven wrong; possible for some modifications to benefit the group (e.g., sterile worker ants); forms aren't DIRECT intermediates, like blends, of two existing animals, they are common descent - triangles, not lines; classification best done with NON functional characteristics; showed that the existing "Natural System" could really just rely on descent with modification; extinction defines, but doesn't create, species

title: The Sun Also Rises
author: Ernest Hemingway
description: Isn't it pretty to think things can work out?
memories: So simple - guy seems to be rich, has a lover who is flirty, and follows her to Pamplona to fiesta and run with the bulls, but it never quite works; very complete characters - Jake Barnes is just kinda a wimp and non-factor, but a nice guy - Lady Brett Ashley is just a flirt who needs approval and love from everyone, chasing something she doesn't even understand (which is why she goes after the star of the show even though he's 19 and she's 34; she obviously gets him because she's head-turningly attractive and confident) - Bill, the joker... so funny - Mike, the asshole boyfriend of Brett - and Robert Cohn, the cuckolded Jewish boxer who can't get over Brett; his description of the fiesta sounded so fun, wine bladders and forced drinks from locals and packed streets, brought back memories of 4am parties in Vegas you want never to end; kinda a sad but real story, where you can imagine all the characters really existing (in another time, because they never worked...), hating the americans and drinking away in Spain before and after everyone gets there for the fiesta; book so famous but so sparse in writing... how?; shows the post-war life and how it still was a letdown; Brett says at the end, we could have had such a great time together, Jake replies "Isn't it pretty to think so?"; Bill says "wonderful" all the time, and had Mike's shoes cleaned 11 times in a row, and says his secret is to never be "daunted" in public

title: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
author: Frederick Douglass
description: Christian slavers were the worst
memories: Amazing that this wasn't the worst brand of slavery, and that they literally held "We'll send you to Georgia" over their heads; feels like they all knew they couldn't treat slaves like people and still enslave them (e.g., working for wages in Baltimore, learning to read, etc.); he criticized the Underground Railroad for being too outwardly vocal, said it hurt chances of further escape; one guy had a rep for being a slave breaker, but Freddie fought him and choked him out, which shut him up; a nice woman intuitively wanted to help Frederick and treat him like a human, but slavery turned her evil over time; slave songs came out of UNhappiness; slavers were never charged for murder of their own slaves, because black people couldn't be witnesses or press charges; black men were once the "foreigners" stealing white workers jobs

title: The Souls of Black Folk
author: W.E.B. DuBois
description: Emancipation didn't solve the issue of race
memories: WEB was a very well-educated guy, amazing the difference between him and Frederick Douglass; too flowery sometimes, and the middle was boring, but had tons of insights and the "On the Coming of John" fictional chapter was 100x more engaging than anything else, his style is much better for ficton; prisons were made to re-enslave blacks; white people didn't believe blacks would work without slavery; blacks were horribly under-educated, and whites wouldn't teach them anything after emancipation, so they had to make colleges (to train teachers) before grade or trade schools; prejudice and social condition of blacks is a reciprocal relationship, one causes the other; black churches were PRE-Christian, blacks gathered in churches even more they could read Bibles, heavily influenced by African religions (e.g., visible displays of being affected by the unseen); slave masters used Christianity to keep slaves docile; slave songs so sad, foundation of American music; story of "John" is an energetic black man, gets into Princeton, learns of his race's problems and sees prejudice so gets way more serious, goes back to town and everyone is sad that he's so sad now, a white boy also named John forces himself on his sister so he kills the white boy, then ends with him about to be lynched; WEB DuBois' epitaph for Alexander Crummell: "He never faltered, he seldom complained; he simply worked, inspiring the young, rebuking the old, helping the weak, guiding the strong."

title: Philosophy of Mind
author: GWF Hegel
description: I understood some of those words
memories: One of the worst, least intelligible books ever -- unbelievable that it's so famous; this is one of those "justify your degree by being obtuse" books, but to the Nth power, I can imagine people poring over every sentence but THAT SHOULDN'T  BE NECESSARY, if he is so smart he should be able to convey his ideas effectively; I read every word and can't tell you why this book matters; it was hot garbage, and a waste of time

title: Meditations on First Philosophy
author: Rene Descartes
description: Descartes can do no wrong -- except overthinking
memories: Very simple and well articulated -- I loved the beginning, but it lost me at the existence of God and then eeeeeverything else following; Descartes was a COCKY MOFO, said that "no way open to the human mind whereby better [arguments] could ever be found" HAHAHA; starts with doubting everything, because the only thing that's deception proof is the fact that you exist (and may be deceived); mind is better known than the body, mind is indivisible and body is divisible, melted wax example of all senses changing but the thing staying the same; God exists because there can't be anything in the mind more perfect than what actually exists, so the idea of a perfect God must be true, we couldn't conceive of it if it wasn't, idea without a cause came from nowhere, no infinite regress because must be something that started the thoughts in your mind, "more reality in an infinite substance than a finite one," to strive for greater things is only possible because God is the greatest thing; clear and distinct again; mathematical things like triangles exist outside of /before the mind, part of perfection is existence, therefore God because existence is part of his essence; God wouldn't decieve me, so my senses are cool and work again yay

title: The Selfish Gene
author: Richard Dawkins
description: Genes, not people, naturally select
memories: Love the writing style: intelligent, incredibly well-informed, but simple -- simplicity on the other side of complexity; genes are replicators, and succeed with longevity, fecundity (speed of replication) and copying-fidelity; genes make the most sense because Dawkins defines them as the thing that's around long enough for natural selection to act on it, individuals die and groups are always changing; greenflies have no fathers, so mothers can give birth to already pregnant babies; genes are the replicators, we are the vehicles (they program our computers); parent and child have the SAME genetic similarity as brother to sister -- no reason why they should care evolutionarily about children (for kin selection reasons alone); also matters how certain you are that someone has your genes... mothers are certain, fathers aren't (usually) so mothers can invest more; most important thing to balance isn't # of children but how much you invest in them; babies should be selfish in grabbing as much parental love and support as possible, up until the point that they start to damage their bros/sis in an outsized way (50% vs. 100% of their genes); can define male by small sex cells (gametes), female by larger sex cells (also means females by definition have more invested per kid); hymenopteran (ant, bee, wasp) moms have males alone (unfertilized eggs -> male) and female with male help -- so sisters are more closely related with each other than with mom (50%+ same, vs. 50%); workers are really running the show for ants, queen wants a 1:1 ratio of male to female but workers want 3:1 (to spread their own genes); memes are the new replicators, ideas in culture; extended phenotypes mean that genes can affect the world (beaver dams/lakes, parasitic animals, etc); life/dinner principle: rabbit cares more than fox, because it's running away for its existence, not a sated appetite; 80% of all species are insects, "To a first approximation, all animals are insects... and extinct"; bottleneck of evolution -- everything starts and ends its evolutionary life as one cell... even elephants

title: A Clockwork Orange
author: Anthony Burgess
description: A real horrorshow of a veshch, O my brothers
