memories: Whoa vocabulary -- this weird mix of Slovak and English that was hard to read but pretty understandable, he did a good job defining them; story is great, simple but powerful: kid and friends commit crimes, battle gangs, rape, then one of the others tries to revolt against him (Georgie), he beats them up to assert power, then they betray him as he breaks into an old woman's house and he gets caught after having killed her - he is sent to prison, which doesn't work, he kills a cellmate, then they try an experimental aversion therapy on him and it works for a bit, so they release him to the world - his family has replaced him, he gets beat up by old friends/enemy thugs who are now cops, he stumbles into the house of the woman he raped (turns out she died) and her husband (author of "Clockwork Orange") takes him in and is super nice, turns out he wants to use him for political purposes, guy writes him a letter to the paper then drives Alex to suicide but he lives, recovers and then eventually finds his old friend Pete married (!) and he's like oh I wanna get married too; says childhood is like machinery; clockwork orange is when the state tries to reduce a juicy human into a machine; kid loves the orchestra

title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
description: Nietzsche was a lonely, weird, self-loving genius
memories: Style sucked, it was like olde english -- hard to read and had a different metaphor on every line, which was super anoying and obscured the message; basically about Zarathustra who is a "wise man" who periodically secludes himself then comes into town and weirds people out; basically his point is that the world should move beyond conceptions of good and evil, that those are for weak, small men, and instead we should look to the future and the SUPERMAN who is the next stage of human greatness, also people should be dicks and selfish and not worry that most people say the world sucks; people should know how to be light and dance; people are pumped about even a little virtue, which they hold as justification of committing lots of evil; most people only want one thing: to not be hurt; each person has to carve their own way and their own morality; dog and traveler both snap at each other out of fear, but could just as well embrace for they're both lost

title: Meditations
author: Marcus Aurelius
description: YOLO: everyone is small and will die soon anyways
memories: Right on the borderline of depressing but so incredibly wise -- literally just a guy's journal from year 150, who didn't think he was a philosopher but was the emperor of Rome, and he struggles with super normal things but deals with them in a super measured way; basic insights were: break things down and they become meaningless, that's all of life; think about how small we are within the range of time and space; humans are social creatures, made for the good of the city/society; all you have control of is your own brain, so don't worry about anything you cannot change, and you can change more than you think because all impressions are made by the brain ("soul") too; all people are quickly forgotten so who gaf about fame or glory; big religious argument re: god made everything perfectly, and we are part of a totality, so we are given everything we need always -- wish I could think like that but nah; things growing old and dying is just part of life, sometimes it reveals beauty too (e.g., loaves of bread cracking); vanity seduces reason, think about the true nature of all things; live each day as if it were your last without frenzy, apathy, or pretext; if you soil a neverending spring, it's totally fine in a hot sec; thinks the smaller things serve the greater, unclear if that's a slavery justification but oops

title: To Kill a Mockingbird
author: Harper Lee
description: A story as calm, collected, and interesting as Atticus
memories: Amazing story -- so insightful, all the characters and events seemed incredibly real, and WONDERFULLY paced; starts with exploring Jem and Scout's childhood (love, fear, adventure, sibling stuff), shows aging in childhood and the importance of first-person perspective, great at holding information until you need it / get it from Scout's perspective, then story moves to the trial of a black man who is accused of raping one of the trashy girls, scene at the jail w/ Attison and Scout saving Tom Robinson (the black guy), brief funny interlude with Calpurnia (Finch family servant, black woman) just cursing out Scout and Jem for seeing the trial, then verdict and aftermath with threats to Atticus and the attack on Jem/Scout by Bob Ewell (dad of "raped" girl), the story of Boo Radley comes full circle as he saves them in the end; hiding things in the tree was communication with Boo probably, boo probably also saved them by killing Bob; loved Atticus' temperament and parenting style, "can't face them if I don't take this case," and how you can see Jem aging into that mature mindset too (with the overreactions/overconfidence of youth)

title: Brave New World
author: Aldous Huxley
description: Soma and cloning killed God
memories: Amazing idea but fairly contrived story -- lots of plot jumps that are believable-minus, e.g., the director guy quits on the spot when embarassed by his love child "savage," the main character somehow becomes super popular because he's the "handler," etc; the characters themselves were interesting and well-thought, covered lots of bases (pretty girl, pretty guy, ugly guy, all lonely in their own ways); best part by far was the last conversation with Mustapha Mond, the leader guy, where he straight up explains why they made the world as it was, talks about how God is unnecessary now that people are perfectly happy and contented, God may manifest itself to this society as non-existant; society does work just because it takes time, too much leisure is bad (this is real now!); the fundamental clash is between truth/beauty and happiness -- this is a society 100% indexed on happiness, no matter how untrue or ugly; philosophy as bad reasons to say what you think instinctually, but Mustapha questions instinct's legitimacy, so it becomes bad reasons to justify bad reasons

title: Schindler's List
author: Thomas Keneally
description: You're safe now; you're with me
memories: Schindler was such an interesting guy: deeply cared about protecting his workers, but also a drunk (although a good one) and a horrible womanizer that ignored his wife; his wife was right that his talent just happened to line up perfectly with what the world needed for the four years of the war, before and after Schindler was nothing special; the story of him pulling people off a train is nuts, esp. the guard saying "we don't care if it's these Jews or some others; people didn't know what was truly happening at the extermination camps -- or maybe just didn't want to believe what they heard; the fundamental dilemma is whether he really helped... other Jews may have died in the places of the Schindlerjudein, but he still saved those individual lives (which "saves the world"), and what else can one guy do?; the sheer brutality of that nazi leader guy was insane; he didn't take fewer rations to struggle with the Jews, interesting detail; book itself was interesting but slow to start

title: Walden
author: Henry David Thoreau
description: Arrogant man builds cabin in the woods; loves nature
memories: God this guy was a douche… so full of himself, "I have a dope memory" and talking down to everyone he ever met on the pond; no plot really, just follows him in a small house watching nature for a year; cool that it was frugal, that he spent that much time by himself, I guess; civil disobedience stuff was way more interesting; don't loiter in winter when it's already spring; the scene with the dumb guy was really interesting; cast your whole vote, with all your influence

title: All the King's Men
author: Robert Penn Warren
description: Talos buys and destroys his way to power… and death
memories: Amazing, amazing prose -- better than Lolita, less metaphorical but much more philosophically interesting, considering everything from every angle, and better metaphors (constantly used); follows Willie Talos, a politician originally famous for opposing a school ladder that killed a bunch of kids when it fell down, was set up to steal from the hick vote one year but then figured it out and became a full-on demagogue and won the next year; constantly bought people off, or found dirt on them to get what he wanted; eventually found dirt on a Judge Irwin to stop his main rival from stealing his spot in the senate, but a series of events led to his own death: he slept with the sister of a doctor who was going to be the head of his new hospital (in a seemingly purely well-intentioned push on his part), then his backstabbing underlings told the doc, who killed Talos and himself; whole story is told as a romance as well, with the main character Jack Burden falling in love with that doctor's sister (and he was friends with the doc) before their falling out when Jack realized that she (Anne) didn't truly love him; Jack is complicit in the death of his father too because he dug up some bad shiz he did, showed him, father killed himself, then AFTER he found out that was his dad; interesting interlude into the story of two brothers, one of whom has an affair then the woman's husband finds out and kills himself, the slaves know because husband left his wedding ring under the pillow, Cass Mastern (the cheater bro) then joins the war and dies because he won't shoot anyone else; story seems out of place originally but ends up being VERY fitting because echoed in the plot with Willie and Anne; whole novel struggles with themes of identity and complexity in people and good/evil (does all good come from evil, because that's all we have, or did God create evil because creation couldn't be purely good or it's extension?; was Willie really a good person, and how did the "good guy" doctor end up being a murderer) and power and politics; meaning is never the event but the motion through the event

title: The Varieties of Religious Experience
author: William James
description: The religo-psychical experience is crazy, also real
memories: Series of lectures where he heavily relies on quotes of first-hand accounts of people going through religious experiences, wish it was more in his own voice; quotes got really repetitive, and didn't work for me as a device to convince me… could have if I was more unfamiliar, though? maybe?; basic points: more important to talk about the descriptive, rational experience of personal religion than the institutional stuff; then pessimistic (reborn) and optimistic (once born) varieties of people with religion, former are the more rational because the world is a bad place but the latter are blissfully unaware and good for them ("mind cure: god is well and so are you"); good thoughts are those that offer inner happiness, consistency with other opinions, or servicability for our needs; talks about conversion, and how it's nearly always sudden -- striking and all within an instant, he says that comes from unconscious activity in the brain suddenly made conscious; goals of conversion (or morals) is to achieve consistency in whole self; when any of the things religion is good for are done in excess, pretty bad (chastity, piety, etc.) like surrendering your thought and will to God wholly is the ultimate, but the radical love for others is a good thing -- no excess possible, good for society; but poverty can be healthy to explore as well; lots of talk of mysticism, and how it's true for the person experiencing it but not for anyone else ot believe -- orison, or movement to god, generally is detached from the real world obviously, and temporary, but feel extremely real afterwards; God is limitless unity, so you're united with God when you pray and when you don't youre stil in Him, etc.; thought in movement -> belief (thought in rest); knowledge about life is one thing, living it is another; says that God exists in the end because prayer produces real effect

title: Lord of the Rings
author: JRR Tolkien
description: Servant eavesdrops, rewarded with eternal fame
memories: Similar enough to the movies, big differences include: Merry and Pippin growing in height after drinking from the Ent's food/water; Saruman showing up in the Shire with the humans, and Wormtongue cutting his throat after Merry and Pippin lead a revolt; Aragorn is more of a badass in the books, healed people; the ghost people didn't fight in the battle at Minis Tirith, they got released after stealing the boats; Tom Bombadil existed; I generally liked the narrative structure, no horribly dull parts, but the first fellowship THEN frodo/sam structure left something to be desired; the battles weren't very exciting, hard to know what was happening/when they landed; generally the book probably didn't have to be 1000 pages long; mostly felt like Tolkien wanted to (and successfully did) create a badass world, the setting of the book truly felt huge and diverse; so much setting description, though, got boring

title: A Prayer for Owen Meany
author: John Irving
description: THE VOICE WAS RIGHT ALL ALONG
memories: Loved Owen's voice, both with ALL CAPS and I can imagine the child-like nasal-i-ness; such an interesting take on childhood/families, all of the characters are complex and interesting and you can see people grow up; Johnny, narrator, originally just trusts Owen in his miraculous stuff but then grows to doubt, but then obv back once Owen saves him in the closet and reveals his dad to him; best I've ever seen knowledge withheld from a reader for dramatic effect, but so intentionally/transparently ("i'll tell you more later"); arm imagery everywhere, Owen eventually had his arms stolen; I didn't understand why Hester loved Owen so much? disdain for society helped I suppose; best opening sentence ever; lose someone piece by piece, not all at once

title: Mythologies
author: Roland Barthes
description: Austere, french, inside jokes for days
memories: The first 2/3 of the book sucked -- literally just stories about French culture that I didn't have any frame of reference for, and pretty petty/uselessly philosophic analysis; the story about the girl poet was interesting ( Minou Drouet), the car one was intolerable and ridiculous; the ideas in the final 1/3 were actually pretty cool... the normal signifier, signified, sign trio just becomes the signifier for myth -- myth as reflexive, second-order analysis stuff; the benefit of myth (for the evil) is that it divorces those concepts from their history, so it can make use of them for political or other gains; meaning vs. form like window and landscape outside -- can't see both at the same time; myth "transforms history into nature"; 7 steps of myth at the end are worth re-visiting

title: The Satanic Verses
author: Salman Rushdie
description: He's either Archangel Gabriel, or a schizophrenic
memories: Very unique style, hopped back and forth between dream, schizophrenic episode, and reality; was surprisingly emotional by the end; truly unique and effective writing style, could feel the differences in characters (particularly in the schizo scene with Gibreel at the end); unpredictable and inconclusive, in a good way -- never knew what was gonna happen or what was real; the parables were awesome, Mahound and Ayesha; cool takes on about everything, beautifully, poetically, parable-ly written

title: The Innovator's Dilemma
author: Clayton Christensen
description: Boring industries; decent theory
memories: Idea is that innovation outpaces the needs of the market, so new products steal share at the lower end of the market with worse products, but then those products get better and end up stealing the whole market; gotta innovate with a small enough org to make their incentives aligned; big firms can and do create the tech but don't have the right cost structures to play ball; manage like your forecasts are wrong; differentiation doesn't matter in commodities because the market just needs what it needs, no more

title: Improv Wisdom
author: Patricia Ryan Madson
description: Took 20 years to write a mediocre improv book?
memories: Don't try to be perfect, just show up and start; say yes; be average; take care of others in convo, reacting to how things are and not how you wanted things to be

title: Getting to Plan B
author: John Mullins & Randy Komisar
description: Pivots are cool
memories: Don't guess, validate; don't be afraid to switch up your business model; find analogs and antilogs

title: The Credibility Code
author: Cara Hale Alter
description: Speak good, business good
memories: Make eye contact, use your voice well, stand with legs further apart, etc.

title: Inside the Tornado
author: Geoffrey Moore
description: METAPHORS ARE BUSINESS NOW
memories: Start by tackling specific niches and making a great product for a few people; then once you hit enough "bowling pins" you have a whole product, and the TORNADO hits; once early adopters like you, pragmatists can see you as the GORILLA, market leader, and you are the winner because you're a standard

title: Crossing the Chasm
author: Geoffrey Moore
description: METAPHORS ARE BUSINESS NOW
memories: Takes different marketing approaches to hit different customer segments, based on whether people are visionaries or boring ppl; boring people care more about industry than product; to cross the chasm and get boring people, you have to secure a beachhead product, just one small portion of a market, before expanding

title: Originals
author: Adam Grant
description: World-changing originality is part-science
memories: A pop-psych book that didn't suck! Unreal!; original people are really risky in one area and balanced risk portfolio elsewhere; originals feel the same fear, just do it anyways; there is no quantity/quality tradeoff -- just write a ton of shit, make a lot of ideas; peers evaluating peers is the best method; have open feedback channels and have people bring problems, not solutions; tell a skeptical audience the reasons not to believe in you; procrastination can work if productive; timing is the biggest killer of startups; tempered radicals -- obfuscate the reason you really want to change, make it palatable; people on the inside think the center defines the movement, but from the outside, it's the radicals; if you're trying to take risks, don't think about consequences, think about if something is "appropriate" given who you want to be; explain reason to your kids, they'll grow up to help people (as with holocaust helpers); criticism of ideas helps! as long as people think others are looking out for their best interests; unearth true devil's advocates, don't assign people to be one. huge difference in the commitment of the alternative viewpoint; argue like you're right, listen like you're wrong; anxiety is okay before situations, just transmute it to excitement; people are suspicious of leaders, want to hear from the actual people helped by a product/program; use the loss-riskiness stuff to incite change, people are more likely to take risky action when they're trying to avoid losses; that's why you have to make people not like the status quo; EB White: "I arise in the morning torn between a desire to improve the world and a desire to enjoy the world. This makes it difficult to plan the day."

title: Elon Musk
author: Ashlee Vance
description: Worldview + Recruiting + Indomitable Will = Musk
memories: Couldn't put this book down… fever dream helped, basically spent all night then all day reading; Musk's formula really wasn't anything crazy -- he had a worldview he genuinely believed in and could rally others around; he took on huge, slow, boring industries and poached all the best talent from them because he was small, fast, and exciting; then he was the ultimate pace-setter and forced those around him to follow suit; also he got super fucking lucky; why didn't he just buy a house in SF? still staying on his friends couches; his problems matter, unlike most people who try to get others to click on ads; critical path = shitlist for people who are holding up the whole company; Tesla wasn't allowed to sell cars, so they sold a club membership for $100,000 that gave you a free car; one great engineer > three mediocre ones; either you're trying to make something spectacular, or you aren't; VCs hated Tesla in the beginning; Elon fired his long-time assistant, who really helped him with everything

title: The 22 Immutable Laws of Marketing
author: Al Ries and Jack Trout
description: …guys I think some of these are mutable
memories: Lead, don't follow -- just be the first in something; basically there were two interesting human behavior tidbits and a lot of BS otherwise that wasn't true; Trump made an appearance, ugh; making a big impression on someone happens in your first impression, hard to build slowly to great; no objective reality, but most people think they're better perceivers than everyone else; go afer one thing; don't try to be better, be different -- do the opposite of what the powerful do; the target is not the market, hit a small group then go out; forecast your competition, not just your market

title: Zero to One
author: Peter Thiel
description: Founders can change the world
memories: Lots of good lessons, only tempered by my disgust for Thiel as a human (supporting Trump, blood boys, etc.); "hard to blame people for dancing when the music was playing" re dot com boom, it was rational to be irrational; monopolies are good because competition crushes profits and disincentivizes innovation; but, our systems love and promote competition; your tech has to be 10x better than the competition to gain a real advantage; Western culture is currently in indefinite optimism, we think the future will be better but we don't know how so we aren't working to create it; VC works when individual companies return the entire fund; don't make a ton of bets with the hope some pay off... if you play the lottery, you've already psychologically prepared yourself to lose; hard truths are the things that are worth thinking about, like in religion, they have easy truths you believe and things that are unknowable like God's will, but nothing interesting in between, but all good founders have to have a secret (hard thought); secrets can be of nature (e.g., undiscovered part of physical world) or people (what do people not want you to know about them); a great company is a conspiracy to change the world, and the secret is hidden from the outside; you can't be Google 2017 with dope perks, but you can be Google 1999 with the right mission and team; everybody is responsible for only one thing; seven questions -- Engineering, Timing, Monopoly, People, Distribution, Durability, Secret

title: The Lean Startup
author: Eric Reis
description: Stay small, be fast, get customer feedback
memories: This is one of those books that was more important than I give it credit for… I already knew most of the things in the book (e.g.,  MVP), but maybe this book created that concept?; didn’t love the reliance on his own case study, but I get it; run 1 test, get politicians for have to lobby for resoures, run 100 tests, you get entrepreneurs; until you know how to sell, don't engineer; MVP = smallest product that allows the Build Measure Learn loop; genchi gembutsu, go and see for yourself; have each "stage" of development capped at a certain # of projects, so you can't complete until you've learned from prior stuff; do COHORT analysis, see what % of people move through different stages, rather than raw cumulative #s; millions of kinds of pivots; smaller batch sizes are good, let you get feedback faster, see if anything is broken, maximizes total performance even if it sacrifices the individual "machine" performance; MVP converts push to pull and reduces batch size, both of which reduce "WIP" which is just undelivered work, even if intellectual; viral growth = above 1.0 coefficient of virality; sticky = keep customers for a long time, you want growth rate > churn; paid = CPA (cost per acquisition) > LTV (lifetime value) of each customer; be tolerant of mistakes the first time, never let mistakes happen twice;

title: Predictably Irrational
author: Dan Ariely
description: Econ is wrong, but irrationality is predictable
memories: Good pop-psych book, lots of interesting experiments, not particularly compelling because I already sorta knew/expected all this?; most people don't know what they want (or how they value things) without context, like that experiment with the Economist print/web subscription; arbitrary coherence is when you get anchored by your SSN or a phone number when estimating something, once anchored on a price, hard to shift very far from that; herding when you assume something is good because others like it; "what if our lives are nothing more than arbitrary coherence," aka making up sense from a series of mostly random events; market norms (money) can crowd out social norms (good will); easier to steal when it's things that are non-monetary; overvalue what you own; zero is categorically different than other prices (might make social norms apply, not market); we have a hard time predicting how we act when "hot" aka aroused in any way

title: Liar's Poker
author: Michael Lewis
description: Traders make money because someone has to
memories: Insider take of making money at Salomon Brothers, a leading investment bank, through their heyday selling bonds to their demise before and during the stock market crash in 1987; Liar's Poker itself is a game where you hold a dollar bill to your chest and go around a circle saying how many of each digit are on the bills of everyone combined -- math is "easy," all a matter of guessing what others think; talks about the bond market mostly, which seems boring and was boring until mortgage-backed securities existed; Salomon never really made it to junk bonds, which made Millken tons o money; investment bankers would keep the good stuff for themselves and sell the bad stuff to their clients; training program was crazy, kids pelted partners with paper wads, had the front row nerds from Harvard then the back row people who just hooted and hollered; goal was to become a Big Swinging Dick; Lewis Ranieri was the head MBS guy, and led a team of fat dudes that ate cheeseburgers for breakfast to generate tons of money -- they even made fun of people for running triathlons on the weekend; nobody wanted equities in Dallas, equity traders took the trainees on a boat ride and they described the next morning as a coyote morning: chew off your arm, don't wake the person you slept with

title: Born Standing Up
author: Steve Martin
description: A wild and crazy guy finds a comedy niche
memories: Cool story of his his act developed over time then skyrocketed into mega-fame; seemed like the turning point was when he realized how his act could be different (don't have punch lines, assume the persona of someone who sucks at magic but doesn’t know how much he sucks); lots of talk of his lovers, a little self-serving ("we were all beautiful"); some of the success does seem random but the way he tells it, he was only encouraged by people who told him to quit; he had a brief period of opener-success where he got on the nightly shows but only as a side act, had to take a step down to have his own show, but that skyrocketed him to fame; classic "fame sucks" stories, which are the kind of problems worth having; he was really lonely when at the absolute top, his act turned into a greatest hits instead of actually trying new jokes; used to lead people out of the club, had crowd get into a swimming pool and he swam across them; he was anxious for 20 years

title: Influence
author: Robert Cialdini
description: People use decision shortcuts; salesmen exploit them
memories: One of those old book where you don't know if the author discovered all this stuff for the first time or if it was just rehashing old stuff; the principles he highlights are -- reciprocation: works even if your favor is small and your ask is big; commitment and consistency: people look to their past actions, want to stay consistent, works even if those original acts were compulsory; social proof: if others are doing it, it's right/cool; liking: be attractive, or at least like other people; authority: Milgram stuff; scarcity: bring your customers to the store at the same time, make them fight for a product; "because" is a trigger word, more likely to comply if you say because and give ANY reason; rejection then retreat = start with high offer, going down feels like a "compromise" and they're more likely to accept; toy stores undersuppply the cool toys before holidays, parents buy stuff before anyways but have to fulfill their promises to kids so go back to buy more after holidays; the consistency stuff works best when there is NO good external reason other than internalizing it as a part of your personality, the war camp stuff in China (write an essay about how great communism is) had a SMALL prize; scarcity implies people want to avoid losses more than secure gains; terrible twos are toddlers' explorations of what it means to have agency and be a person; experiencing scarce commodities < possessing them

title: Let The Great World Spin
author: Colum McCann
description: Everybody has a story
memories: Ten perspectives in one book, all intertwined; Corrigan the priest who took care of hookers and kept his vows until he met a Hispanic woman he loved; Ciaran the brother/observer who had sex with Tillie; Tillie the hooker's mother who promised Jazzlyn would never go on the streets but did, who went to prison and eventually got to meet her two grandkids, who had "a body that Adam would have left Eve for... Jesus himself woulda been in the background  saying Adam, you're one lucky motherfucker"; Jazzlyn, Tillie's daughter, who probably loved Corrigan but flew through his windshield during the crash that killed them both; Jaslyn, Jazzlyn's daughter and Gloria's daughter, fell in love on a plane and was a great friend to Claire even at the end of her life; Claire, the nervous housewife who just needed a friend after her son died in the war; Gloria, who became that friend and who adopted Jazzlyn's two daughters after a night of getting mugged and Claire's kindness (and being reminded of her three kids who died in the war) and who said she gave her parents "all of the truth and none of the honesty" after college; the judge who was Claire's husband, seems like he was a washed up Yalie who wanted to be more than what he became, but he got to sentence the tightrope walker, also had a friend at a nearby restaurant who said wine was "to glorify the gods and dull the idiots"; the walker, who created something amazing for a whole lot of people by walking between the twin towers on a line; the hackers who placed bets on if the walker would fall and die; the artists who were washed up druggies and drove the car that killed Jazzlyn and Corrigan, particularly the wife who felt terrible afterwards, went to the funeral, stole the box of Corrigan's stuff from the hospital but returned it to him, then became a friend of Tillie's in prison, brought her her babies; the photographer of street art who had the "you have to look on the world with a shine like no one else has"; the Spanish woman who Corrigan loved, they slept together for the first time the night before the accident and who wondered in the wake of Corrigan's death whether he would have stuck with his vows or her;

title: The Black Swan
author: Nassim Nicholas Taleb
description: Stats is horribly broken by mega-outliers
memories: Seems true, seems like this up-ends my idea of stats being awesome; basic thesis is that we oftentimes live in "Extremistan" where outliers can ridiculously skew stats to the point where it's useless (e.g., one day in five years of stock returns counts for 50% of total variation); there are some things where Gaussian distributions actually hold (physical limits like height of a person), but most important things aren't Gaussian (they're Mandelbrotian; like wealth) -- test is to imagine two people who combine to something extreme and ask if they're equal or ridiculously unequal (e.g., 14 foot tall combined is for sure 7ft and 7ft, versus $1B net worth is likely 75,000 and just less than 1B); things are almost never like their Platonic forms in practice, so the "ludic fallacy" is  assuming real probabilities are like the fake ones in your lab (or casino); we tend to narrativise the past, even though we have no idea why most things happened (retroactive reduction in complexity and variation); knowledge of the individual minutiae of daily business can be bad for your decision making --show people a blurry and then step-wise less blurry fire hydrant picture, those who  see fewer intermediate steps recognize it first; "expert problem" is deference to authority and platonic models that have no bearing to reality, like the "Nobel" in economics; variation matters more than the guess in estimation; future is unpredictable b/c MacIntyre card from debate about inventing the wheel; contingencies are crazy impossible to predict -- the ninth bounce of a pool ball can be affected by someone STANDING NEXT TO THE TABLE; maximize the serendipity around you to exploit positive black swans; 85-90% totally riskless investment, 10% CRAZY speculative == barbell; his advice: invest in preparedness not prediction, seize every opportunity (open-ended payoff) ever, go to parties to learn new random ideas, beware of precise plans by corps or govt; Heisenberg uncertainty is silly to focus on when there are such bigger fish to fry, like "will my marriage survive" or "will Trump get elected"; be very aggressive in pursuing positive black swans, very conservative when avoiding negative ones; life takes place in the preasymptote, not the Platonic long run

title: Thanks, Obama
author: David Litt
description: He was definitely there; Obama is the real deal
memories: White House memoir from a speechwriter for Obama; Obama was brilliant and always the smartest guy in the room, except for that first Mitt Romney debate; you get a different color badge if you can go into the actual white house; healthcare.gov really sucked

title: Sapiens
author: Yuval Noah Harari
description: Well, humanity escalated quickly
memories: We beat Neanderthals (who were physically larger and had bigger brains than us) because of language; we then used that to basically destroy the ecosystem of the world and make all other big animals extinct within centuries of our first access to them; first big change was the agricultural revolution, where we learned to domesticate wheat and animals -- probably around making a huge temple or something, allowed for higher concentration of people in one place; then was the scientific revolution which Harari credits to being able to admit our ignorance and start questioning stuff; that sci revolution basically let us have another agricultural revolution too, where we started mass-producing and oppressing animals even more; then obviously the huuuge developments of the last 100 years, pace of change is rapidly accelerating; we are now one world community, where for a long time people lived in totally separate worlds; author makes lots of bad jokes (not funny and kinda undermine his argument), but that can be forgiven because he's a great researcher, it seems; a few fact-checky moments where I think he massaged facts to fit a narrative (e.g., giraffe necks for higher leaves); low life expectancy back in the day was mostly due to child mortality; makes a big point about "imagined orders" like Christianity, nation-states, and corporations -- cool analysis of how to make someone believe in one (which was necessary to unite into societies, he claims); we all build "pyramids" e.g., shows of our wealth and nice houses; genetic superiority of africans to diseases in america were a main reason why they became slaves; money = mutual trust, counterfeiting = treason to the king's signature on the money, value of money is how much OTHERs believe in it, because arbitrage evens out market prices for stuff; Christians slaughtered other Christians "to defend slightly different interpretations of the religion of love and compassion"; syncretism (combining parts of religions we like) is the real global religion; history shows we have more possibilities than we would otherwise think possible; "the real test of knowledge is not whether it is true, but whether it empowers us"; western culture won the global war because of capitalism and modern science, both paved the way to be ready to exploit technology and exploration to the fullest; "empty maps" is a bomb-ass idea -- when we learned to accept that we didn't know everything -> exploration; if profits of production are reinveted in increasing production, capitalism works -- but not if people just get filthy rich *cough*Bezos*cough*; industrialists argue that capital can influence politics, but politics can't influence capital -- SO TRUE, WTF; Napoleon gave his most distinguished guests Aluminum, because it was rare, and others had to use gold

title: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone
author: JK Rowling
description: The perfect introduction to a complete world
memories: Hagrid actually says "Harry… yer a wizard"; Hagrid has a drinking problem, swears it off after giving up the secret to getting past Fluffy to Quirrell; forgot about the keys challenge, or that there was another ogre protecting the Sorcerer's Stone as a challenge; Dumbledore is weirder than I remembered; the foreshadowing / character quirks that only make sense after knowing everything are CRAZY -- like the Snape disdain for Harry, I know *why* now!; perfect example of no wasted motion in a novel, everything is called back for a reason, only slight "why" was the leaving the invisibility cloak after the dragon-to-the-tower incident then just sorta getting it back

title: Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets
author: JK Rowling
description: Harry saves his future wife from an evil diary
memories: Gilderoy Lockhart is such a hate-able character; Rowling is sooo good at making sure everything introduced in the book actually becomes useful to the plot; Hermione gets petrified by the Basilisk; Lockhart gets taken by Ron and Harry into the Chamber of Secrets; their car rescues them from Aragog, the huge spider that got Hagrid expelled (b/c Voldemort convinced the headmaster Aragog was Slytherin's creature); Voldy is the heir of Slytherin IRL, gave Harry parselmouth (NOT TONGUE!) in the attack; Hagrid gets thrown in AZKABAN because they think he's behind the attacks

title: Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
author: JK Rowling
description: Turns out that Ron's rat betrayed Harry's parents
memories: Lupin is the DADA teacher; Moony (Lupin, warewolf), Wormtail (Peter Pettigrew, rat animagus), Padfood (Sirius, dog animagus), Prongs (James Potter, stag animagus) made the marauder's map; it was Lupin in the shrieking shack all along, path goes to the whomping willow; Sirius sends Harry a firebolt; plot device of the timeturner is awesome, but there was soooo much exposition with the Sirius/Pettigrew/Elder Potter storyline; Trelawney makes a real prediction that pettigrew will help Voldy come back to power; first patronus book, Harry learns from Lupin; the marauders almost killed Snape as a kid by sending him to Lupin, Harry's dad saved Snape

title: Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire
author: JK Rowling
description: Baby Voldemort fights teenage Harry; Cedric dies
memories: So much good in here, mostly Rowling wrote this as a book for adults (and/or just older people) -- lots of heavier themes, like House-Elf rights, poverty (Ron: "I hate being poor."), life and death, people killing their fathers,  unabashed pursuit of power and how that can blind you (e.g., Fudge); Dumbledore is fallible, lets in the Polyjuice Moody somehow; Rowling is the queen of using every plot device to the fullest (e.g., Priori Incantatem after dark mark, also in Voldy's graveyard scene), Winky plotline with invisibility-cloaked Fudge the Younger, Fake News Rita Skeeter, Wormtail's escape, veritaserum, portkeys, Bertha Jorkins death and secret-keeping from Fudge then breaking to Voldy; Malfoy/Goyle/Crabbe parents instantly folded and went back to Voldy; Herm-own-ninny explanation; Krum as a real teenager was a good touch, he just loved Hermione; beauxbatons sent boys and girls to the Triwizard Tourney; first task wasn't as exciting as in the movies, nor was the third; "spy-d-errr"; both Harry and Voldy's wands have feathers from Fawkes in them; Sirius as secret, protective godfather is awesome; there's a powerful protective spell on the Dursley's house; "parting of the ways" between Dumbledore and a politically-blinded Fudge

title: Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix
author: JK Rowling
description: Harry is the moodiest…. He should've listened to Hermy
memories: Really dramatic book that's almost ruined by how shitty of a hero Harry is -- he's SO moody, it's annoying, always snapping at Ron and Hermione; this is the emergence of mature, smart Hermione, and a secretly super-talented Ginny; starts with the Dementors, Harry uses Patronus and gets expelled but then has a hearing, goes to meet the Order of the Phoenix at (also moody) Sirirus' house at Grimmald Place, goes to trial at the Ministry of Magic and is saved from mean Fudge/Umbridge by Dumbledore's lawyer skills, goes to school where Umbridge starts as DAD teacher but quickly becomes High Inquisitor with Inquisitorial Squad and such, Luna emerges, carving into skin pen at detention, plan Dumbledore's Army where Luna, Neville, Ginny and crew learn spells, lots of drama with Cho, Harry kisses her but is clueless on their first date and Cho gets jealous re: Hermione, Hagrid returns after a long time away trying to court giants (and bringing back Grawp), Harry sees Ron's Dad get attacked in a vision, saves his life but that alerts Voldy to the connection caused by his scar, Harry fucks up Occulmency lessons by making Snape give up memories of being bullied via pensieve too, Hermione gets Rita Skeeter to publish the truth about Voldy interaction at graveyard in the Quibbler, Centaur stuff, OWLs, Ministry of Magic showdown (baited by Voldy's acclumens, trying to get the prophesy made by Trelawney at her Hogwarts interview) leads to Order of the Phoenix having to save the DA and they do, but Bellatrix kills Sirius, Dumbledore fights off Voldemort and the fake news saga is over and Ministry is forced to admit Voldy is back; Umbridge is a racist against half-breeds, ends up being her downfall with the centaurs who "won't attack fawns"

title: Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince
author: JK Rowling
description: SNAPE KILLS DUMBLEDORE
memories: Surprise new favorite on the re-read -- just so much good character development and BOLD MFING STORYTELLING by Rowling here; Dumbledore is obviously the trump card for the Voldy resistance, and having him die is so ridiculously unexpected but makes sense to let Book 7 be all about Harry and Voldemort; Snape is the greatest character of all time, so complex/deep and clearly tormented, so good but so petty, etc; starts with the Minister of Magic talking to Prime Minister scene, then Bellatrix putting the unbreakable vow on Snape + Narcissa, then Horace Slughorn comes in and his whole purpose is the memory of the Horcruxes (and to teach Harry potions by prompting saves by the Half-Blood Prince, who is Snape in the end), Harry suspects Draco the whole time and Dumbledore KNEW it was coming, but couldn't confront Draco or Voldemort would kill Draco and his family, Slug Club meetings, the awesome Harry-Ginny love story and Ron-Hermione one, both started by the Felix Felicitis that Harry takes to get the memory from Slughorn (makes Ginny break up with Dean, and Ron break up with Lavender), Hagrid is mad because they're not taking care of magical creatures, also inconsolably sad after Dumbledore dies, great slow learning by the reader in Dumbledore's lessons, his dissatisfaction with Harry when he doesn't get the memory from Slughorn is awesome, the cave with Inferi and Dumbledore's dying power (for a FAKE locket!) with the fire and paralyzing Harry once Malfoy lets in the death eaters (and they're battled in Hogwarts by Dumbledore's Army and the Order of the Phoenix, then Malfoy can't do it and Snape has to kill Dumbledore, Dumbledore's last words are "Severus... Severus, please..." and there's a brief moment where Snape shows his emotion for killing Dumbledore when Harry is chasing them across the lawn, then Harry has to leave Ginny to find the horcruxes after an emotional Dumbledore funeral... what a fucking ending and setup for the last book, all the budding love (Tonks and Lupin, Bill and Fleur, Fleur making up with Mrs. Weasley, etc.) after Dumbledore's death is what he would have wanted

title: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows
author: JK Rowling
description: The perfect ending to a perfect story
memories: Snape is the greatest character in all of any form of fiction -- tortured, singly devoted to his love of one person, and willing to brave literally anything to see Lily's eyes in Harry; Dumbledore got most everything right, except that Voldemort would kill Snape (wonder if he knew that DRACO was the true master of the Elder Wand?); Harry repairs his original wand in the end with the Elder Wand; starts with the seven Harrys, which went Dumbledore -> Snape -> Mundungus; Hedwig dies and George loses his ear; Dumbledore's will: Harry gets the first snitch he caught with the sorcerer's stone & "i open at the close", Hermione gets the Deathly Hallows book, Ron gets the deluminator because he's a regretful deserter; the wedding -> ministry falls, Shacklebolt's patronus "The Ministry has fallen. He is coming."; quest for the horcruxes begins: Ministry of Magic -> Umbridge -> locket; then Doe -> sword -> locket death; then Xenophilius -> Three brothers thing -> malfoy manor -> dobby death; then Gringotts for the cup; then Hogwarts for the diadem (dead by fiendfyre, Crabbe dies) and basilisk fang -> cup; Aberforth Dumbledore helps them; Fred dies; then to the whomping willow -> shrieking shack, where voldy kills snape, snape gives Harry the memory, Voldy gives Harry an hour to turn himself over; Harry finds Lupin, Tonks, Colin Creevy all dead; runs to Dumbledore's office for the pensieve, learns that he has to sacrifice himself and Snape was a good-ish guy, Harry goes to the woods, opens the snitch and turns the sorcerer's stone, gets the strength to sacrifice himself and does, but doesn't die; Neville kills Nagini; Mrs. Weasley kills Bellatrix "NOT MY DAUGHTER, YOU BITCH!"; Harry + Voldy have a long pre-battle speech then Harry expelliarmouses himself to victory; then Harry marries Ginny, Ron Hermione, kids are Albus Severus, Lily, and James Potter; Rose and Hugo Weasley; Scorpius Malfoy; Teddy Lupin, snogging on the train. Harry's scar doesn't hurt.

title: Venture Deals
author: Brad Feld & Jason Mendelson
description: Boring but useful book on venture finance
memories: Hire a good lawyer but not a jerk; all that matters is economics and control, go into the negotiation knowing what your VC generally expects/wants, and ask them what their most important terms are; you should probably only care about participation/preference, valuation, board control/protective provisions and such; watch out for some ridiculous terms like full ratchet; don't ask for a referral

title: Cryptoassets
author: Chris Burniske & Jack Tatar
description: Dumb title/concept, but good review
memories: Arguing for complex finance to have a place within crypto; makes arguments for network effects via developers; precursor to monero was ecash, 100moffer to integrate into windows 95 but rejected…; endpoint sensitivity; dollar cost averaging = buy in slowly; order book thin = nobody buying and selling; volatility = stdev of percent price change; price over long term is just utility value; bitcoin has doubled in price over one month six times; (my idea from reading: fat protocol are dumb, because you don't capture 100% of the total profit in the blockchain world... you give some back as consumer surplus. race to the bottom means that consumers keep getting more and more... fat consumers; e.g. from book: money remittance is 7.5% vs cents from bitcoin)

title: A Supposedly Fun Thing I'll Never Do Again
author: David Foster Wallace
description: DFW saw better than any of us; bummer he's gone
memories: Collection of essays, classic DFW topics -- state fair, tennis, tennis, cruise ship, television/media, movie guy, literary theory; first essay is tennis, all about how he outlasted kids by playing the middle of the court in the wind, then learning to harness it really well, he was a borderline-great player but eventually got outgrown when he didn't hit puberty, all about how kids pursue greatness, friendships and sport; second one, he argues that television took the "i am being watched and know it, but I'm pathologically able to pretend like I'm not being watched" role, which took something away from avant garde fiction, now people are kinda banal and just show disjointed scenes and such, he predicts that eventually the subversive role might be played by just straight earnestness; third one is the illinois state fair, all about the gross food/rides, the people who travel from fair to fair as carnies or whatever, how people go there because everyone else is going there, travels with his female friend who is cool with getting harrassed by the carnies; fourth is pomo stuff I didn't really follow; fifth is about David Lynch, who had a good movie then a lot of duds, but DFW admires how Lynch sticks to his pretty gross style; sixth is about Michael Joyce, tennis pro, explains the process of qualifying for the big tourneys, getting smoked day in and out, how he thinks he's good but then goes to this thing and they're a whole different level, Joyce never really chose to get this good but now it's his whole life and he's just participating in it, etc; last essay is great, about a cruise he goes on and he just eviscerates the whole idea of cruises, basically he calls out the weird marketing about pampering and bliss, and he clearly chooses to see the stuff you're supposed to ignore like Greek mafia bosses running the ship and how you look as a mass of white people landing on the shore, etc.; "authors are monkeys who mean"

title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
author: Ben Horowitz
description: Management book that admits startups are hard
memories: Amazing, real war stories from a CEO unafraid to admit how hard it is to be a CEO; I feel like I'll go back to this as companies I build scale; don't always be positive with employees, be real; CEO is about choosing between horrible and worse; beware hiring "out of central casting," e.g., the sales rep who was a short, hardass but an absolute sales savant; needs trump wants in M&A; sometimes only the founder has the courage to ignore all the data; markets are good at converging, not necessarily on the truth; ask "what am i not doing"; WFIO: We're Fucked, It's Over; difference between hero and coward isn't what they feel, it's what they do; show it, sell it; hide it, keep it;

title: A Higher Loyalty
author: James Comey
description: Political-hopeful leads the FBI; screws over America
memories: Mostly I leave this book being mad at Comey, but very happy that he took the notes he did with Trump. Before the election, he made absolute garbage decisions -- "conceal" vs. "disclose" is a ridiculous binary, and why wouldn't it equally apply to Trump investigation stuff?; he assumed Hillary would be elected and that affected his choice

title: The Inevitable
author: Kevin Kelly
description: Futures thinking seems like fun
memories: Most of his ideas are reeeeallly generic, but smart; he presents an irrationally optimistic future (intentionally) and some ideas are really cool, e.g., everything is streaming, tech changes so fast we are never used to any tech, everything has AI, filtering algorithms are crazy important because there is just way too much content, remixing is super important and possible with video just like quoting in a book, the section about "Screening" aka staring at screens was really depressing, everything ever will be tracked soon, the little kid thinking analog pictures should have pinch functionality is crazy, the girl got violently ill when her phone was taken, commercialization on the Internet was illegal originally; five nines of uptime performance (99.999%); bottom up is the best way to start but a bad way to scale well, even Wikipedia needs some editors; no information scarcity means attention scarcity; new information is growing at 66% every year, which is almost exactly the same as Moore's law -- information is created to fill available space; anonymity is good in small doses only; what about a Wikipedia for laws in the US??

title: We Were Eight Years in Power
author: Ta-Nehisi Coates
description: Racism is essential to the American story
memories: Drug war was just a way to get more black people in jail, "cocaine-crazed negroes"; the idea of the black family being broken is not a necessity, born from slavery where families were separated; like Bill Cosby, who blamed black people for their own probelms, Obama did some of the same; Obama trusted white people, so they could trust him back; society accepts black slavery as normal, white as bad hence "white working class" stuff which is nuts because "white" was the explanatory variable; Southern leaders literally said that black oppression allowed all white men to be free and equal; difference between Obama and Trump proves racism

title: Astrophysics for People in a Hurry
author: Neil Degrasse Tyson
description: Complex science can be understood simply
memories: Basically just walks through physics and biology concepts to explain them simply and in an accessible way, not much I didn't already know (other than remembering bosons or whatever); more varieties of carbon-based molecules exist than all others combined; helium comes from helios, found on the sun (only element first discovered not on Earth); old lightbulbs are so inefficient because they give off infrared light that comes off as heat, that's why LEDs are so efficient and not hot; NDGT thinks that the cosmic perspetive could help our earthly problems fade away

title: Nudge
author: Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein
description: Simple idea: people are lazy, don't change from default
memories: The idea is super simple and pretty self-explanatory: people can make radically different choices depending on seemingly minor things; feels like this is radical to an economist but obvious to everyone else, of course people are most likely to stick with a default option!; applications across many, many industries were cool; nudges are changes that don't restrict choices, they advocate for libertarian paternalism, nice nudges, and transparency with all data

title: Turtles All The Way Down
author: John Green
description: OCD girl battles with disease, young love, and friendship
memories: Just so enjoyable to read -- witty, funny, at times profound, intriguing with the weird quirky details based on the personalities/hobbies of the characters (e.g, Star Wars fanfic, astronomy); all about Aza, who has OCD/anxiety, and how she struggles to go through the world with her disease, picking her finger scab, sweating, eventually drinking hand sanitizer to avoid germs from kissing; her thought spirals are pretty spot on for how anxiety feels, you can't choose your feelings, etc.; friend frustrated but loving in the end, guy can't really make it work; cool perspective shift at the end, that the girl couldn't know how her story ends way down the line, similar to the reference he made to a classic book where one of the characters asks James Joyce to pull her out of the narrative; "went out to the meadow" meaning lost ourselves in the music/flow;

title: Talking to Humans
author: Giff Constable
description: Here's an idea: talk to customers, but be smart
memories: Nothing ground-breaking, basic takeaways are to have a plan, have assumptions to test, be relentless, and get creative with finding people

title: Business Adventures
author: John Brooks
description: Business is boring unless John Brooks tells the story
memories: Lost all my notes here, ugh. Great book that makes boring topics fun: stock market fluctuation, Edsel (Ford car flub), federal income tax (and yacht exemption if for 50% business), insider trading for a mining firm, xerox as a success story (invented xerography then CSR), making defrauded stock market customers whole, GE's "communication problem" of saying to follow the law then winking, cornering the piggly wiggly stock, where you buy enough so all the people shorting the stock have to re-buy from you, Lilienthal (TVA lead, turned free market businessperson and development consultant, earnings calls and the hilarious people that actually go to stockholder meetings, ip protection and how people are allowed to leave companies but not steal secrets (astronaut suit guy, hired by latex), sterling currency crisis around devaluation and the gold standard and international monetary cooperation

title: The World As It Is
author: Ben Rhodes
description: Obama was the real deal
memories: Journey of a foreign policy and speechwriting guy who was around Obama always; Obama is a smoker, getting roasted by Fox News really sucks, Trump is a clown; mostly this book made me mad because they did soooooooo much work only for a vindictive Trump regime to tear down that progress; he led the Cuba negotiations and helped with the Iran deal; Obama was worried about his staffers on the day after the eleciton, that "said a lot"; Obama's foreign policy was "don't do stupid shit"; Benghazi was a conspiracy theory and a total joke of an investigation; was Obama 10 years too early?; Favs called election season a "pollercoaster"

title: Leonardo da Vinci
author: Walter Isaacson
description: Leonardo: Divergence, procrastination, and observation
memories: Leonardo's number one skill was observation -- he saw how water swirled in eddies and that a dragonfly's two sets of wings moved at different times; he was ahead of his time in SO many fields, from studying fossil records to human anatomy, physics to astronomy to dentistry; said to start observing with the details, don't try to understand the whole thing before you get and memorize the small parts; reasoned by analogy a lot, like from air flows to water flows; Leo was a collaborative person who worked best when surrounded by other geniuses (particularly those that focused him and wrote up their work); left-handed, so his hatching strokes from bottom right to top left are distictive signs of his work; didn't like to finish work (Isaacson says this is like sfumato smudging, meh) because he kept learning, like when he went back to fix neck muscles he painted some 15ish years later; he wanted to be known as an architect and engineer in his early career, his application to Sforza didn't mention painting; wrote in mirror script in his (innumerable) notebooks; Michaelangelo was a total dick; soooo many unfinished potential masterpieces, either due to bad paint or outside stuff (bronze for horse going to war)

title: The Cosmic Serpent
author: Jeremy Narby
description: DNA literally communicates with shamans
memories: Starts as a anthropologist with the Ashaninca Amazonian tribe, doing ayahuasca and seeing snakes among other visions, he's trying to protect their lands by showing how much they understand about plants and such; he quickly realizes that they know way too much to be randomly guessing at plants (e.g., 400 species of the poison that tips their darts, now used for surgery in the West, and it's really hard to make even once you have the right species) so they must have access to other knowledge that they claim comes from spiritual beings; he learns eventually to take them literally, and finds that his vision of snakes was incredibly common across all geographies and ancient tribes (even those that didn't have snakes and had never seen them!) and the twin snakes as creators of the world was the most common myth before Christianity and other man-first religions came to the fore; saying that the shamans literally talk to plants through their DNA sounds crazy, but that's where he ended up -- lots of arguments about how little we understand of evolution and natural selection, the fact that DNA emits photons (?!), junk DNA (which often repeats) may "hear" the signals put off by other DNA, and oter wonderful theories, but of course those fly in the face of everything we "know" about the world; he ultimately says anthropology and science aren't ready for his theories even though they are testable; he also thinks that DNA probably came from outer space (panspermia), because of the timing of life vs. when Earth cooled / that DNA has been entirely unchanged in structure for as long as we can see, counter to every other physical thing on Earth

title: An Anthropologist on Mars
author: Oliver Sacks
description: Mental disorders can bring good along with the bad
memories: Author is a super well-connected neurologist who writes biographies of his patients, basic thesis of these seven short stories was that defects can also give profound abilities; painter goes colorblind, hates it obv and calls things dirty, but learns to paint again anyways, and doesn't want to go back to colored sight (but glasses to change things to b/w helped him); hippie has a stroke, is totally unstuck in time and only in the present moment, very very calm, but can't remember the past (after 1960) and doesn't anticipate the future, huge Stones fan, also he's blind but can't be convinced that he's blind (!), claims to see things ; tourettic surgeon, totally loses his ticks when in "flow"; man blind throughout his life regains his vision via cataract surgery, but hates seeing, doesn't really understand how to use his eyes, and ultimately goes blind again seeming from sheer force of will; artist only ever draws his childhood city of Portito (or something like that), something to do with emotional trauma of youth, mixed with his nearly photographic memory; autistic savants for a chapter, follows a kid with amazing photographic memory, but basically no internal life, question is whether he can start developing personal relationships and the answer is basically no with a few exceptions; zoologist and animal rights activist is aspergian and can't relate to humans; autism is sometimes caused by rubella, which is obviously something you vaccinate AGAINST; people with tourettes often has super fast reaction times; sight has no pre-built "distance," we have to learn to see it, so take someone out of the Amazon and they won't understand that they can't reach out and grab a mountainto; GK Chesterton quote about science being impartiality, which makes no sense for humans that are obviously "partial" and subjective, so try to get inside the person to understand them

title: Draft No. 4
author: John McPhee
description: New Yorker writer has been doing this for fifty years
memories: Not as good of a writer as I would have expected, I thought the cut-and-paste style was a little disjointed and weird, the interludes from his stories were relevant but very poor transitions and guidance for me, the reader, as to what points he was trying to make; Draft 4 is where it is fun and comes together; follow what interests you and write down everything; a piece of writing has to move, so have a structure; writing the lead is really hard but sets up the whole tone and structure of the story; when introducing something new, start with an indefinite article ("A") rather than a definite one ("The")

title: The 4-Hour Workweek
author: Tim Ferriss
description: Automate a lifestyle business, travel the world
memories: Mix of productivity tips and supply chain automation stuff, genuinely game changing and inspirational; the goal is not to make a billion dollars, but to make enough money to be totally independent and work as little as possible on making money, freeing up time for what you really want; this sounds awesome, I'd just start like three of these, work 12 hours a week and make $120K a month; way too many practical tipsto list, but includes stuff like fear setting, dreamlining (dream timeline with steps for today and tomorrow), outsourcing everything to India, only answering emails on Mondays (oh god, panic attack), etc; what you fear most is what you most need to do; stop multitasking; "if this is the only thing I accomplish today, will I be satisfied"; call super successful people and ask for one super specific question; only two eye-tracking things per line if speed reading; have a reputation for being assertive, and difficult when it counts; "is that reasonable?"; if you spend your time ($25/hour) doing something you could outsource for $10/hour, it's bad economics; search for magazine advertising opportunities for your "muse"; explain your main benefit in one sentence or phrase; DO THE EXPERT THING; how to make your company look "Fortune 500": multiple emails on the site, don't be the founder and CEO,  hire a voice receptionist; "it is far better for a man to go wrong in freedom than to go right in chains" - thomas h. huxley

title: The Tiger
author: John Vaillant
description: Tigers are scary
memories: Story of a tiger attack on a human, and all the various theories that pop up as to why it happened; the central conflict is that in far east Russia, tigers and taiga-dwelling people basically live in harmony, tigers eat dogs sometimes but almost never attack humans; tigers are hella smart and will remember individuals who have wronged them or owe them things (e.g., they'll leave part of a kill and if a hunter takes it, the hunter now owes the tiger); really thrilling storytelling, gave good background but always tied it back to the main narrative, best part was that you were led to believe what the people were led to believe at the time, and then the author would be like "nope, but that's what we thought too"; measured by the back part of their paw, 5 inches is huge (500+ pounds) and they can still jump over a 10 foot fence; this tiger waited in a guys bed for him, hunted the humans because he was shot so many times; they can be 16 feet long and 900 pounds; game where hunters shot at each other in the dark;in 1930s Tanzania, 15 lions killed 1500 people; Viagra comes from the Sanskrit word for tiger; after perestroika, when russia became less communist and opened itself to the world (China) for trade, lots of Chinese people came hunting tigers for their folk powers like virility;

title: Shoe Dog
author: Phil Knight
description: Morality is secondary to winning for Knight
memories: Phil Knight isn't a bad guy, at least in his own eyes, but he isn't necessarily a good guy either. Just practical and did what he needed to to become a billionaire by selling shoes; started with a partnership with the legenday Orgeon running coach Bowerman, who was a consummate inventor; then recruited a ragtag bunch of dudes, most of whom weighed over 300 pounds, one in a wheelchair, one neurotic guy, and they built an epic company over 40 years; started by reselling Japanese shoes "Tigers" but doublecrossed the factory about to doublecross him, and started the Nike line; lots of bank troubles, kicked out of at least two for living in the margin and constantly reinvesting all the cash they earned into more shoes and bigger orders; a couple of his most defining moments were legal battles that they ultimately won; very little talk about Adidas, seemed like their strategy was all about being the hometown heroes and regular Joes; many times when he thought the company died; he seems a little neurotic and like me in the eye contact and holding himself (and winning obsession) stuff

title: Yes We (Still) Can
author: Dan Pfeiffer
description: Communications director can only tweet, not write
memories: Objectively not a very good writer, but definitely had a privileged position in the White House -- I'm certainly a better writer than this, which is cool; main point was all the Pod Save stuff, like Fox is ruining America, so is Twitter, that media is polarizing, etc., covered Obama birth certificate things; best parts were direct Obama quotes, and Obama having great relationship advice (how interesting are they?); no assholes rule on the Obama campaign; campaign strategy was "how do we win Iowa"; Pfeiffer has a blood vessel that enflamed in his brain and gives him mini-strokes if he gets stressed

title: The Undoing Project
author: Michael Lewis
description: Good partners share brains
memories: Amos Tverky and Daniel Kahneman were always the smartest people in the room until they met each other, sparked a super intense and productive relationship that redefined modern economics, psych, and a variety of industries; Amos was a crazy paratrooper bro for the army, one of his party tricks was to look like he was going to faceplant after falling from a height just to pull himself up at the last second; Kahneman was super insecure and wanted everyone to like him, and everyone generally loved Amos so he got all the credit, which made Kahneman mad and eventually "leave"; the end was devastating, they "broke up" and then three days later, Amos found out he had cancer; all the classic heuristics like availability, representativeness; Kahneman always wanted to ask "what is this true of?" rather than blanket "is this true"; Tversky was scared when he flew into the United States, "I've never landed."; Amos discovered that similarity is not reciprocal, like distance (e.g., toy boats are like real boats, but real boats are not like toy boats); changing the context can make two things seem more similar, like two randos in the US are just randos, but take them to Japan and they're both American; "Life is a book. The fact that is was a short book doesn't mean it wasn't a good book. It was a very good book."

title: The Tribe
author: Sebastian Junger
description: War and strife bring people closer
memories: People feel happiest when they are needed, like for survival purposes -- following natural disasters and in war; modern society is super isolating, and even nice things like giving early boarding to troops isolates them more; in Israel, where everyone serves in the military, there isn't as big of a stigma; men are more individually courageous, women more communally courageous/protective/empathetic, different sexes will assume both gender roles depending on what's needed

title: The Stand
author: Stephen King
description: M-O-O-N, that spells "wtf just happened"
memories: Classic Stephen King, really fucked up, little bit of mysticism, lots of good storylines; world was super expansive, basically covered the whole USA; 99% of people die from superflu ("Captain Trips") and the resulting survivors group by good and evil; Frannie is preggers; Harold is a big fat nerd who becomes a murderer; Stu is a simple man who becomes a leader; Larry is a pop-star druggie who fights with being only self-interested but dies sacrificially; the dark man is the bad guy; Mother Abigail is 108 years old and the good one; Nick is deaf, mute, and half-blind, and ridiculously smart; Nadine is the dark man's bride; Trashcan Man is the world's best pyro who eventually kills the bad guy's fort in Vegas with a nuke; Tom Collins is a slow person who thinks every world is spelled M-O-O-N

title: Principles
author: Ray Dalio
description: Radical transparency -> success
memories: Basically, he got the feedback that his employees hated how they made him feel, and instead of change himself he started an organization where they have "radical transparency" and don't care about each other's feelings; this turned out to be a very good idea and actually something I'd want to draw from; the most basic ideas are that the organization and all markets are machines where you can map inputs and make predictable bets on things; they are very high-tech behind the scenes; they have an "idea meritocracy" that's enabled by that transparency and "believability-weighted" decision making, which is basically whoever has had multiple successes at that thing gets a bigger weight than others; they have baseball cards for all employees and rely heavily on psychographic stuff like MBTI to see who would be good in what roles; maturity is rejecting good alternatives for better ones (commit!); you can have anything, but not everything, you want; makes some sketchy distinctions between young people being able to argue with anyone about the beenfit of the idea meritocracy, there are a few golden geese that he won't let you question; he loves the five step process which is basically to treat problems like coal in a steam engine and burn them to get better; double-do not double-check; think about leverage as a manager, how many hours of your time leads to how many hours of work

title: The Effective Executive
author: Peter Drucker
description: Executives should be good at their jobs
memories: Lots of words about only a few ideas: focus on strengths, not lack of weakness, think about where you're spending time, make good decisions; there are lots of different kinds of good leaders but they all have to listen well; organizations are relatively recent in history (like 1900, really); he suggests long meetings, which I'm not totally into but maybe should experiment with, particularly with people development; feed opportunities and starve problems (when allocating people)

title: Fear: Trump in the White House
author: Bob Woodward
description: Trump is too disorganized to understand rationally
memories: Mostly confirmed everything I thought about Trump: he's an idiot, he's senile, he doesn't work hard, he is surrounded by sycophants, liars, and criminals; interesting part was how he did have a few moderating influences (like generals), but fought against them in "Groundhog Day" style where they had the same arguments (e.g., free trade) over and over again; lots of people trying to make themselves look good through this book, like his lawyer Dowd; amazing how much Trump literally only cares about making a profit for America, doesn't care about national security or international stability except as it relates to the wealth of the US, saying "we have to mine their minerals" in afghanistan, what a dummy; this book was horribly written and organized, made no sense, but then again neither does Trump... left way too many storylines unclosed, though, and missed some big events slash barely talked about others

title: Who Can You Trust
author: Rachel Botsman
description: Interpersonal -> institutional -> distributed trust
memories: Trust is key, it's how Alibaba was built; gotta punish transgressors; trust goes from idea, to platform, to individual; "strangely familiar" is the goal of a new tech, like Jobs' skeuomorphism, making things in digital world look like the outside world; TOR was originally made by the Navy to protect US government communications; China is actually making the Black Mirror rating system

title: The Last Days of Night
author: Graham Moore
description: Tesla thought, Edison reveled, Westinghouse developed
memories: Great historical fiction account of the AC/DC debate, starts with the frying of the worker on the electrical lines, goes through the journey of the young lawyer Paul Cravath as he defends Westinghouse against Edison; Edison was a total asshole and Harold Brown did his bidding to kill animals with AC; Tesla's lab burned down; JP Morgan's guest appearance and help in the hostile takeover to get Edison off the throne of GE; Paul's eventual wife Agnes is a strong character, girl who lied about her past to make it big as a singer; electric chair stuff that totally backfired

title: The Lessons of History
author: Will and Ariel Durant
description: History repeats, but not simply
memories: Review of huge themes across thousands of years of history; defends history as what separates us from past generations, which is super true and something I've been thinking a lot about recently (e.g., "business is applied history"); biology and competiton/selection; some pretty weird, racist stuff about separating races in there; religion as source of morality, but not really truth because it's been so constant and human-influenced; peace is unstable without total equality or total dominance; overthrowing oppressors then you do what they did, shows not a lot of moral difference, just conditions; democracy rocks, but naturally trades off with socialism through cycles; lots of socialism because this was pre-cold war; talked about how industrialism really changed things, new age of history; history as lived is different than recorded, because nobody wants to talk about happy marriages; democracy is tough because it requires widespread intelligence and people are generally not that intelligent

title: The Race Underground
author: Doug Most
description: The sorta boring story of the world's second subway
memories: London was first! Why was this the story to tell? I guess the author is a Boston guy; Boston was first and much quieter in construction than NYC, who brought out a Tiffany shovel; NYC was late due to political struggles; lots of accidents while making the tunnels but then people seemed okay, all the irrational stuff just took time to pass; story of the guy walking out from the shield to show safety then getting hit in the head and dying; rest of notes in the research doc

title: Becoming
author: Michelle Obama
description: Michelle let us borrow her glorious husband
memories: Very heartfelt, awesome to hear of Obama in the early days, he smoked a lot, he had an ease and slow pace about him (I could use that), and deliberately built up a worldview by himself that he then found ways to make change in the world; things happened SO fast between state senate, senate, presidency; cute anecdote of not being able t find middle C without the crack in her piano at home; Michelle experienced white flight; being President/First Lady is so ridiculously emotionally hard; the double-standard of Republicans is personal to her, as it should be; makes me so mad ot hear about how Trump endangered her girls; "what are you thinking about?" "oh, income inequality"

title: Devil in the White City
author: Erik Larson
description: Architecture was more dramatic than murder, somehow
memories: Story of how Chicago won the 1893 World Fair exposition, build a huge city in three years with the collaboration of all of America's biggest architects/landscapers… and how a murderer built his murder hotel right down the street;
