started off and I couldn't care about the world's fair, but somehow it became way more intriguing than the murders in the end; the murder story is basically that a guy with blue eyes renamed himself HH Holmes, and was hella persuasive/personable/touchy/attractive, and exploited that power to kill a bunch of people, ultimately three children -- he seemed to get a lot of satisfaction from talking himself out of tough situations, like when he led the three children and their mother around the same town at the same time and kept them unknown to each other, or when he pulled a Donald Trump and basically refused to pay all of his contracted workers as he built a huge department store and a hotel with a bunch of gas rooms and a torture chamber in the basement, murders included one with acid on the floor of a small cell, footprint from the woman trying to escape found on the inside door melted into the steel; the fair itself was a masterpiece, unbelievable that it happened at all, Daniel Burnam pulled together all of NYC and Chicago's best architects to build huge strutures with exhibits from all over the world, including like captured native tribes and other wild stuff, like the cross section of a cruise liner, also the first ever Ferris Wheel that was about 300 feet tall, lots of setbacks like strikes and fires, and it wasn't complete until well into the running of the fair, but it was still amazing, story of the Ferris wheel guy who freaked out until a woman took off her skirt and put it on his head, Omstead as an epic landscape designer who build coves and such into the landscape for the event; they turned down the man Eiffel building a taller tower in America; Elias Disney, Walt's dad, was a worker at the fair; pledge of allegiance made to honor the first day of the fair; fair started Frank Lloyd Wright's career too, inspired by Olmstead; generally, the fair is known as inspiring the nation to view cities as beautiful not just functional, cost equivalent of $500 billion to run

title: Dreams from My Father
author: Barack Obama
description: People simply want to belong to a larger order
memories: Genuinely interesting and vulnerable, definitely far too "unpresidential" by normal decorum standards, he admits to smoking weed and buying coke, says n*gger a lot, generally seems to shoot super straight, but that's so cool to see; connected with his development in this book, from the early days of microaggressions (yikes) to people "just joking" to the angtsy teenage and college years where he got radical but it was just a cover for insecurity and fear; he only saw his dad once, I feel like I haven't really talked to my dad about much "real" stuff; started with a really high standard of his dad as a saint, but then that image fell from glory when he learned about his dad's political troubles (basically exiled by Kenyatta, couldn't find any job, once Kenyatta died he got back to some semblance of power), but then he had to reconstruct the image of his father and grandfather by going to Africa and seeing the complexity that exists there too, like tourism as a relic of colonialism (white people own stores, even if black people control the government), found religion and some sense of inner peace through Rev. Wright's sermons, cool to hear what community organizing is all about, from the failures (riot at the town hall) to the successes (asbestos) and everything in between; question about whether he was running away from life by going to law school... that hit home; he would read a lot into the night, bascically trying to build his own worldview for a long time; met the interesting people on his safari in Kenya, incl a warrior tribe guy and a white guy who had deep ties to Kenya but felt he didn't belong; talks about fear of black kids because they have nothing to fear/care about

title: The Audacity of Hope
author: Barack Obama
description: Future President tries on his President hat
memories: Not remotely as interesting as his first book, but still good for a politician, very middle-road-y, especially on same-sex marriage; nobody listens to arguments in the senate; post-Reagan everything got more ideological and sound-bite-y; CEO pay is a huge problem; when he started in the Senate, tried to enjoy being a bacherlor-ish, but no use, he was "fully domesticated, soft and helpless"; TV is very expensive, so politics is a lot about raising money, because TV is the only way to reach constituents (other than Twitter now...)

title: The Wizard of Menlo Park
author: Randall Stross
description: Edison was anti-semitic, deaf, and a jerk
memories: Seems like his inventions were less inspiring than his ability to manipulate the press into believing he was the second coming; half-deaf, ignored all small talk but seemed mostly okay otherwise, still insisted on controlling the musical selection for which acts would be recorded for his phonograph; totally underestimated the consumer potential of the phonograph (thought it should be for business dictation, not music); can't see how a phone works so you default to the inventor; he was the world's first celebrity, people showed up at Menlo Park expecting to be shown around his lab, they had to work from 8PM to 8AM; he started as a telegraph operator, traveling the country via train and generally being a nerdy bum; arc light and gas both kinda sucked, but lightbulbs were WAY better indoors, much less harsh and no chemicals; Edison overpromised a LOT at the beginning of the lightbulb, stock fell from $3500 to $400; battle between gas, arc light, and AC/DC all got ugly (around 1882), when he actually finished (after announcing in 1878); people surprisingly didn't care about safety (?); used to bite the piano to hear it better, lol; Insull was a great businessman and also not a racist; Insull had bad Edison stories but held onto them to not sully his reputation

title: AC/DC
author: Tom McNichol
description: AC/DC was all about Edison for this author
memories: Didn't teach me very much, the sections I actually knew things it was clear he left a bunch of stuff out; Franklin came up with a lot of the early language around electricity in only three-ish years of research (positive, negative); Tesla was fairly important to the AC movement becuase of his motor; Edison tried to have "exact imitation" of gas with electricity; Edsion built the whole system around lights, from switches to sockets to the station; Brown was a bad person, claimed a LOT of expertise he didn't have; more DC went into homes than AC (110 vs. 50 volts); AC at Telluride was a great proof of the use case; AC won the world fair in 1893, huge win and proved to everyone (but Edison) that the battle was over;

author: W. Joseph Campbell
description: Eventful year, maybe, sweeping impact less credible
memories: Claims the Internet got going in '95 because of Netscape's IPO, other events were OJ trial, Monica Lewinsky affair begins, Bosnia peace negotiation, and OKC bombing; judgments were way too sweeping in his interpretation of events though; OKC was from a digruntled white guy, muslim extremists were blamed for days and one guy was deported from London to the US, led to stuff like Penn Ave being closed in DC; Bosnia re-started American Exceptionalism after the Vietnam war, said that we rocked, maybe justified more aggressive action after that; Lewinsky stuff was just gross, Bill was a bad man and it was super taking advantage, lots of "crush" language... eew; OJ was probably guilty but could afford ridiculous lawyers, the glove thing was a horrible idea by the prosecution, he was later convicted in civil court for the same charges, went to prison later and quickly faded from public view, black vs. white reactions maybe overstated but probably not, author cites popularity of Colin Powell as evidence that the country wasn't racist...; original wiki maker didn't know how to commercialize it; NSF owned the Internet's maintenanceuntil 1995

title: Fooled by Randomness
author: Nassim Nicholas Taleb
description: Magnitude, not frequency, of success is what matters
memories: Most people choose what wins a lot, rather than what wins big and avoids losing big (e.g., "the market is going up with 70% probability, but I'm short because drops will be huge"); lots of trader stories of people who evaporated 20 years of winnings in a few minutes by huge negative events, like russian roulette; count the number of monkeys on typewriters, if there are a lot there are bound to be some huge successes just because; you observe almost only variability in the short time frame, so maybe take reports less often; Monte Carlo is his tool of choice to model alternative histories, avoid thinking this is the only one that exists or ever could exist; "summing under histories" is to take account of all possibilties; have stop losses in place in life, aka contingency plans for when everything goes to shit; `unless you have confidence in the ruler's reliability, if you use a ruler to measure a table you may also be using the table to measure the ruler (Wittgenstein's Ruler")'; no way to beat the mental models bad stuff, so just know you're dumb to win still

title: Creative Selection
author: Ken Kocienda
description: Apple was all about demos and deciding for customers
memories: Demo demo demo; Apple had small software teams working on huge projects (10 for Safari, 25 for iPhone); Jobs tested if people were capable of adding by just asking their opinions; tech and liberal arts combined (aka design thinking without the jargon); someone had to clean all the profanity out of the Netscape code before they released it as Mozilla; Steve Jobs practiced his speeches a lot; tech people sometimes forget that people matter; "design is how it works"; he shits on Google's AB testing saying it doesn't let you get 2x - 10x better with a feature; no separation of R&D and actual designers/engineers; design team called themselves Human Interface; Jobs' involvement seemed absolutely necessary because he was the sole authority; decide what to do at the end of a demo meeting and confirm before leaving; Jobs picked one big goal, e.g., speed for Safari and they actually worked towards it in everything (with page load test); Ken worked on Safari then WebKit then iPhone keyboard; keyboard design was far from obvious, nobody had done that before on a glass screen; they "lived on" their software

title: How The Internet Happened
author: Brian McCullough
description: Market crashed, technology didn't
memories: Netscape was the first big winner, formed by Andreessen and some CEO guy from the team that build Mosaic at Illinois, the first real browser for the Web; beat out the info superhighway which was a corporate-favored option among Disney and such; Mozilla means the monster that eats Mosaic; lots of the cultural things in Silicon Valley come from them, like long hours and ping pong and such, because they were all college grads; people liked the info superhighway, which would be delivered by TV, because it had high bandwidth; AOL was literally half sexy chat in the beginning; AOL cd thing worked sooo well, 10% response rate; first ever Amazon order was a Hofstadter book; people would email Amazon their credit card rather than put it in a form; Amazon was one of the first good web companies in 1994; turning point for Amazon was a WashPo article; eBay started the whole rating thing, getting us to trust strangers; dot-com boom was a result of 82-00 stocks basically never going down; Internet added $300 billion and 37% of all new jobs when it crashed; dot-coms copied successful formulas in the shit storm, from being first to a market to IPO early to who cares about profit; Microsoft was tied down after 2000 by the monopoly trial, which let a lot of companies form without getting squished; NASDAQ lost 80% of value, 1.7 trillion lost; Marc Cuban won in the crash by having an "options collar" after his Broadcast.com sold to Yahoo; Google was made post-crash and actually offered to sell their tech to the leading search engines at the time... with the condition that it had to be their whole search algorithm; Jobs thought music was the key to the digital hub; Blockbuster got rekt by Netflix because they had lots of annoying late fees and didn't have movies in stock when they were popular -> "managed dissatisfaction"; Reed Hastings basically knew streaming and them being a content creator was coming in 2002; Google's IPO was insane, showed they had 1/2 a B in FCF and 60% operating margin; Google wanted to raise "e" of money, "an irrational number"; Google ads basically started blogging by buying Bloggerwhich is ironic; sharing, tagging, and upvoting all came after the crash; usage of the Internet never dipped in the crash; Sean Parker had a car, which helped him get into FB lol; users hated FB news feed, but coudn't stop using it; Apple got a share of AT&T plans revenue for the iPhone; "we all know this is the one we want, so let's make it work." -jobs

title: Tiger Woods
author: Jeff Benefict and Armen Keteyian
description: Tiger was the best golfer ever, bad at everything else
memories: His dad got obsessed with golf, practiced for hours in the garage when Tiger was a baby, so he internalized it and was great from his first swing; on TV at age 2, had a coach by age 5; Dad used prisoner of war tactics to break him down/toughen him up mentally; assassin mentality; enter every tournament to win; dad cheated on his wife (tiger's mom), and Tiger got addicted to sex because he was incredibly self-centered (wouldn't return human courtesy and internalized that he was "the chosen one," per his parents, and "the black hope" for golf; eventually destroyed his body weightlifting and having a mid-life crisis about joining the Navy SEALs after his dad died; super callous and unfeeling, didn't even text Lindsey Vonn after she got a nearly career-ending injury after they broke up; people protected his image early, dating all the way back to not reporting the truth of his dad's crazy comments; dad was really opportunistic and loved his son's fame and how it trickled over to him; at 11, won all 33 golf tourneys he entered; Tiger was a Sigma Chi; noticed a difference in weight of 2 grams in a driver set; his mom never got a gravestone for his dad after he died because of all the cheating and verbal abuse; won his last major in 2008 with a fractured leg and a torn ACL

title: Bad Blood
author: John Carreyrou
description: Elizabeth Holmes should be in jail
memories: Just an incredible about of fraud from a person who desperately wanted to be Steve Jobs but dreamed too big and built too much hype before she could deliver; insisted on impossible to accomplish product goals like doing tons of tests with a simple fingerprick; she was incredibly shielded by her high-powered lawyers, amazing what money can buy, including intimidation and tracking people; Sunny seems like the devil; Holmes had a weirdly deep affected voice, paired with Jobsian and/or serial killer staredowns and attention; Holmes believed there was a 9/11 conspiracy because "Steve" let a movie about it on iTunes; board full of incredibly impressive military people who knew nothing about medicine; built up a culture of fear by firing everyone and one of sycophancy where those who promised impossible things were rewarded and all naysayers, even low key ones, had to go

title: Angel
author: Jason Calacanis
description: Angel investing is high upside, all about hustle
memories: Makes me want to be an angel, I just don't have nearly enough money yet to be an accredited investor -- goal: make $300K in 2020 so I can be; all about being nice to founders but saying no, getting to the important stuff in interviews (what are you building; why are you building it; why now?; what's your unfair advantage?) to get at founder-business match, founder commitment, founder success potential in life, and winning upside (only invest in crazy shit); advises starting with syndicates and always getting pro rata; ask what people's parents do;

title: Who is Michael Ovitz?
author: Michael Ovitz
description: Appearing all-knowing is a choice; Ovitz made it
memories: Man did anything to build his company, mostly just worked harder than everyone else in a sleepy industry; package deals with all artists and directors and such as CAA clients; his "all-knowing" aura that he carefully maintained; lying when necessary; having all the agents represent all the clients, 10 > 1; the pitch to Coke with the polar bear vs. his advertising opponents who literally stopped halfway through the presentation because they knew they lost; Sony deal and Japan fear similar to China fear; obsessed with art and Lichtenstein; eventually up to M&A and advertising; got book deals before the books were even published; made Rain Man happen; Scorsese and Spielberg to swap films so Shindler's list; "no conflicts, no interest"; wanted his employees to be a little anxious but also safe; power is only power until you exercise it; get everyone talking about themselves; "no pressure" -> constant follow-up; eventually failed as Disney #2 but could have been AMAZING, deals denied like buying Yahoo stake by Eisner; huge falling out with his partners who resented his showmanship and public success; Bill Murray at a funeral: "there are so many people here I'd rather be eulogizing"

title: High Output Management
author: Andy Grove
description: Old-school management by an old-school manager
memories: Best concepts: task-relevant maturity (for interviewing, hiring, promoting, and compensating); fix problems at lowest-value stage; pair metrics with counter-metrics (e.g., quantity and quality); leverage = action of manager -> output of org; higher task maturity -> less hands-on; management by objectives (and key results; KRs are tangible benchmarks on the way to the goal); mission-oriented (independent) vs. functional (centralized/shared) org structures, he advocates both and dual reporting for one employee to have two bosses, effectively; performance, not potential

title: Winners Take All
author: Anand Giridharadas
description: Everything that's not taxes or government sucks
memories: Private philanthropy and accumulating wealth now to make an impact later is dumb and premised on the idea that you're smarter than people who could just have more of your money through higher wages or taxation benefits; super critical of the Davos class people who fly on private jets to talk global poverty; MarketWorld prefers win-win stuff that doesn't challenge people in power (e.g., make a change AND money), but lots of the world is win-lose and distributive, and the top has taken all of the growth; consulting thinking is bad and too simplistic, 80/20 ignores 80% of the stuff, no long-term thinking, just short-termism and metrics; thought leaders who give pithy advice and tell people what they want to hear are bad; insistence on "shareholder value" hurts everything else

title: The Truth Machine
author: Michael Casey and Paul Vigna
description: Blockchain can do everything (but not yet)
memories: Companies spend $75B on security and suffer $400B of losses; blockchain pushes control (and responsibility, and challenges) to the network edge; check out WannaCry ransom attack; forks because no leader and real money on table w/ individual investment; once big-name VCs in, crypto up; Snowden as a break in time;

title: The Messy Middle
author: Scott Belsky
description: Entrepreneurship is less glamorous than many admit
memories: Be an energy giver, not taker -- "free radical"; if you want to win, sometimes you have to take the difficult path forward in development; credit is less about rewards and more about getting the right people on the job the next time; you are the chief internal marketer and have to report back on how everything is going, when people do good stuff, etc; don't smooth over roughness in promoting things, roughness gives people a chance to grasp on; make your boss make decisions -- great leaders kill ambiguity; learn how to "friendly front stab" rather than backstab; know what you're okay being bad at, maybe by finding industry values you don't optimize for; onboarding = "why you're there, what you can accomplish, what to do next"; in the first 30 seconds, people are lazy-vain-selfish; novelty comes before utility; interest drivers to product don't need to be highly used; clickbait is based on "information gap," you open up a question people need the answer to; invest when people are riding an existing trend and making it happen a better way; best companies are profitable from their science but known for their art

title: Blitzscaling
author: Reid Hoffman
description: The Samwer brothers were villains, but also right
memories: Risk is being safe, not growing efficiently; blitzscaling is prioritizing speed over efficiency in the face of uncertainty; Paypal grew at 5% daily, lost money on every transaction; Airbnb hacked their demand side with a Craigslist integration, one click to post there too; stop blitzscaling when you have a declining growth rate, worsening unit economics, decreasing per-employee productivity, increasing mgmt overhead; promote from inside for core functions, outside for rando stuff; Steve Jobs had management offsite, top 10 priorities, at end of day crossed off all but top 3; point of launching early is to learn early; planning = best case scenario; corporate managers plow money into failing projects so they don't fail, entrepreneurs pivot to something that will work better; let some fires burn

title: Steve Jobs
author: Walter Isaacson
description: Jobs was mercurial, self-aware, and taken far too early
memories: Jobs and Woz were a funny, prank-y duo, Woz was really immature and Jobs very mature, so they met in the middle; Jobs worked at Atari and didn't shower so he was super stinky, left to go to India to find a yogi; India taught Jobs the power of intuition; some guy named Ron Wayne sold 10% of Apple for $800... yikes, don't be him and be so risk-averse; Jobs cried a lot; "impute" = people judge a book by its cover, Apple box-opening experience; Xerox PARC invented the GUI and Jobs realized how valuable it was; goal for Jobs was always greatness in product and building a company that lasted (later); Gates and Jobs were both contemporaries, Gates more anti-conflict and safer and a better business person (debate was open vs. closed, Jobs preferred closed control but only he could execute it); Sculley's wife confronted Jobs when Jobs was trying to get Sculley fired, said "look at my eyes" then "oh god no" when he started staring, soullessly; "it's not late, it's five years ahead of its time"; Jobs owned 80% of Pixar when they IPO'd; crazy diets all the time like fruit and would constantly send dishes and drinks back ad nauseum; Jobs loved moderation to enjoy decadence, knew things lead to their opposites; couldn't buy furniture, "what's the purpose of a sofa"; Katzenberg tried to make Woody an asshole in Toy Story; Gil Amelio was Apple CEO for a while but sucked, quote about "it's my job to steer the boat" "but what about the hole?"; Jobs hated powerpoint, would constantly cut products and simplify the corporate strategy; design is the opposite of veneer, it's really understanding deeply the purpose of something so you can simplify it to its essence and remove all frills; no major design reviews for their products, just constant tinkering and models; when Jobs came back, started to design the whole business (e.g., ops improvements to decrease working capital), called one supplier "fucking dickless assholes" who then made jackets with "Team FDA" on the back when they finally delivered; Sony never made the iPod because their divisions were at odds; if you don't love something, can't outwork people; his letter to Laurene on his twentieth anniversary is beautiful (pg. 530); hero/shithead dichotomy; he was mean but very sensitive so he could destroy you by exploiting your insecurities; could have operated on his cancer much earlier but never did because he didn't want to be opened up; don't let sales guys run the company, no sex in the products after that; he just wanted to "add a little something to the flow"; last words were "oh wow, oh wow"

title: The Defining Decade
author: Dr. Meg Jay
description: Leaving 20s with career and relationship on lock is sick
memories: Lots of 20-year-olds struggle with under- or unemployment; fertility is real, also lifestyle things like can you see your grandkids; brains are still plastic, take advantage; don't be afraid of unthought knowns, declare your dreams fully; cohabitation can hurt divorce/marriage prospects; growth mindset is like my GMAT learning, means people can improve and aren't just smart or not

title: The Blockchain and the New Architecture of Trust
author: Kevin Werbach
description: What's the opposite of a hot take on blockchain?
memories: Skimmed, very little original thinking that was interesting; argument was that law and blockchain should work together and learn from each other, lots of explainer, etc.; nakamoto means "of central origin" lols; the Imogen Heap song on Ethereum generated $133 in sales; P2P as a case study of what blockchain might become if legacy stuff figures its shit out, legal onslaught killed it while another better UX/UI won

title: A Game of Thrones
author: George R.R. Martin
description: Nobody is safe; love and honor are weaknesses
memories: What an insanely big and awesome world; Jon and Sam are bros; Dany goes from abused little sister to mother of dragons; Ned goes from executing a traitor to being executed; Cersei and Jaime are banging; all the Stark kids are sooooo young, thank god Arya got trained by Syrio Forel; Mountain and the Hound are cool; young Sansa is super annoying; Tyrion is smart AF; the lore of everything is so seamless and I want mooorrre

title: A Clash of Kings
author: George R.R. Martin
description: Commoners don't really care about kings
memories: Everything through the Battle of Blackwater Bay -- all of the Tyrion scheming back at King's landing, the Renly assassination by Stannis/Melisandre, Sansa being tortured by Joffrey and protected by the Hound, faked deaths of Bran/Rickon by Theon (Bran then learns his warg skills from the Reeds; lots of Jon and eventually gets captured/kills Quorin Halfhand; lots of rumors and misinformed characters; Jamie captured

title: The Teachings of Don Juan: A Yaqui Way of Knowledge
author: Carlos Castaneda
description: Non-ordinary reality can still be logical
memories: Grad student goes into Mexican Sonoran desert and meets a guy who has mastered three kinds of hallucinogenic drugs, has a mini-conversion where he starts believing in the rationality of the stuff, but has a legitimately super spooky interaction that scares him off; his master don Juan was acting really possessed, Carlos had to fight him off using traditional techniques like slapping his leg and throwing a rock; the drugs give the sensation of communicating with another plane of reality with "allies" and non-physical beings that can give knowledge, help you have visions, etc.; in the end, don Juan's "possession" convinced Carlos that there was little separating the alternate and real realities in terms of verifiability of actions or the truth of perception

title: A Storm of Swords
author: George R.R. Martin
description: Weddings are dangerous
memories: Stannis spends most of his time being broody on Dragonstone, ignoring his wife for Melisandre, and sacrificing people to fire, before coming in clutch in driving back the wildlings and taking over the north to save Jon, Sam, and Gilly; Arya starts off with Gendry and Hot Pie but eventually loses them, teams up with the Hound, and crosses a bunch of names off her list (the Tickler, the Hound (because he was nice to Sansa), Joff); Joffrey dies, woohoo; we find out Lysa poured Tears into Jon Arryn's drink at the behest of Littlefinger, Littlefinger smooches on a recently-escaped Sansa and pushes Lysa out the moon door; red wedding, tragic, but Robb's wife actually wasn't there; sewed his head to the body of Grey Wind and threw Catlin's body in the river... but now she's BACK, in the epilogue, as Zombie Catlin; Joffrey goes from healing from his wounds to back in action to getting sideswiped by Cersei, learns that his original wife was NOT a whore, that was a lie from Tywin, and he gets accused of killing Joff, Red Viper dies vs. Mountain, day before he's sentenced to death, saved by Varys but goes up to his dad's chambers and kills Shai and his dad first; Dany is finding that it's harder to rule than to conquer, learns of Barristan the Bold's true identity, befriends Daario, exiles Jorah Mormont for spying on her; Jon get captured by wildlings, falls in love with Ygritte, runs away, goes BACK to their camp, survives through Stannis's assault, gets the King beyond the wall's baby, then goes back and gets elected Lord Commander of the Night's Watch.

title: Who
author: Geoff Smart & Randy Street
description: Even a little structure in hiring goes a long way
memories: A players have a 90% chance of being top 10%; ask everyone "who are the most talented people you know that I should hire"; TORC = threat of reference check; have "deputies" who you give $5k if they refer someone to you; screens = goals, strengths, weakness/disinterest, who were last bosses and how will they rate you 1-10; "who" = "top rating" = what were you hired to do, good, bad, who did you work with/what will they say/how to rate your team when you joined, why did you leave; interrupting is okay; gotta align skill, will, and scorecard; make scorecards that persist into jobs with job-specific tasks and OKRs; sell with "fit, family, freedom, fortune, fun"; cheetah CEOs are best (move fast, aggressive, persistent, proactive, work ethic, high standards)

title: A Feast for Crows
author: George R.R. Martin
description: The Stark kids stay alive... and Mama is undead
memories: Lots of differences between show and book in this one -- Mama Stark is back as someone undead and pissed off at the Lannisters (and she ends the book forcing Brienne to go after Jaime); Cersei is now imprisoned by the High Sparrow after trying to wreck Margery but failing; Cersei generally being incompetent; Sam learning a lot from Aemon Targaryan then heading to Old Town (where people don't believe in dragons and such); Sansa is now "Alayne" and living with Littlefinger at the Eyrie; Arya is trying to learn awesome assassin skills from Braavos and the many-faced God people; lots of stuff happening in Dorne that nobody cares about really; iron islands now taken over by Euron, who is CRAZY and wants to take over Westeros basically; The Hound presumed dead, killed by Brienne; apparently the onion knight is dead too

title: What You Do is Who You Are
author: Ben Horowitz
description: Culture is about your actions
memories: I liked that he chose non-white people and counterintuitive examples but his arguments weren't actually all that compelling; it was basically like "you have to act according to your principles" but he admits right away that his first example (Toussaint Louverture) didn't do that because he set a "shocking rule" that nobody could cheat on their wives but he did exactly that; other rules I remember include applying your internal rules to the whole world to make sure you're fair and consistent, using culture stuff that already exists; communication is inversely proportional to trust; be yourself (the sales dude who was super counter-cultural but who was needed... said who makes the decisions and Ben said "hey motherfucker, I'm CEO"); basically the CEO has to know when to break the rules; always emphasize the why behind cultural values; be yourself even if it means some people don't like you; think about subcultures and what is best for the whole company vs. individual divisions; at HP there were 15,000 fewer chairs than employees, aka people just weren't coming to work; encourage employees to bring you bad news (have to tell me one thing on fire to come to the exec meeting)

title: A Dance of Dragons
author: George R.R. Martin
description: Hopefully not the end of an expansive world
memories: The Dorne plot actually got interesting! Prince of Dorne comes searching for Dany (much like fAegon, a dude who thinks he's Aegon?), but eventually gets burnt by dragons, turns out he's not actually Targaryen; Dany and Hizdahr is an annoying plot, didn't really care about the city, Dany sacrificing her rule for her people but it's so clear her rule doesn't mean much, poor dragons are chhained; Arya becoming faceless was so dope, had to learn how to kill people (guy who tasted the money -> poisoned coin); Jon was a legit great leader but was leading a racist group of people, was a small insurrection that ultimately killed him for teaming up with wildlings and allowing giants to help and such; Cersei getting owned by the High Septon but not blowing up the Sept yet, she did kill Kevan and Pycelle though (or was that just Varys?); Tyrion's plot is cool, being enxlaved with the other dwarf and now a second son; lots of inconsistencies and places where it seemed like George RR Martin copied and pasted text?

title: The Master and His Emissary
author: Iain McGilchrist
description: Right brain rules!
memories: Painful in the middle, but damn look how much I learned!!! The left brain: divides things into parts, has no context, "re-presents" stuff by building up a model of how things work, is focused on "what," is literal and sequential and linear, holds language RULES (not all language),  is super selfish, builds up the rational and either/or dichotomies that come with it, unrealistically confident; the right brain is focused on "how," is connected to reality, is expansive (sees peripheral vision, can recognize both sides of the body), understands metaphor, treats art nicely, empathizes, enjoys and finds wisdom in contradictions. And now, basically all of society is taken over by the left brain, but we probably Gucci because this has happened before and then Romanticism happens; bisect a line and it'll be left of center because it's the right brain that's judging (and that controls the left eye); we see things whole first, then separate out and categorize, then bring back to earth (right -> left -> right); strong universal tendency to cradle infants to the left, so they're by the more expressive left side of the face and your left eye can see them (right brain); depression happens with left brain deficiency (because left can't say no to right, and depressed people are more realistic; right brain deficiency = schizophrenia, left brain categorizing to the point that the world is foreign and you don't understand motives so everything seems maniacal and threatening; music came before language!; language and grasp are forms of the same thing -- the desire to control the world, which started in the left brain (get a grasp on something, put your finger on it, etc.); objectivity of the left brain isn't really no stance towards observation, it's a detatched view towards observation -- not the same, right brain is almost more unbiased because it sees things individually; "belief" isn't a weaker form of "fact," it's a right brain way of saying "I will act as if X is true"; gesture is more meaningful than speech, more true to intended meaning when there's a conflict; Pascal: the ultimate achievement of reason is to recognize that there are an ininity of things which surpass it"; newness = seeing the same thing in a new way, novelty = manufacturing different absurd things

title: Pet Sematary
author: Stephen King
description: Sometimes dead is better
memories: Plot: family moves to new town bordering indian lands; elderly neighbor, takes them to pet "sematary"; dad is doctor and sees a horrific crash in ER, dead patient tells him not to go beyond the sematary; trauma with wife, her sister died as a child and her parents are mean to husband; cat gets hit by a truck, old man takes dad up to the pet sematary and beyond, buries it in ancient burial ground, cat comes back to life but is super weird; son gets hit by a car, dad brings him back to life after sending family away, son comes back a murderous monster, kills old man and mom (eats her?!) before dad kills it then bring mom back to life

title: The Space Barons
author: Christian Davenport
description: Space is hard
memories: Musk = hare, Bezos = tortoise, Branson = playboy bunny, Gedmark = intern; accidents with Spaceship Two and the static burn test failure, but Musk and Bezos perservered; lots on the monopoly of the legacy dudes and how their programs are terrible (cost plus model), but now these new startups are crushing it; Obama stopping the "constellation" program helped SpaceX and Blue compete; huge availability bias with deaths, space is actually fairly safe compared to what you'd think (3% of astronauts have died)

title: Can't Hurt Me
author: David Goggins
description: Pain only stops the weak
memories: guy started as a chunker, lost > 100 pounds in 3 months to prep for SEAL training, took three tries because he got injured twice but eventually did it, then Army Rangers, then ultramarathons; basically he thinks anything is possible even though he severely hurt himself many times while living that philosophy; he's genuinely inspiring though given his super rough background (abusive dad), and his strategies are cool (i.e., cookie jar of good stories about yourself; list of your weaknesses); eventually he learned to stretch

title: A Heartbreaking Work of Staggering Genius
author: Dave Eggers
description: Novels are weird, man
memories: Parents both die months from each other from different and unexpected cancers; he (22) raises his brother (8), is very angry and unforgiving, but very smart and self-reflective in the novel, he writes a lot about whether he's exploiting these characters (he is) but he makes cool observations about "what do I owe you, reader" and such. Mostly he choses very sad stories of people he knows who died or almost died, or had their parents die/divorce. Loved the part of the middle of the book (super long chapter) where he is "being interviewed for the Real World show" but is just talking dialectically with the book itself;

title: The Making of a Manager
author: Julie Zhou
description: Experience is the only way to learn management
memories: So many of her rules are things that are true but only useful if you have experience... it's like "sometimes you should X, other times Y," example being micromanagement vs. being too hands off; generally this book was good empathy-wise, so I know that even big fancy managers don't know what they're doing, but it didn't teach me all that much; underscored need for feedback and culture in team-building; you actually have to care or people will see through you, find unique strengths in people;

title: I Am Legend
author: Richard Matheson
description: Vampires are people too
memories: Way different than the movie, the protagonist is a horny alcoholic who is very unit and doesn't have that many cool gadgets; he's lcoked himself up in his house really well but doesn't have very much else figured out; eventually does start figuring out a cure, but then the "living" vampires, as opposed to the dead ones, reveal themselves to be a super advanced society that has to feed on blood but is otherwise smart, and they kill him

title: Shortest Way Home
author: Pete Buttigieg
description: Values-led leadership is age independent
memories: Very inspirational dude, seems like he has a real set of beliefs and genuinely wants to lead (mostly) not out of ego; army experience was REAL, friends who died; McKinsey experience was funny, he basically said it sucked and he was happy to be done; finding Chasen on Hinge taught him why love rocked so much; Chasen had a great move of giving him a watch and "time" rather than proposing too early; Chasen's parents kicked him out of the house when they learned he was gay but came around; being a mayor sounds fun, lots of real issues and no BS politicking

title: American Kingpin
author: Nick Bilton
description: Ross wanted to change the world, and did
memories: Even if he became a villain by the end. Incredible that this happened all in a few years (2011-2013-ish), transformation from ideals to having to making really tough and high-stakes (deadly) decisions; shows the importance of having the right people around you, who can talk you down and be rational when you're facing new dilemmas; also the USG is still undefeated, loved the casino analogy (they have infinite funds and will fail before you do); normal-ish tech founder story, incredible how important the Gawker article was; honestly the thing proved some troubles with criminalizing drugs (i.e., pushing people like Ross underground --> he thinks he's a kingpin and can have people killed) and lets them draw the line for themselves; if the govt draws a more reasonable line, I think people would be more reasonable, not push further, because they don't think of themselves as criminals (once a criminal --> more serious criminal stuff; not "not a criminal but doing risky things" --> criminal and risky things)

title: Influence
author: Robert Cialdini
description: People are exploitable
memories: Re-read as part of CEO school with John Gedmark -- before CoS appointment! Reciprocation works even when your favor is small and what you're asking for is big; People look at their past actions to determine their present character (because they want to be consistent with those actions); The example of the car salesman scheduling all of his customer visits for the exact same time is both sketchy and brilliant; People enjoy the experience of acquiring a scarce commodity more than they value possessing it; The word "because" is super powerful; I should probably send out more thank you notes

title: How to Be a Bad Emperor
author: Suetonius
description: Roman Emperors were pretty evil
memories: Lots of killing rivals (and family); lots of people betrayed late in their lives by people they had been horrible to forever; general incompetence and lack of interest in actually leading, or belief in their exceptionalism that led to their downfall

title: Consider This
author: Chuck Palahniuk
description: Suspense is a science
memories: Plant a gun or establish a clock to drive timing; don't be afraid to explore your deep dark side, in fact that's how to stay interested yourself and honest with your readers; have short sentences without commas, no cliches, describe physical movement, think about horizontal story (this then this then this) vs vertical (growing in depth and importance); lots of importance to getting your story out in public, maybe just a scene at a time; write in modules, where every one has a pre-defined purpose; kill people in the second act; use up your best idea right away, and see where the story goes; be aware of myths to know how your audience thinks your story will end; get the small stuff right to build your factual credibility, and say deep dark real things to establish emotional credibility; allow the epiphany to happen in the reader's head before it's written on the page; don't do tennis match dialogue, escalate or avoid or don't answer things; the longer you can stay with something unresolved, the more beautifully it will resolve itself; avoid "is" and "has"

title: Predictably Irrational
author: Dan Ariely
description: Honestly, the title nails it
memories: Seemed like a "here is all of my research" book, the theme was surprisingly weak for being as provocative as it was; lots of really interesting individual experiments but would have been better with more of the world-building interpretation stuff he has at the very end of the book; all the classic experiments in here, big huge section in the middle on the difference between $0.01 and $0.00; arbitrary coherence means you accept anchors even when you know they are meaningles (e.g. SSN); herding

title: The Gatekeepers
author: Chris Whipple
description: Strong, not flashy, is best
memories: Fascinating that the modern model of the CoS started with HR Haldeman, Nixon's CoS who ended up going to jail by perjuring himself in Watergate stuff;  secret seems to be a strong personlaity who can say no to the Pres, super organized and understands how to et stuff done, and the ultimate "no" person whenever something is going to go wrong; the "staff model" is the key innovation, where everything has a process; JFK and LBJ tried not having CoS, didn't really work for JFK (and Robert Kennedy basically became his CoS at the end, unofficially), then LBJ was super effective... argument disproven?; loved the idea of all the CoS helping onboard the new guys past partisan differences; CoS balcony is bigger than the presidential one apparently; beter to not have a personal relationship, not one of the "true believers" necessarily, because then it's easier to say no; often the president has true believers as part of staff (e.g., Valerie Jarrett) and other folks who understand how to get stuff done in Washington in the current system; Jack Watson called being CoS a "javelin catcher"; Reagan's surgery after his assassination attempt was delayed due to his wife consulting an astrologist; Rahm Emanuel and Obama went on a daily 5pm walk called "the Wrap"

title: Secrets of Sand Hill Road
author: Scott Kupor
description: VC isn't all that secretive anymore
memories: Important to understand VC economics with pitching and negotiating term sheets; board normally NOT independent, per courts; be careful with governance rights; sooo much to do with avoiding lawsuits when things fail... ridiculous; board is in charge of looking out for common shareholders, not preferred

title: I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
author: Maya Angelou
description: Privilege is freedom of mind and body
memories: Incredibly traumatic description of being raped, and all the guilt that came afterwards; strong mother and grandmother, who she worshipped; relationship with her brother who was at various times playmate, protector, and partner-in-crime; lots of the "powhitefolks" aka poor white people still thought themselves superior; really deep nad honest telling of what childhood feels like; her grandpa had to hide in a bin of potatoes to escape being lynched; they didn't believe that their ancestors were stolen from Africa to be slaves

title: Rosshalde
author: Herman Hesse
description: Loneliness can be a beautiful emotion
memories: Super simple, beautiful story of a house divided by an artist who cared more about his craft than his family; Rosshalde is the house. where the painter, wife, and son live; painter and wife are estranged, fight for affection of son; friend intrudes and shows the painter the life he could be living away from the burdens of family, but painter wants to stay because he loves his son; still he decides to leave, as soon as he does his kid gets meningitis and dies

title: Dead Wake
author: Erik Larson
description: Churchill definitely wanted it to sink
memories: Lots of ridiculous coincidences that brought these two particular ships together, but it seems inevitable that some other big disaster would have happened if not this one; Churchill explicitly brought in an old guy to be his #2 and that guy was basicaly senile, and Churchill left him in charge for a while; also Churchill explicitly said he hoped a liner carrying Americans would sink and then draw the US into the war; the Lusitania was operating at 3/4 speed with one boiler turned off; it got shot in the perfect spot on its side, causing a severe tilt and tilting it so basically all of its lifeboats were useless; there was a recently opened channel where the Lusitania could have gone but was never told about, would have been safe there too; perfectly still water that day; also a second explosion, maybe from the boilers; Germany had a huge advantage with their U-boats, nobody really knew what they would be used for but they ended up being extremely decisive to the victory (the huge navies never really got used at least during this part of the war, they just hid); torpedos were still really bad tech, mre than half missed; the British NSA knew a lot about the movement of U20, the uboat that sunk lusitania

title: Only the Paranoid Survive
author: Andy Grove
description: CEOs are often the last to know about changes
memories: All of a sudden, industries change. Or, rather, they change really slowly, but people trapped in corporate heirarchy and not actually in touch with their customers think it happens all at once. Usually way alter than it actually happened; Grove tells the story of when Intel switchde from memory to microprocessors and from a startup into a big company, both times he was surprised; his framework is basically Porter five forces, but what if something 10X's... but then he says later that the internet isn't 10X in any dimension but is still an inflection point, which was pretty dumb and immediately contradictory with the main claim; very interesting that Intel lower managers had already shifted like 2/3 of the factory capacity away from memory to microprocessors by the time that management made their decision, kinda shows that it doesnt' matter what the managers do in a big company if the culture is sufficinetly permissive; gotta act before all the data are in or it's too late; "what would a new CEO do first if they joined" is a good way to judo your way into objectivity;  let things be hectic, but then impose ridiculous clarity; the end goal is conviction and clarity

title: The Everything Store
author: Brad Stone
description: Founders shape companies to be like them
memories: Bezos started extremely frugal; cares most about the customer; repeats stories ad nauseum, so his entire leadership team can repeat them and they're shaped in his mold; lots of dumb acquisitions in the dot com days; tons of very cutthroat tactics, like pulling publisher books from Amazon to show how reliant they were on Amazon when negotiating; duped all the big publishers with $9.99 pricing, then they got pissed and conspired with Apple to increase prices with the release of the iPad; lots of parallels to John, incl. a booming laugh that might be used strategically, and the intensity of being under the CEO's focus; makes me think that the CEO should be the north star of the company, folks are pulled forward by the gravity of the CEO so they should be the most demanding person at the company; super clear vision from the beginning, willing to lose money to win sales; hired versatile people even if they weren't that experienced, "athletes"; most suppliers were afraid that they would start a race to the bottom, as they often did; 6-page narratives and press releases started everyhting and replaced powerpoint;

title: Permutation City
author: Greg Egan
description: Don't overestimate our self-understanding
memories: Thought provoking and fun book, so much to take from it: the infinitely recursive, n-dimensional game of life stuff, the personhood and social dynamics of a brain-mapped AI future, the different ways to live and think about that world; basic story is that a guy wants to create a world independent of any physical reality in our world (and to prove that he wasn't a schizophrenic), so he fully buys into dust theory, which basically says that a spatially-dispersed set of atoms can find coherence and create a consciousness out of a pattern; our memories make us who we are, and forgetting them is the same as death in a meaningful way

title: How to Win Friends and Infuence People
author: Dale Carnegie
description: Just be nice and stop talking about yourself
memories: Don't criticize people, appreciate them and see their inherent strengths (even if they don't actually exist yet); say people's names; talk about what other people want to talk about (especially if you want something from them); don't talk so much yourself, just ask questions; make people feel important; don't argue; admit your own faults quickly to make people argue for your side; get folks saying yes; genuinely try to understand how other people see things (everyone thinks they're the hero); don't just give ideas, plant them. Say "and," not "but"

title: Diversity in the Workplace
author: Bari A. Williams
description: Privilege is freedom of attention
memories: Intersectionality makes folks feel left out of diversity discussions if not explicitly addressed; privilege can just mean you don't have to think about things related to your personal self at work; folks in minorities are asked to take on "unpaid labor" to teach people about diversity and things related to their minority group

title: Alibaba
author: Duncan Clark
description: The strategy to win China: be Chinese
memories: Seemed like a right place right time situation, nothing particularly magical about them other than how early they were to everything and how inspiring Jack Ma is for his employees; they worked incredibly hard in the early days; they have a lore that's very strong but not all that honest, the SARS scare in particular seemed to take on huge importance but there wasn't actually that big of a risk to anyone in the company; very Bezosian in having just a few core stories he repeats all the time, but Jack is probably a better improvizer than Bezos; amazing that he truly has no techincal chops at all; seems to be a puppet of the state at this point, just bending over backwards to do exactly what they want (buying a newspaper to shut down discourse on Hong Kong riots, transfering AliPay to be in a separate entity in his name, etc.)

title: Genghis Khan
author: Jack Weatherford
description: Genghis wasn't so bad; his imitators were
memories: The author's basic argument is that Genghis was misunderstood; Mongols became a bad word thanks to Europe and in justification of colonial/crusade activity, but they really did a ton to open up the world to each other (concluding, horrifically, with the black plague); Genghis basically conquered bigger territory after bigger territory, starting with the murder of his older brother and ending with his kids conquering Baghdad and China; at each stage, Genghis was great at 1) making the talented folks (and resources) from his conquered lands to be part of his army, often marrying them into his family; 2) knowing what each person did best and sending different generals on different missions befitting their style; 3) being very consistent with his ruthlessness: submit and you're totally safe, resist and you're totally destroyed; 4) minimizing casualties by caring about each soldier, staying mobile, and using psychological tactics as much as brute might (e.g., conquering surrounding regions and sending refugees into the main base to spread fear, disease, deplete resources;  false retreats to stretch out armies; Kubilai, his son, pretending to be Chinese); 5) learning their new tech and incorporating it into his superior military tactics; other interesting Mongol stuff: allowed for freedom of religion, huge cultural taboo around death and blood, so they never tortured people and preferred bows to hand-to-hand combat, they elected leaders by summoning kuriltai, basically quorum-based elections where whoever showed up voted in favor of the decision made, seems like they got pretty wrecked by alcoholism (but not Kubilai, which is why he was a better leader perhaps); had the cool 10-10-10-10 structure where it was basically concentric circles of army with the leader at the middle, fairly divided the spoils of war this way too, no looting; Mongols founded modern China, including Beijing and their own "forbidden city" which was like a central park at the time; assassin comes from hashish, linguistically; freed the south Asian countries from Indian rule (by conquering them), tried to invade Japan and Indonesia but failed

title: The Splendid and the Vile
author: Erik Larson
description: Churchill helped others find their courage
memories: A great back-and-forth between the inner workings of Churchill's governance during WW2 and the personal lives of all of his various advisors, family members, and such; he would go on the roof to watch London be bombed; he was completely devoid of any personal shame, walking around naked, taking meetings in a bath, etc.; had two baths a day, napped and stayed up super late; the whole key to his first year as PM was getting America in the war, the Lend-Lease act helped a lot (basically meant that US would lend Britain equipment, if they break it they buy it); Pearl Harbor was an amazing day for Churchill, so much happier not to bear the burden of the world on his shoulders; France surrendered real fast, but London withstood almost a whole year of blitzkrieg before Hitler got bored and attacked into Russia; lots of retreating on the ground, worried about actual invasion (likely should have happened right after Dunkirk, may have been fatal); two big tech stories were the guidance systems for bombers and parachute bombs; the RAF was much more efficient than the Luftwaffe because they thought themselves underdogs; Lord Beaverbrook whipped aircraft production into shape and burned a million bridges doing so, he ended up resigning fourteen times and was rejected 13; Hess, #3 from Nazis, flew himself to Britain to try and broker peace but lol just became a POW; Churchill faced internal resistance the whole time, but was an incredible orator which helped a lot (also turned one negative session into a vote of confidence which he knew he would pass), ended up leaving office after the war; the aircraft guns didn't really do anything for London, but helped the public morale because they were "fighting back";

title: How to Be an Antiracist
author: Ibram X. Kendi
description: There's no such thing as "not racist"
memories: A new spin on institutional racism, basically that if you don't actively counter racist policies, you're a racist. Traces a lot of racism to deep-seated cultural stuff, for example "I don't see color," as evidence of bias towards being non-political if you're in power. More diversity between parts of Africa than between skin colors. It's possible for black people to be racist and they often are between light and dark skin, "them niggers" jokes, etc.; that's evidence of an untold bias towards whiteness as beauty/correctness; young black males are more violent than young white males ONLY if you don't account for employment; antiracism is culturally relativistic; white people get bailouts if they're rich, black people get welfare if they're poor, and which one does the Republican party go after?; segregation and assimilation both assume white spaces are superior to black spaces; black solidarity against racism is okay... honestly a lot of this book feels self-contradictory and just like he put a word to his own messy worldview; median wealth of a black woman is $100, white woman is $42k; don't try to persuade people to not be racist, just change policies and let them see how it's in their self-interest -- he thinks self-interest is at the core of racism, then people justify racist worldviews backwards from self-interest

title: Way of the Peaceful Warrior
author: Dan Millman
description: What's left at the center of the onion? Everything.
memories: Okay this is tripping me out: I thought I didn't log this book but it turns out I did, and I didn't like it that much last time but remembered it as the best book ever, and soaked up the lessons really intently this time, so the book struck me as profound; I was in a mental state to believe everything it presented, and it sounded great and I rally loved the lessons; maybe part of the lesson is to be open to experiences (!) but also just that these ratings kinda stink and don't capture how much I'll think about a book after reading it; I'll summarize the learning in a Twitter thread: https://twitter.com/cdkeil/status/1320437949637349376?s=20

title: Chaos Monkeys
author: Antonio Garcia Martinez
description: Cynicism is the path of least resistance
memories: Goldman and Facebook both didn't want to open up data because it was their competitive advantage (Goldman for the spread of some assets they priced; Facebook for their user data); founders are important because you do the most important, poorly thought out work of your life with them, mutual destruction and proof you're not wasting your time; as CEO, you're the chief therapist and the person most in need of therapy; chaos monkey is like a monkey walking through a server room pulling out cords; lots of back room wheeling and dealing on the phone; better to firewall the company away from non-work distractions like fundraising; Sam Altman could be dropped on an island of cannibals and be king in five years; startups take focus and the ability to endure endless amounts of shit; gotta stalk people before meetings; CEOs internalize stress for everyone else; acquisitions turn into zero-sum games with investors and founders; humans love immortality projects; Sheryl SAndberg was awesome at picking up on subtle cues, running meetings with big egos; Facebook was lots of bold experimentation, failure, occasional huge success, and then integration into culture; "the more powerful the class, the more it claims not to exist" - Guy debord; only one miracle per startup idea; "irreverence without disrespect, competence without arrogance, ambition without ego"; either I hire you now, or I hire you in a year to work for the guy whose job I'm offering you now

title: Masters of the Word
author: William Bernstein
description: New comms centralize power first
memories: Just massively lost my attention, felt like a slog of proper nouns the whole time without a strong enough lattice for any of it to actually stick; basic theme is that each wave of innovation is centralizing because the new thing is so expensive and only controlled by a few, then eventually it get democratized over time; Greek was the first alphabet with vowels, before you had to guess at what vowel sounds to make so reading was really a ton of interpretation and guessing; pillage/fire is great for historians because wood becomes charcoal and it preserves clay tablets; "ghoti" is fish: tough, women, nation; modern Hebrew is actually Aramaic; "audited" is "read aloud"; preliterate societies can only remember things for three generations; Athens ostracized tons of its greatest eaders; newly literate kids write without word spaces; church hated the printing press because it let anyone read and interpret the bible; Gutenberg didn't invent moveable type, just made manufacturing so precise that individual letters fit together perfectly; Gutenberg needed 100k pieces of type to keep all his people working on printing the Bible; Martin Luther may have reached EVERY German-speaking person; Tyndale's Old Testament was amazing and translated Hebrew to English amazingly well, helped come up with tons of phrases like a broken heart, drop in the bucket, fly in ointment, old as the hills, white as snow, etc; the stamp tax from Britain was a tax on libel; Senate deliberation was originally private for first 6 years (even to press); two days after transcontinental telegraph line finished in america, Pony Express went out of business; hitler understood that he was trying to persuade the "credulous" people who believe everything they read; downfall of Soviet Union was partially because they mass produced radios that the US (CIA) and UK could broadcast to to break through jamming and fight propaganda;

title: Homo Deus
author: Yuval Noah Harari
description: Science and humanism run the world
memories: End of the book was so bad. So bad. It was this weird theory that we'd start worshiping data and only value things that add to the information flow for... reasons? Also data is the ultimate abstraction, and therefore basically unfalsifiable... data is just the absence of randomness, so yes, we will worship non-entropy, really insightful bro. The argument of the book is basically that humans conquered disease, famine, and war, and now we need something else to do. What will that be? Being happy, living forever, and making ourselves Gods. Most interesting part of the book was the stuff on humanism vs. science (e.g., natural selection implies no free will, but humanism demands it). Societies that can collaborate effectively win; people live their lives to participate in stories, but later, those stories seem meaningless; you have to distort reality to tell compelling, uniting stories, but distort too much and you're screwed; you can either go on a solitary spiritual journey and think independently, OR create systems that others can follow simply (religion) and there's not an easy way to do both; peak-end rule means make stuff better at the end and people will remember it all as not that bad

title: The Game
author: Neil Strauss
description: Bros seduce other bros for a living
memories: Pretty silly book, recommended as a guide to human behavior; it's all about how to manipulate women to sleep with you but only lightly covers the actual "science" they develop as a weird community; the most notable message is to create your own version of reality and have it be stronger than anyone else's; other mildly interesting human behavior tidbits, but for the most part just an entertaining read about a fucked up corner of humanity; point of a neg is to lower a woman's self-esteem; smile, well-groomed, center of attention, funny; focus not on the target but on everyone else, then eventually pivot to them once they want your attention; appeals to what other people think/say turn off the critical part of the brain; Tom Cruise had a very firm grasp on his reality; men love ascending rankings of things; say "thank you" when complimented; ultimatums are expressions of powerlessness; Parsifal sees the holy grail guy in pain, decides not to ask because you don't ask the King unless addressed first, then King dies -- trust gut

title: Shark's Fin and Sichuan Pepper
author: Fuchsia Dunlop
description: Chinese culture is food
memories: Recommended by Maryli's fam; talks all about how China IS food; follows a foreigner that trains to be a Chinese chef; she was like an alien to Chinese folks; government officials are corrupt and feast on crazy stuff while everyone else starves; lots of chemicals in the food from pollution nowadays; lots of China is being leveled for redevelopment; very different regional cuisines in NSEW - North is Beijing and official, tons of courses, East is Shanghai (?) and fusion cultured; South is peasant food, West is Sichuan and spicy; food is naturally quite healthy and they will counteract health conditions with food (e.g., cold, eat spicy); the Mao thing was a problem because each official reported higher levels of food than they had, leading to bad rationing then a huge famine; chinese culture doesn't think of animals as anything other than moving things, so they'll eat anything; chinese food, texture matters a lot and people seek out interesting ones (like gelationous skin and cartilage and stuff); Chinese love tech because they recently needed it to escape famine; north is noodles, south is rice; Chinese are straight up racist against Uyghurs; the word for population is people-mouths

title: Nonviolent Communication
author: Marshall Rosenberg
description: Observations, emotions, needs
memories: Pretty great book, extremely practical and mind-bending, instantly worked to help me express myself to Maryli; it's basically a great way to not be  misunderstood, and to get past anger to see what's on the other side in terms of emotions and your deep needs; it's surprisingly hard to even identify what emotions things make me feel, but I am willing to learn that skill; rule: don't speak for other people's emotions or needs, but you can ask about them; vulnerability can help resolve conflict; evaluations are not observations; then try to make your internal self have positive self talk and think about your needs, not judgments; even compliments can be judgements that put you above other people if you don't tell them how they have met your needs

title: Uncanny Valley
author: Anna Wiener
description: Everyone's equal in SV, some more equal
memories: Anna was an employee at Github and Mixpanel, and wrote about how Suhail was megalomaniacal, and how tech bros are pretty self obsessed and hate art; also SV is probably sexist and certainly unappreciative of business/non-technical people; she also befriended Patrick Collison and he seems like a true G, although the piece about Patrick being more important than her was tough to read... it's true that in SV, there is a very real heirarchy of importance. Makes me realize how lucky I am to be at Astranis and to have risen super fast. Talks about how people in SV trusted early employees like family and gave them God Mode over user data; VC worship, describes a VC panel as "watching two ATMs in conversation"; meritocracy and rationalism help people already in positions of power and ignore history; tech helps fill all spare time with other people's thoughts

title: Why We Sleep
author: Matthew Walker
description: Go to bed and wake up at constant times
memories: You get deep sleep early in the night and REM late in the night, which is why circadian timing is important; 7-9 hour sleep opportunity; no blue light; stable bedtime; if you don't do these things, you'll die; this dude is a bad writer, had to skim basically the whole thing; there's no "wake up in the middle of the night" thing, but we are biphasic with a longer sleep and an afternoon nap as natural; sleep reactivation of memory is real; 65 degrees is ideal temp; snoozing bad because alarms hurt your heart;

title: The Circle
author: Dave Eggers
description: Tech "utopia" doesn't sound so great
memories: Woman gets hired at The Circle (Google), a tech company in SV run by the Three Wise Men (tech wunderkind, business CEO, inspirational guy), and the core innovation was a real ID solution for internet-wide sign-in. Then they made a ridiculous amount of money, started doing tons of side projects, and eventually wanted to make "the circle complete" aka everyone is a user, every everything everyone does is tracked, and transparency is perfect. They think "privacy is theft" from the world; expose everything to light and it'll get better. Girl joins work, her friend is at the tippity top of the org, she's in customer service, fights to get 100 ratings, meets a series of boys who she schloops in increasing weird ways, her dad has MS and her ex boyfriend is anti-tech, escalates to where she is REALLY trying to increase her PartiRank (participation in the company activities) because she's gotten ragged on for like not going to a random Portugal happy hour. She eventually gets caught by company cameras (installed all over the world) while stealing a kayak for a nighttime trek, meets the inspirational founder, who brainwashes her basically so she goes "clear,' recording and livestreaming her every move; she then rolls out a product that can find any criminal within 20m through the power of the network, "finds" her exboyfriend, who kills himself. Turns out that one of the boys she's been schlooping is the young wunderkind founder, obviously, and he tries to warn her about the risks of complete domination by one company, but she doesn't listen. the end, bad guys win

title: Liftoff
author: Eric Berger
description: SpaceX almost died... many times
memories: Sounds so epic, exactly what I want out of a company: a mortal struggle, epic payoff; the story about the Flight 4 buckling and a guy having to climb inside to release the pressure is insane; Musk seems like John, having to decide all the things, folks that succeeded in the org melded to Elon's culture (but John needs to establish more of a culture); Flight 2 was super close, similar to Astra's fourth flight; Flgiht 3 was a step back, first and second stage collided, only had like three months between 3 and 4, needed to get it to survive, but then there was still a huge risk to survival after the launcht that engineers didn't appreciate, saved by Commercial Resupply for ISS contract; lots of the early employees trashed their family lives and got divorced; Kwaj sounds really fun (for a fresh-out of college kid);

title: Valley of the Gods
author: Alexandra Wolfe
description: The Thiel Fellowship exists, kids are kids
memories: Total trash book, very badly written and not very insightful. She allllmost got there at the end but mostly it was a bunch of totally random chapters, not woven together into a narrative, and the people she focused on were not that interesting. She also didn't explain them well -- Laura Deming, woman working on human longevity, is just hot and that's all we know. Constantly introduces then re-introduces concepts. Presumably this book is popular because this woman is in like Plutonic orbit around Peter Thiel.

title: Zucked
author: Roger McNamee
description: Early advisor to Facebook has his regrets
memories: Basically unreadable, 0/2 on whoever recommended these books; McNamee famously advised Facebook not to sell for $1B to Microsoft, and then was an outsted advisor who thought Facebook was the absolute worst in the wake of the 2016 election; his basic point is something that I agree with and is now obvious, which is that social media is breaking peoples' brains; maybe it was more controversial in 2016? probably not; argues that Bushnell from Atari started the chill dress culture of SV; BJ Fogg "Persuasive Technology" seems to be the growthhack bible; Bell Labs invented the transitor; less than 1% of my data profile online is what I have given sites; the author says that people are clinging to order, and includes TATTOOS as an example of this; the book is generally full of garbage takes and is about 5x too long; you're happy for the first ten minutes on a platform then sad after; treat Twitter as a fiction source, and it'll be more fun

title: The Topeka School
author: Ben Lerner
description: Politicians and other experts "spread"
memories: Wasn't about all that much -- kid who's a debate champ, has psychologist parents, and there's a mentally handicapped kid in his high school who throws a pool ball at a girl after being bullied; but it's really thematically awesome, builds inside jokes with you the whole time, and very wise in its dissection of relationships, mental health, etc.; the dad commits adultery a lot, leaves first wife for second, has kids, but cheats on her too; wife becomes famous as a writer and it affects her friendships; chalelnges of psychologist-friend relationships; cookie-cutter-ness of many neighborhoods; they include the mentally handicapped kid as a cruel joke;  also had smart things to say about policy, LD, and extemp, mostly that the kids are approximating intelligence more than actually being knowledgeable

title: Creativity, Inc.
author: Ed Catmull
description: Pixar's open culture sparks creativity
memories: Story of Ed Catmull, one time digital graphics nerd turned Pixar CEO forever, 25-year relationship with Steve Jobs, sounds like John Lasseter was truly the ridiculously talented one, but there were other directors who were also amazing (e.g., Brad Bird, who created The Iron Giant before coming to Pixar for Ratatouille and The Incredibles); Ed credits a lot of their success to being an open culture where anyone could give anyone else notes, Disney think worked because ti was basically a Pixar acquisition of Disney Animation (Ed became the Pres; Lasseter the Chief Creative); Jobs had an extra gear he could kick into when he needed it, Microsoft acquistion of Pixar almost happened when they were selling an animation computer; their "braintrust" seems like a dope concept, basically just a group of people who get to rescue the company every once in a while; goal is to combat fear and increase comms in the company; messaging can seem like lying, share problems!;

title: A Promised Land
author: Barack Obama
description: Obama did a LOT before obstruction
memories: Prevented a depression, saved the auto and banking industries, killed bin Laden, capped the Deepwater Horizon spill, repealed don't ask don't tell, passed Obamacare, fended off Trump's birtherism, and so much more; really made me want to be President, but also convinced me of how many things have to go ridiculously right for you to become President; you have to be a little selfish and a lot crazy, for sure; Obama's insistence was always on having a good process in place to make decisions, which feels right and insightful for business stuff; genuinely tried to be bipartisan; also appointed two supreme court justices; chilled with his body men, who seem to have the best job ever

title: No Rules Rules
author: Reed Hastings
description: High talent density, fewer rules
memories: Basic idea: fire everyone who's merely meh, then take away all of the rules; don't have expense policies, just ask people to spend company money like it's theirs; radical autonomy for people but they get held to account if they mess up; sounds like a Finance/HR nightmare but kinda refreshing?; also they want super blunt honesty all the time, in meetings and at any time; open sessions to grill leaders; take tons of real vacation, leaders do it so everyone else does as well (unlimited); "lead with context, not control" is the basic idea, but honestly it seems like they skimp out of writing down stuff and instead make people "use their judgment," high openness with financial data too, which is part of "context";

title: Prisoners of Geography
author: Tim Marshall
description: Geography determines politics
memories: Chinese immigrants are heading into Russia and might take over Siberia; access to water is hugely contentious, as is shipping lanes (e.g., Russia has no warm water ports); mountains separating China and India have largely prevented conflict (except over high ground of Tibet/Nepal); lots of this is from colonialism and then terrible straight-line map drawing; India/Pakistan/Bangladesh is a good example, when British left they gave TONS of land to India and split Pakistan in two (Bangladesh = East Pakistan), also Bangladesh is falling into the ocean via flooding; Europe has lots of navigable waterways; South America all the activity is on the coasts, arctic is going to be hugely contentious as the polar ice caps melt; Africa has the Sahara desert but also the S____ thing beneath it where the desert is expanding; Japan used to occupy Korea so they aren't friends; "strategic depth" = ability to fall back, like Russia; Russia divided by Ural mountains into east (Moscow) and west (siberia); that's why Russia invaded Crimea, to keep a warm-water port, when Ukraine became more western-leaning; lots of people want Russia's oil; Mississippi Basin has more navigable river than the rest of the world combined; Iraq is really three countries: Babylonia (Baghdad), Assyria (Mosul), Sumer (Baghdad); Israel/Palestine is all about the Temple Mount (where Abraham went to sacrifice Isaac, where Mohammad aschended ot heavem); if India invades Pakistan, it falls back into Aghanistan (so the two fight a proxy war there); Nippon (Japan) means sun origin because there's nothing East of them but ocean, so they own the sun; Brazil is bigger than the entire EU

title: The Art of Loving
author: Erich Fromm
description: Love is collective and individual both
