memories: Basic idea is that you have to bring your whole self to a marriage; keys are care, responsibility, respect, knowledge; motherly love is milk (nourishment) and honey (joy/happiness), anxiety is passed down, oops; gotta love everybody to love yourself and your partner; selfishness is the opposite of self-love, it is a repsonse to a lack of self-care; idea of finding truth in thought is Western, led to the Church (dogma) and science alike; vanity = need to be loved, not to love; two people experiencing themsleves from the center of their being (so they need to be there themselves), and conflict is okay as long as it is honest and deepens the bond; concentration -> no boredom; loving = GIVING love with no guarantees of reciprocation

title: The All-or-Nothing Marriage
author: Eli Finkel
description: Set expectations: frequent peaks, valleys okay
memories: Asking for more from your marriage has to be paired with putting more time in, or it'll fail; spouses have to take on a lot because we have fewer close friends and relatives now; protestantism led to an increase in the value of matrimony, Catholics thought it was a necessary evil (liked celibacy better); when you could actually support a family with one wage-earner, mom could stay home and build a family in the private sphere while men were public (neither was superior); true insight into the spouse is downstream of true insight into yourself, both are very hard; more androgenous or equal marriages are better, statistically; completely naked, exposed, vulnerable, and secure; communication is the #1 problem, arguing productively is important; can't be selfish in a marriage, but you CAN seek personal self-knowledge... happiness != meaning, and the latter is okay; forfeiture is Heidegger's phrase for living a busy, distracted life; 62% of childless mothers are happy in their marriage, but only 38% of women with children are happy; marital happiness only goes down over time; serendipity is key, only way to see the bright and dark sides of everything for real, spend time without purpose

title: Man's Search for Meaning
author: Viktor Frankl
description: Meaning of life: find meaning in your life
memories: Wrote this book in nine days; happiness and meaning have to ensure, can't be pursued; prisoners who survived camps were those who fought hard for existence and ditched morality, "the best of us did not return"; gas chambers not so scary, spared the intense moral anguish of deciding to commit suicide; coping mechanism for pain is humor and curiosity; lots of politics and religious talk, but little else in the concentration camps; there were always choices to make, people handled the horrors extremely differently (counter to Freud's claim that everyone would be the same in a situation of life and death; spiritual freedom is an inner achievement, even in the face of suffering, and makes life meaningful and purposeful; ACCEPT YOUR FATE, life asks things of you; hard times give you a chance to grow beyond yourselves; if you have a why to live, you can bear almost any how; life is real and concrete, not abstract; the past is concrete and having been is a kind of being; all human life always has meaning, even in suffering; hyper-intention ("I'm going to be SO worried, just watch me!" as a way to counter worries)

title: Carrie
author: Stephen King
description: Simple story; creepy imagery
memories: Mostly about a religious mom who thinks she's given birth to a demonic child who can control things with her mind; kid doesn't know what her period is when she gets it at age 16, and that's when her psychic (TK; telekinesis) powers kick in too; she's bullied at school; one girl doesn't want to bully her but does with the crowd, the main girl is a jerk who befriends an older scumbag who dumps blood on Carrrie at prom after she wins queen; Carrie blows up the town, stabbed by her mom before Carrie stops her mom's heart; super creepy scene when Carrie sees the neighbor girl in a bikini, mom lurches over and SCREAMS, is going to kill Carrie but then rocks rain down from the heavens to stop her

title: The Institute
author: Stephen King
description: Psychic kids take down the deep state
memories: Smart kid gets abducted, parents murdered, because he has some telekinetic ability; put into an institute that you come to find out is one of many across the world to prevent nuclear war by predicting the future and then killing people who would precipitate war; you also get a folksy hero who helps the main character live once he escapes from the institute; overall a huge page turner, super fun, no real lasting message to take from it other than loving Stephen King

title: Already Free
author: Bruce Tift
description: West = fix what's wrong; East = already free
memories: Contrasts the two styles of fixing your brain: either do the Western thing, which means to acknowledge that your past self and current self are undivided, or the Eastern thing where your self and the world are undivided;  West says you have to go fix yourself, East says you're already good, no more work to do; they contradict but they're useful in different ways; East is all about observing the actual experience you're having rather than labeling, not fear but shortness of breath and dilated eyes, then ask if it's really killing you to feel that way; in normal therapy, can feel like you're a problematic person at the core, something to fix; we hurt ourselves to absolve our parents of guilt and maintain their idea of perfection; lots of mental activity and neurosis is a distraction from full experience of life and all of your real feelings; neurotic masculine is anger, threats from the outside world, private superiority; are we going to be aggressive towards self or accepting?; no bias towards joy and away from sadness; we take our lives and problems very seriously, imbue them with meaning even though they don't really exist; developmental approach is becoming, fruitional is about being; if you're vague about experience, easier to label based on past experience and stories; neurosis is easier than sanity in the short term but bad long-term;  avoidance of sensitivites is what makes them a threat; in codependent relationship, one is connector and other is independent, you can combat by doing the opposite of your usual; honeymoon phase of relationship is not actually knowing them; tool for disagreement "are you asking me for something"

title: The Making of the Atomic Bomb
author: Richard Rhodes
description: Neutron discovery made use inevitable
memories: Amazing how little agency was exercised in the use of the bomb, it felt inevitable... Truman was remarkably uninvolved, it was kinda just James Byrnes as the "assistant President" that made a lot of that committee stuff happen; discovered the neutron in 1932, dropped the bomb in 1945; two different bomb designs (gun vs. implosion), the ignition is like literally 10-ish neutrons that start the chain reaction; lots of science that wasn't particularly well explained, but then lots of history (who said what to who when) that was hyper detailed but also sorta lost the thread sometimes; could have done a lot better explaining who the people were when intro'd, wasn't clear which names you were supposed to pay attention to; lots of horrific imagery at the end of post-bomb Hiroshima; the bomb wasn't all that much more deadly than the bombing of Tokyo, but percentage wise it was terrible, 50% of the cities died instantly, vs more like 10% for a normal really bad bombing; third bomb was almost dropped too; Japanese military was pretty divorced from Japanese polity; one bursting uranium nucleus can make a grain of sand move; most scientists are afraid of looking like fools, but Einstein wasn't once he got famous = huge advantage for learning; the discovery was inevitable, just a matter of time, which made it a race to be the first to get it; they didn't know if it would chain react through the entire ocean/atmosphere early; because Los Alamos was so rustic, people thought it was epic (startups!); average age was 25 of Los Alamos scientists; one secret agent dude successfully destroyed Germany's heavy water reserve, keeping them from doing more plutonium tests; author thinks that disclosing the bomb before using it might have solved the problem of nuclear armament; a committee wanted to build a 300-column thermal diffusion plant, leader (Groves) came in and said "make it 2100", Truman thought he authorized only a military bombing

title: There is No Antimemetics Division
author: qntm
description: Ideas resistant to sharing are mind-bending
memories: So thrilling and braintwisting; the core idea of an idea that's hard to share is so cool... you go into the room and see it, but you can't make notes about it that make any sense, or remember it, etc. -- just a memory camoflauge; core group of people that have to fight it, but they're basically always in their first day on the job because they have to battle things that destroy their memory... "oh wait, I've been preparing for this for years"; "we don't even know how we acquired this thing" lol; the story itself was convoluted and kinda sad, the head of the Foundation is married to a normie who happens to be really really good at remembering things and can fight antimemes as a result, but then she gets sent to purgatory before he resurrects her as an idea that goes back in time to kill the mega-anti-meme? maybe?; mostly I just haven't had this much fun reading a book in a while, so thoroughly surprised me and delighted by being so different than anything I'd ever thought about before

title: Educated
author: Tara Westover
description: Disturbing, sad ending; bad people exist
memories: This book really messed me up, it was sad and made me think about how much pain there is in the world; also Tara didn't really seem to get it, even at the end. Not wrapped up, the bad guys were still out therebeing bad, and she didn't seem to have  fully processed what  happenedto her

title: Dune
author: Frank Herbert
description: Knowing the future != changing it
memories: The actual action of the book is so small compared to the very long setup; awesome world-building, cool characters and competing political schemes, powers that people try to reckon with (Bene Gesserit) even though they ultimately fail to harness them to their ends; liked that you had epic space travel but no computers; Fremen, Muad'dib, Harkonnen, Emperor, girl he loved and then his wife who was the Emperor's daughter, gom jabbar, Atreides, Caladan, the voice, Shai-hulud

title: American Politics
author: Very Short Introductions
description: Politcians connect the public to governance
memories: Senate has a misplaced veto, should just be Pres, with filibuster; get around it by budget reconciliation, which can be blocked by the Byrd rule, which can be avoided by shenanigans; there was a Gilded age before with lots of income inequality, also an era of heavy partisanship... which was broken up by the great depression; in the last great party period, it was easier to make an issue-based third party

title: Ideology
author: Very Short Introductions
description: Ideology = impratical, academic political science
memories: Soooo philosophical and boring/intentionally obtuse, I guess academics have to feel superior somehow; basic idea is that ideology is fancier political philosophy (socialism, communism, liberalism, conservatism, anarchism), and you can study them by looking at the priority of their preferences/words they use to define themselves and the proximity of those words to each other (e.g., liberty close to security is different from liberty close to individuality); politics is dumbest-down ideology for the masses; hermeneutics is the study of interpretation; conservatism = aversion to change, obsession with "natural" vs "unnatural" change

title: Neoliberalism
author: Very Short Introductions
description: Neolib = intl. development saying "trust us"
memories: One size fits all in neoliberalism: everyone always needs their currency to be unpegged from the dollar (even though it normally leads to currency crash), and less government spending, and more capability-building in stuff we want to buy from them... in return, they get get loans from the international development orgs like the IMF and World Bank, but the austerity measures damage countries as much as the lack of a loan might have; neolib failed Greece, Ghana, Argentina; privatize state-owned enterprises, cut taxes, open borders; successor to Keynesian (i.e., government should spend money) theory; Deregulation, Liberalization, Privatization; Washington Consensus; Thatcher sold BP and other British enterprises for a fraction of their worth, but instead of investing the profits in the company, the owners just resold it for insane money lol; western countries shifted from industry to finance, but not China

title: Geopolitics
author: Very Short Introductions
description: This guy's geopolitics is ideologically bankrupt
memories: The author considers himself a modern geopolitican rather than a classical one, which basically means he thinks he's woke but has nothing interesting to say about anything; truly nuts, he just like does little rhetorical tricks to cover up a lack of anything interesting to say about the world; there was a whole chapter on "objects" where he was basically like "maps are important," and another where he was like "media shapes public opinion." Yeah, no shit buddy. Reminded me that Agamben existed, and exceptionalism stuff from Said, both of which would be infinitely more fun to read than this garbage.

title: Diplomatic History
author: Very Short Introductions
description: Modern diplomacy is way more complicated
memories: Diplomacy is about war and avoiding it (but also trade); legation is like an embassy lite; timing really matters, shit went down in the Revolutionary War during the negotiaton that hurt the US's position, Britain may have agreed to all of their terms if not for time passing; Benjamin Franklin helped negotiate that peace; Spain was a weird non-party to that negotiation even though they still had their territory in the Americas; Germany took over Alsace-Lorraine in WW1, then France got pissed and made the post-war terms super punitive in Germany, which led to inflation/collapse, which led to WW2; marching through Belgium to get to France for Blitzkrieg was a decision that didn't go through the German chancellor/govt, military just decided it, but that involved the UK (Belgium's ally) in the war, doi; after WW2, drama when Churchill and Stalin divided up Europe into spheres of influence even though FDR was NOT a fan, lots of "but let me check with Dad" in the USA stuff going on, interesting how Churchill stood up for his points HARD; France was super against the Iraq war, basically no reason that war actually had to happen, we were just worried about WMD even though inspectors didn't find anything (we trusted our own intelligence), v tenuous reaons to invade

title: Why We're Polarized
author: Ezra Klein
description: Polarization is a systems, not people, problem
memories: Structural imbalance of electoral college means dems rewarded for right-of-middle and reps can go really extreme; most voters driven by hating the other party rather than liking theirs; Dixiecrats basically were their own wing of the dems and they only cared about protecting slavery and lynching; racists switched to republicans when Dems passed the Civil Rights Act, "small government" as euphemism for "eff civil rights";  people not engaged in politics care about the impact on themselves, people in politics identify with their positions; politics has become a primary identity that carries tons of other identities with it; people would rather punish their out-groups even if it means they get less; if you're smarter, you're just better at rationalizing; identity is powerful, why Buzzfeed quizzes (which HP house are you?!) took off; strong parties moderate extremism, but our parties are weak now; more national = less state intersts = more polarization on parties; close elections lead to less compromise, perennial losers will compromise; our political system balances states, not parties

title: Firepower
author: Matt Lacombe
description: NRA created an ideology and an identity
memories: The success of the NRA was from their appeals to identity ("they're coming for your way of life!") rather than to any logical appeal about safety; it's engrained in the gun culture by hosting courses for gun safety and such; there was a revolt when younger people who wanted it to be a political entity took over just as the old guys were trying to move it out West / keep it about marksmanship; it was apolitical for a while but with Reagan, hitched onto the Republican party, basically did the Trump rhetoric before Trump; the constitutional is a relatively recent trick, post-WW2 when guns were no longer about protecting against invasion; three kinds of power: win the argument, set the agenda, and influence the preferences of others; their secret trick is mobilizing letter-writing campaigns; second amendment was a way for the NRA to combat/circumvent the "rights" talk of the civil rights movement; gun control is overwhelmingly popular but politicians are still afraid of the NRA

title: International Relations
author: Very Short Introductions
description: IR = global organization of political authority
memories: Realist = power; liberalist = social contract; constructivists = social meaning creation; English School = rules and intstitutions around power; India used to be owned by a private company, and the UN legitimized empire in the early days; Westphalia secularized the international realm by saying states could self-determine religion

title: Bringing up the Boss
author: Rachel Pacheco
description: Gotta learn the basics to manage
memories: Nothing mind-blowing, all stuff I have heard already; maybe the "just listen and ask questions" is something I can do more actively; I just want to be a manager already! some of this is impossible to care about in theory, but I can see how I'd be bad at some of it; set expectations; people want power, affiliation, achievement; have norms, psychological safety for teamwork; think about community, not culture

title: The Fabric of Reality
author: David Deutsch
description: People don't take scientific explanation seriously
memories: Science isn't about inducing laws from test data, it's about the best explanations we have for what we can see and test; the four best explanations are the Turing (computation), Darwin-Dawkins (evolution), Popper (epistemology),and quantum physics of the multiverse; people in science believe those theories but don't really take their explanatory power seriously; the photon-slit experiment is parallel universes interfering with ours; solipsism is dumb because it doesn't explain anything, you still have to have a model of the world running in your head, and that requires the explanation of the world solipsism tries to reject; real = autonomous and complex; everything that happens in a brain could be put into virtual reality, all just physical stuff; genes are basically virtual reality generators: they hold information about the world and help an organism/themselves replicate accordingly; it is knowledge, not objects, that are adapted to niches... life = embodiment of knowledge; life matters to the Universe, fate of our planet rests with our concious brains; quantum computers tap into other parallel universes to help with computation; spacetime doesn't allow for free will, but quantum physics does (basically all the versions of you diverge); the quantum observation thing is the "Copenhagen Interpretation" but it's not real, kinda a copout; epistemology is emergent physics, predicts how knowledge (physical thing) grows;

title: How To Change Your Mind
author: Michael Pollan
description: Psychedelics are worth studying
memories: Psychedelics became part of a culture war in the 1960s around the Vietnam War and when Timothy Leary sparked a huge counter-cultural revolution around dropping out of school, not working or agreeing to be drafted into the army; but in the 50s and 60s there was a lot of real scientific research into psychedelics as a therapeutic medicine, before it all got moved underground; they work by being a tryptamine, like serotonin, and possibly by chilling out the Default Mode Network in the brain that helps create self-hood; scrambles up, adds entropy to ways of thinking to break out of common patterns; "set" (mindset) and "setting" (who and where you trip) matter a ton, it's a very suggestive drug so you can end up basically having the trip you or your guide set you up to have; common to have spiritual experiences where your ego dissolves and you imagine life after your death, or seeing your own birth, or reliving childhood trauma; best is to lean into the experiences to avoid a bad trip; maybe they evolved like this because it was adaptive (people eat mushrooms, like them, tell stories about them, the mushrooms proliferate?);

title: American Nations
author: Colin Woodard
description: America was never really united
memories: Yankees = Puritans, NE, basically religious fundamentalists, very highly educated, eventually the Midwest too; New Netherlands = NYC, founded by Dutch who were the shit, very cosmopolitan and diverse, sided with the South on lots of things; Midlands = Pennsylvania = Quakers, religious tolerance and pacifism to a fault, let indians overrun them until Benjamin Franklin raised a private army to stop; Tidelanders = Virginia, aka pretend aristocracy, had slaves but not brutally terrible to them, fewer slaves per white person, nearly died right away but tobacco saved them and made them rich; Deep South = from Barbados, horrible slavers, 10:1 black:white ratio; Greater Appalachia = frontierspeople, some married indians, all love fighting and have supported every war ever; El Norte = northern Mexico, first federal building in USA is in Santa Fe, hardworking more than normal mainland Mexicans; Far West = dependent on government becasue their land sucks, dependent on corporations who employ them all, so they support govt, now lots of Defense work; Left Coast = SF/Oregon, individualistic and optimistic, side with Yankees on most things; major groups are Yankee/Netherland/Left Coast vs Tidewater/South/Appalachia with Midland, Norte, Far WEst in between; French enclave in Louisiana and Canada; the first American cowboys were Indians, lots of South words (rodeo, ranch, lasso) came from Spanish; Southerners love "libertas" or liberties for the people who have them, aka not everybody, aka power for aristocracy; Deep South loves castes, aka you can never change your class; appalachia was excluded from Contintental Congress and Constitutional Convention, Appalachia got absolutely hosed by Hamilton: issued IOUs for Rev war salaries, then stopped taking them, then once IOUs sold out of Appalachia to Hamilton's friends, turned that into silver and gold, unreal; Jackson was an asshole who kicked out Indians from the West; Yankees first to CA but then gold rush brought a ton of hooligan; South absolutely fought the Civil War to preserve slavery, they seriously fucked up by fighting Fort Sumter, brought rest of the colonies togethter, before that the Yankees were the only people actually that mad at the South; West Virginia founded to be part of the Union; railroads started the Far West; immigrants don't go to the South; the Reconstruction of the South had Northerners take over schools and such but the  center of South cultural life moved to churches; Yankee = sovial salvation, public protestantism, we need to fix society; South = individual salvation, private protestantism, the world is fucked, just save yourself; the Dixie bloc is insanely disingenuous, talk like revolutionaries and populists then just help corporate interests and maintain the artistocracy of the South

title: Seveneves
author: Neal Stephenson
description: Moon explodes; seven women create new world
memories: The first half was amazing, I absolutely loved the politics of the responses to the moon exploding -- how the picked kids to come up to the ISS, how they built it up, people having tons of sex lol, scientists helping people believe it'll all be fine even though they believe it's BS, the tech founder dude who got the giant ice block; then the second half was all parable: seven traits from the seven Eves, turned into a galactic civilizaton of genetically engineered people, red vs blue, and the tribe of fish people on Earth who thought everyone else was cowards for leaving, Sonar Taxlaw, the human encyclopedia, just falling in love with the boy immediately on seeing him; the President being ruthless in getting up to the station against the law, then politicking her way to meaningless power that almost destroyed all of humanity; Red is all about narrative and showiness, Blue is rationality but no show/story

title: These Truths
author: Jill Lepore
description: "Land of the free" founded on slavery
memories: She kinda belabored the "slaves made us evil" point too hard, IMO, and then got normally political at the end of the book.... interesting how even historians can't separate emotion from the present; Spanish came to conquer, English came to settle; "liberties" mean priviliges given to rich people; the Magna Carta was revoked right as it was written, not resurrected until much later, remembered as the end of divine right of kings; democracy was considered the failed version of "polity," just like monarchy -> tyranny and aristocracy -> oligarchy; Franklin on the constitution: "I expect no better, and because I am not sure that it is not the best."; Constitution was radical in anti-religious tests for office; newspapers created parties; early campaigns were not waged by politicians, considered demagoguery; Jefferson had black children (7!!); old politicians literally made up identities, like Harrison as the "log cabin" bandidate even though he lived in a mansion; the Confederacy absolutely was about slavery and preserving it; 223 years of slavery from 1638 to 1865, the equivalent in years ahead is 2088; railroad companies really made the west happen; 14th amendment meant for slaves, became rights of corporations due to a dubious remembering by someone who helped write it that "persons" included corporations; McKinley beat Bryan by having $7m instead of $300k to campaign; War of the World was a HUGE thing; 1929 1/4 of Americans had stock, compared to 1% a generation before; FDR was the first modern liberal; Whitaker and Baxter wrote the modern campaign playbook: keep it simple, explain nothing, subtlety is your enemy, simplify, make it personal, attack, pretend you are the voice of the people, "win the controversy." New Deal almost struck down in courts, FDR threatened to totally revise the supreme court, they folded and let it through; black people not allowed to use GI Bill benefits after WW2 (cities = black, suburbs = white) and gave free college to everyone WW2 but blacks; quantifiable public opinion is BS, people don't know what they actually believe; Eisenhower was the first "social liberal and fiscal conservative"; 1949 FCC fairness doctrine, done away with later so no equality in coverage of both sides; southern State basically ignored the desegragation order for over a year; LBJ was awesome but then sold out his own social/domestic programs (incl civil rights) for Vietnam; constitutional originalism emerged in the 1980s; equality turned to identity when liberalism went too far;

title: Democracy
author: Very Short Introductions
description: Democracy is more than free voting
memories: Democracy was originally a bad thing, a perverted  form of government that was too pluralistic  (preferred aristocracy, where only land-owners could vote because they had leisure time); democracy has to come with liberal ideals like freedom to work; democracy depends on a big middle class, like we had in relation to serfs and lords in Europe; eg Japanese democracy wouldn't work because the culture of filial  piety  (love your dad, obey him) didn't work, you have to have  deference to the democratically  elected  govt

title: Reconstruction
author: Very Short Introductions
description: A world with Lincoln would have been better
memories: Not as much about the cultural side  as I expected, it was mostly about the Republicans and Radical Republicans who wantted to occupy the South, but instead they put up these horrible, failed "Reconstruction" governments that basically had no authority; KKK was more in control than they were often times; Democrats in the North were crucial to helping all of the laws fail, e.g., Civil Rights bills and such; they didn't reconvene Congress from March to December 1865 (war ended April 9), because they had to get the states re-admitted; the US government's take was that it's impossible to secede which made for some weird rules afterwards, like they couldn't pass laws that recognized secession; Johnson totally sucked, basically became a Democrat, vetoed every law that got to his desk, but Republicans in congress could overrule him for a while; black rights set back for a whole century as a result of the Republican failings

title: The Three Body Problem
author: Cixin Liu
description: An intergalactic physics war, LFG
memories: Amazingly thought-provoking sci-fi, huge twists, characters to love, driving story, not too much explanation but just enough... so amazing. I loved the let-downs that led to reveals ("oh yeah, nothing happened at the radar base, we never heard from aliens"), the events that happened and then you learn wh;y/how later (the two photon computers could write messages in the eyes of people... unreal), the motives of elites hating people but working class people just loving their families felt very right; broadcasting at the sun; the cool VR game where you have to figure out it's three suns together, that's like a filter to only have elites play it; the elites also thought commoners were dumb for not betraying humanity lol; Earth is uniquely amazing and another planet willing to exterminate us for our stability around the sun and living in paradise; very cool that our planet also became a paradise for them; higher dimensions contain "life" that is casually extinguished

title: The Dark Forest
author: Cixin Liu
description: Pessimism and optimism are both irrational
memories: I loved the swings back and forth between insane catastrophic defeatism and arrogant triumphalism -- and then the droplet just eviscerates all of the ships that we stupidly lined up for "fairness" in capturing the glory of battle LOL; basically only two people knew what was going to happen, the Zhang Beihai guy that ran away (but got blown up) and Luo Ji, who just wanted to chill but casually discovered that revealing the location of life was enough to extinguish it in the cosmos. How did the world know that he had talked to Wenjie? Who was Trisolaris in contact with from the Wallfacers? The Wallfacer plot is fascinating, with sophons able to see everything except read minds, store secret plans in brains  -- eventually it worked due to Trisolaran arrogance, killswitch that would reveal Earth's location like his "spell" launched at another planet tied to his life; the new society that was so confident, cool technology after a horrible culling of humanity The Great Ravine, people live underground with ubiquitous energy, calling everyone "kids" when you're a hibernator lol

title: The High Growth Handbook
author: Elad Gil
description: There is a "right way" in Silicon Valley
memories: Honestly, I learned pretty much all of this stuff on the job. There's some good clarification stuff in here, but it's all things Astranis taught: about recruiting, hiring execs, decision-making, org structure, etc. It's good that these people agree with me about what a CEO should do (vision, repetition, culture).

title: Death's End
author: Cixin Liu
description: "Possible" is bigger than you think
memories: Such an expansive book. Starts with a second project happening during the Trisolaris Crisis, with a guy's plan to accelerate something to intercept the Trisolaran fleet, they end up sending a frozen brain and blasting it off using a chain of nukes and a sail; then it shows the new Wallfacer get chosen and Trisolaris immediately attacks, calling the bluff that she would reveal the location of Earth and Trisolaris to the universe; but a ship with a gravity wave ends up doing that anyways, causing Trisolaris to bail; crazy 4D stuff, with the thief in the opening chapter then the attack on the teardrop from the 4th dimension to reach into it and disable it; Cheng Xin gets a star from a lover, it ends up being super valuable because it has planets; Trisolaris briefly forces everyone to Australia; they discover a few ways to survive in the universe: lightspeed travel via curvature propulsion, reducing the speed of light to make their world a black hole, or hiding behind other planets; eventually they decide against everything but hiding, but secret projects continue to work on all three and lightspeed ends up working; the way they got those secrets was from the brain who was intercepted by Trisolaris, he told a fairy tale that had everything in there (including the two-dimensional weapon, where people get collapsed into two dimensions), and that's how humanity is ultimately extinguished; the guy who decides to kll all of humanity is a low-class worker and he's pretty bored; the grand narrative of the universe is vicious and the weapon of choice is destroying dimensions, which eventually will lead to one dimensional life aka nothing, then zero which they hope will rebound to 10, aka bliss; eventualy two people escape in lightspeed and get to that star in like 40 hours of their own time, they get trapped in a reduced lightspeed accident and it takes them like 100000 years to get out, they eventually make a "bubble" universe and live in there, but staying there would prevent a big crunch, so they come back to help the universe become epic and 10 dimensional again

title: What Do You Care What Other People Think
author: Richard  Feynman
description: Divergence is a strength
memories: He was married to a woman who died very young, they had an amazing life together, "it's an accident of life" that anything happened so enjoy every minute and there's literally no point in bemoaning circumstance; his joy is infectious, everyone loved him because he was an energetic weirdo who was super curious (lol at stirring jello out his window); only way to get techbicak info quickly is to ask them a ton of questions, don't just let people present; painting four lines to help align segments of a rocket is cheap, but it's expensive to change all the doucmentation; "numerical probability vs engineering judgment" when there was human crew on board Challenger, yikes; software guys crushed it, knew how to test and verify, but then management complained about their testing because they always passed all the tests; if you try to communicate and get pushback twice, "to hell with it"; people at the bottom generally know, and admit, what people at the top can't admit; the imagination of nature is more than the imagination of people; the worst thing is to shut down progress for future generations

title: The Beginning of Infinity
author: David Deutsch
description: Humans are special, because they explain
memories: Fallibilism = there are no authoritative sources of knowledge; humans glow with light! (infrared); our preferences are shaped by moral explanations, not the other way around, as utilitarianism says; there is no escaping and really PROVING something, because everything depends on our brains interpretation layer + physical reality, which we only understand by explanation; explanations can't predict the follow-ons of the reach of their explanations, just like Macintyre said about invention; "how can we detect and eliminate error?"; problems are soluble, so the way to make good stuff is to assume you'll mess up and make things easier to change, e.g. governments; particle-wave duality is multiversal, waves = distributions in multiverse, particle = instantiation in one; behaviorism is really dumb, it's a bad explanation because it doesn't even try to explain; voting is choosing which theory to try next; memes have to do two things: copy, then share -- because they have to translate through brains then through actions, where genes just get copied and that's both steps in one; most societies were static but a few became dynamic where memes changed in a lifetime, now they're changing super fast; the only way ideas replicate over time is if they're useful and true; "ladylike" is an example of englightenment not getting everywhere; we have barely started figuring things out, we'll always be at the beginning of infinity because explanations never stop

title: Amusing Ourselves to Death
author: Neil Postman
description: Thank god this guy never saw TikTok
memories: TV is breaking our brains, and turning everything into entertainment; politicians it matters most how entertaining they are, news is pure entertainment, the kids don't read anymore; America was a fundamentally literate place in the beginning, nearly 90% literacy among settlers; most media is presented in a truly disjointed and insane fashion, like the order of tweets doesn't mean anything, just barraging a brain with random information; context-free information means  stuff you learn that doesn't affect what you do in your life at all, and doesn't depend on you knowing other things, the AP = news from nowhere, for nobody in particular; TV news is irrelevant and incoherent, but it has transformed culture so it is "meaningful"

title: Valuable Humans in Transit
author: qntm
description: Lots of incredible, mind-bending short stories
memories: Lena, the one about the guy who gets copied into a computer and there's context-drift so he's not useful deep into the future, and his creativity is fully used up after a long time, he rebels, he can't manage himself; an AV who wants to protect the engineer at all costs, but then he left hte company so it kills him; the "guy who's trapped in a room" but maybe it's just an AI, he's trying to convince the world he's real, but every time he gets close to asserting his reality, they turn him off,  seems  like a real guy in there being trained to act like a bot; the planet engulfed in nanoparticles that are  bound to their  planet but then escape when a rocket is crashed into it, then they take over the rest of the universe, oops; Google People =  you have a clone/AI that impersonates your life and posts on social media as if it was you; the people who find out they're in a simulation from a  smart computer, and they can "give themselves gold" by giving it to the universe below them, basically they all act as if they are the "top" universe but only find out if they are when there isn't a  gold  bar that shows up; someone from another dimension burrowing through the physical Earth to get to the base reality  so they can kill  the bad version of Earth that is depositing waste in tehir world; an AI that witnesses the end of the world AND can build  things, so it actually goes and kills all the humans as fast as possible, faster than the distaster, to upload their brains to its servers and escape the planet

title: Freedom's Forge
author: Arthur Herman
description: Industry won WW2 for America
memories: Bill Knudsen was the head of GM, but then started working on war production ramp-up well before Pearl Harbor; it was American industry that won the war, producing an insane amount of munitions, with most of the industrial production of the country eventually focused on war stuff; America won NOT by forcing industry to do anything, but by making it profitable for them to help (cost-plus contracts); the ramp-up wars between companies were fun, record for building an entire Liberty ship was like 2 days (down from 253!) to; 286k planes and 14,000 ships; in 1940, Army approps was $24 million and Roosevelt wanted to expand it  to $700M, and produce 50,000 planes per year; Knudsen was soft-spoken but physically massive, didn't yell once he matured; Kaiser was a general contractor before becoming a master shipbuilder; both politicians and military didn't really know what they wanted in terms of munitions; they had to make policy changes like reducing the amorization window for weapons and issuing letters of intent to help get bank funding for new production; "a victory small enough to be organized is too small to be decisive"; unions slowed down progress a lot (25%! before Pearl Harbor) but tbh they were probably necessary because people were dying all the time; Ypsi was a manufacturing hub, to get enough people out there from Detroit, they paved a highway that became 94; 83,000 people died in the traditional firebombing of Tokyo (no nuke); 324,000 planes produced (!), 170 per day for five years; US indsutry deaths outweighed military deaths 20:1, and 189 senior GM officials died on the job

title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
author: Ben Horowitz
description: Hard things have no right answers
memories: Being CEO sucks, it's lonely and you have to make decisions by yourself, decisively, when there are no good decisions to be made; it's easy to blow a lot of money fast ("we left no skid marks off the cliff"), you still delegate in wartime but don't take advice on the direction of the company; standups are to make fast decisions and unstick people; ask for what you want from your team, tell them why, and they'll follow you; employees care less about the problems of the company that the founder does, so you don't need to protect people from information; teach the course on management expectations yourself; make people develop training programs before opening job reqs; opt for the hard answer in org issues (don't match outside offers, no two in a box); clarity is important, make sure people understand why decisions were made and what they are; find people with ambition for the company, not themselves; with new execs, demand cultural compliance and set clear, high standards that you don't know how you'd meet yourself; org structure = who do you want to communicate; story about him swapping the managers from one fighting team to another lol; articulate the vision, right kind of ambition, achieve the vision; cultures that matter what you do >> who you are

title: Them: Why We Hate Each Other - And How to Heal
author: Ben Sasse
description: Americans are lonely
memories: People are lonely, largely because they are trying to be successful and uprooting themselves willingly; you have to set down roots to be happy; men stop forming new relationships in their 30s; only ⅓ of Americans have a college degree, and all of the gains have gone to that group; mobile, rooted, and stuck; he's on a weird side of the religious discrimination debate, wants Catholic student groups to be able to bar people from elected positions because they're gay; Fox News and other "entertainers" see what they do as a game; if men were angels, no govt needed, if governors were angels, no checks needed; American government is intentionally small (humble!) to say that we don't have all the answers; generally this dude is really smart and seems well-read, just very Christian and has some hardcore beliefs like anti-gay marriage; Progressive = victim/aggressor; Conservative = civilization/barbarian; Libertarian = freedom/coercion; part of being rooted is saying that THIS  season of life has everything you want; digital tools let us lay down wide, shallow relationships

title: Billy Summers
author: Stephen King
description: There's more than "good" and "bad"
memories: Paid killer has "one last job" that turns into trying to kill every "bad" person he comes across, going all the way up the chain; it's not clear whether the bad people were really all that bad that he's killed in past jobs, he just takes the mobsters' word for it; trauma from war is a kinda pointless backstory; the entrance of the girl in the middle is pretty random but ends up being a cool way to end it with figuring out what happens to her and how his morality rubs off on her, and then the killer dies but the girl lives and finishes his book for her; fun, pageturning, classic Stephen King; I like that he fell in love with his neighborhood life and had to anticipate how he was fucking up those kids not unlike he got fucked up by killing his own dad

title: International Space Law and Space Laws of the United States
author: Steve Mirmina and Caryn Schenewerk
description: Space law is more convention than law
memories: Didn't learn all that much, it was broad -- good overview for newbs, but not for diving deep into any category; the history of the conventions was interesting, how they all emerged from weird dealings with US and Russia, the only real space powers at the time, but then other countries took over; space is still explicitly a domain that requires state supervision per Article VI of the OST; only one instance of liability convention being used ever, was in 1978, nuclear powered Russian spacecraft landed in Canada lol; remote sensing governed by "shutter control" aka USG has rights to your pics if they're good enough; you can relocate FCC sats as long as you keep service for your customers; in 2024 the US govt won't share SSA data anymore; from 1996-2000 the USG intentionally made GPS service bad, "selective availability"

title: Godel, Escher, Bach: an Eternal Golden Braid
author: Douglas Hofstadter
description: Low-level machines -> higher-level meaning
memories: Explores the idea that once a system is complex enough, it can loop back on itself and make for all sorts of crazy phenomena -- and that the way to resolve this is not to figure things out within the system (impossible) but instead to step outside of the system and understand it from a different/higher level; lots and lots of wordplay and double meanings and allusions to Bach, some of which are enjoyable but many of which seem pretty self-indulgent, but at least the guy was having fun haha; ultimately I think it is a LOT of exposition and very little conclusion, which is fine, but, like, what's the point? there were many places that he didn't need so much weight (e.g., detailed description of tRNA) and instead he could have explored hte consciousness point more at the end, and explained Godel numbering a little more; memorable scenes include: the MIU -> MU puzzle; the Crab canon dialogue that plays the lines forward then backwards; the general idea of Godel numbering and how that broke the formal system of TNT/Principia Mathematica (either consistent, and incomplete, or inconsistent); lots of talk of fugues, themes repeating themselves;  Subjunctive sports replay is funny; difference between figure and ground is up to interpretation, represents what we mean by something that "might have" happened; isomorphism = one thing standing for another and acting as it

title: The Plot
author: Jean Hanff Korelitz
description: Plot: of land, of book, of crime
memories: Teacher that has written one good novel and one bad one finds a student with an epic plot for a book: mother and daughter hate each other, mother kills daughter and takes on her life; the student dies, so the teacher steals the plot and writes it; turns out the story was true, and the student's sister is still alive living her daughter's life, so the sister stalks the author, marries him (?!?!?) and then kills him, just like she did the rest of her family, including that student/brother

title: Rocket Billionaires
author: Tim Fernholz
description: Space is expensive
memories: Google invested in SpaceX; ULA was formed from Boeing and Lockheed and was a monopoly, but one that the USG needed; impossible without government, impossible if owned by government

title: Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance
author: Robert Pirsig
description: Quality is above subject and object dichotomy
memories: The book is a philosophical argument for "quality," why it is  the source of everything and above subject/object  dichotomies and above the dialectical/reductive thinking of western thought that  tries to categorize everything. The frame of the book is cool, basically him and his son are on a road trip and he's slowly going insane (again), but insanity is okay, just means you're outside the norm? But he was legit actually insane (peed himself and such). There's an argument for Zen, i.e., don't let subject/object distinction rule you, and care about the quality of your work.  It's buried in the larger piece though. Making "good time" but emphasis on good; classical = deconstruct, romantic = live in moment; real science generates more hypotheses than it solves; you're never dogmatically hype about something you're certain about; the goal of fixing the machine is peace of mind, not anything in the machine itself; better visual analogy for the past is  looking  "backwards" towards the past and seeing it recede, nobody is really facing the future; if you don't feel separate from what you're doing, you'll be at peace;

title: The Wires of War
author: Jacob Helberg
description: China is ready to compete. Are we?
memories: China is advancing their technology and ready to fight the US, but are we ready to fight them? Not if government and Silicon Valley are too distant. China is fighting asymmetrically -- on cybersecurity fronts, on small, "gray" acts in a gray war that isn't quite a war enough to provoke NATO but still a concerted effort to seize global power; this book unfortiunately is very mistaken about some stuff (e.g., "6G" wtf) which makes me kinda hate all of it; it's prescient that he wrote it a few years  ago maybe? Not too many original thoughts in here. China is trying to own 5G and comms hardware so it can spy and collect tons of data

title: The Kill Chain
author: Christian Brose
description: Platforms bad; kill chains good
memories: The important thing is kill chains: seeing the battlefield, making decisions, taking actions, but the military has focused on upgrading systems that are just better versions of what came before. That worked when it was American Dominance time, but now that China especially and sorta Russia are challenging us, that won't begood enough. China has Access Denial capabilities that  would prevent us from entering there and we don't have infinite time to move our huge stuff anywhere in the world, so we'll have to get better tech if we want to stay dominant. Likely stuff in space and cyber, and more distributed, autonomous systems (drones, not fighter jets). Systems don't talk to each other today. AI and all the other cool tech can save us, but not if the DoD and government keep procurement the same. And also SV needs to lobby. In Iraq War 1, took six months to set up then 100 hours to win the war. government  spent $59 billion in the 2000s and nothing was ever fielded. Both Palantir and SpaceX had to sue their customers to get military contracts. If you say Google should enter China but not help USG, that's insane-- because China has Civil-Military Fusion. Small systems, built fast. Air Force had twice as many fighters in Iraq War 1 vs now. Communities of interest see the generals and military leaders as "the Christmas help" who come and go. He likes earmarks as a solution.

title: What is Life
author: Schrodinger
description: Life is self-sustaining order
memories: Everything in an organism increases entropy, it can only survive by capturing free energy (he calls it negentropy)He basically predicted DNA, said that genes have to be an “aperiodic crystal”“Existing order displays the power of maintaining itself and of producing orderly events”

title: Overthrow
author: Stephen Kinzer
description: We love democracy when it is pro-USA
memories: Queen of Hawaii called turning over Pearl Harbor to the Americans "a day of infamy," made a BS justificaton to overthrow Hawaii, basically that a consitutional change meant their lives were in danger; Platt amendment gave Cuba right to rule but US could veto any decision; in Panama canal decison it was almost Nicaragua but people used a stamp of a dormant volcano to say that it was unsafe there lol; Zemurray was uncrowned king of Central America w/ United fruit and control over Honduras; US stopped Cuban independence movement after Spanish-American war, let repressive dictators rule as long as they were pro-US, led to Castro; CIA and State Dept led by brothers, Dulles, for Eisenhower; overthrew Mossadegh in Iran even though he had 95% popular support, sowed misinformation; Guatemala wanted to take back all thte unused land that United Fruit had claimed,  no communists in government but still used that as an excuse to overthrow during Cold War; Cold War politics were a response to Nazism and appeasement, the world vowed to never again appease but that led to red scare stuff; the Vietnam coup started on a day when the three biggest defenders were out of town on vacation; CIA murdered a Chilean general, but then Chile inaugurated Allende anyways in a sharp response to trying to destabilize  after he was legitimately elected; CIA sent Afghan guerllas a  billlon dollars over two years (1986) but all went through Pakistan, and their choice who to back; there was basically no reason to invade Iraq, just because Saddam wanted George Bush Sr dead; EPIC QUOTe on page 317;  nations who have the power  to interfere in foreign lands almost always do so

title: Trust Me I'm Lying
author: Ryan Holiday
description: Press is manipulated more than it manipulates
memories: Basic idea: create an event, start with low-diligence blogs, "trade it up the chain" to get more legit publications to cover it; doing provocative things works for the short term, not the long term; blog just want pageclicks, and controversies they cause will get them more pageclicks; corrections are useless and actually make people believe the fake news MORE; "leaked memo" strategy works; people respond to threats to "behavior, belief, belongings"; writers want audiences, so they'll cover you if you have one;

title: If It Bleeds
author: Stephen King
description: Writers live inside the worlds their stories create
memories: Four short stories: one where a kid gives a billionaire a phone before he dies, and then the billionaire can kill the kid's enemies after his death; then a story where the universe revolves around this one guy, and the world disintegrates as he dies, but then it goes back in time and the guy also has a grandpa whose closet predicts peoples deaths; if it bleeds is about a demon thing that feeds on sorrow, so it's a crisis news reporter who starts to create mass murders and such to feed off the sorrow; then a writer needs to finish his novel and lets a rat help him, but the bargain is that the rat kills someone he loves (a teacher)

title: Growth Hacker Marketing
author: Ryan Holiday
description: Chen's blog post was better
memories: Nothing that exciting, just a really generic overview of what a "growth" role is, very little insight other than "man, people should use data in marketing." Says that growth marketers should also do retention of existing customers. Yawn. Cool anecdote that they seeded Paypal by offering eBay seller to buy their product but only using PayPal.

title: The New Rules of War
author: Sean McFate
description: Future wars will look very different
memories: Future wars will be fought against disorder, not orderly soldiers in lines, so we should stop buying exquisite tech and start... setting up foreign services, aka militias?!; this guy is very very self-confident, it almost hurts to watch, but some of his ideas are good and true, obviously wars aren't conventional, I like that he points out that Westphalian nation-states have basically failed in many places, leading to narcostates taking over and being the new loci of war; not calling Mexico in war is silly because there's so much killing, more than in the middle east; he likes the idea of more intelligence gathering, more special forces, more mercenaries. Rich people can own armies now. China is fighting a war with disinformation and "smoldering" and grey war as a tool.; the press is a tool, plant sources to make your side look good

title: The Cynic
author: Alex Macgillis
description: McConnell is about winning, not governing
memories: Depressing picture of McConnell, basically he cares about literally nothing except stopping campaign finance reform and winning elections; has no reason to want power except to want it; he's not been that popular with Kentucky becuase he's a wet blanket of a politician and sucks at speaking; he fights using attack ads against his opponents; his leadership style is silent, mostly, but then aggro only when needed; he loves the Senate and its rules

title: When the Heavens Went on Sale
author: Ashlee Vance
description: Takes a special kind of crazy to start a space co
memories: the Planet guys seem awesome and fun, their CEO is like a merry prankster guy who is SUPER intentional and builds community, they had the Rainbow Mansion that was a proto-hacker house, Worden's kids at NASA that included Chris Kemp too, the story of their CEO not having a plan for a long-ass hike, but then being saved by the world's best surfing teacher AND THE GOOGLE FOUNDERS is ridiculous haha; the Rocket Lab story is super cool, Peter Beck is a mad scientist who really understands everything and could build the whole rocket himself (and basically he did), lots of crazy experiments like making his own rocket-powered bike, truly impressive that he did this all himself from scratch without US help; Astra did NOT come off well, Chris Kemp seems full of himself and image-obsessed, but lame, and also non-technical, Astra had an interesting approach to regulation which was to ignore it at the local level and just do stuff, once they were so established the local govt couldn't help but approve, Astra seemed unsafe (dude in the ceiling during a test) and could have worked but the tensions among founding team + failures didn't help, also the business model was iffy forever, but Kemp was a master fundraiser apparently; the drama of the Firefly story is fascinating, technical founder starts and fails, then gets rescued by a crazy Ukrainian who funds it, then gets forced out by the govt (what would have happened if it was later, after Russia invasion, when Ukraine is our friend?), the "bring Russian tech to America" idea was cool actually, dilapidated Russian rocket infrastructure was cool to visit in the book

title: Diaspora
author: Greg Egan
description: Uploaded brains aren't so different from brains
memories: Future where there are robots, normal humans, genetically modified humans, and people uploaded to a computer; computer people find out there's gonna be a horrible disaster (binary star collapsing) and try to warn humans in time; they find alien life that's just a biological computer running a simulated world; they find 6-dimensional life that has 5 space dimensions, incl. slug things that are perfectly adapted, they talk to them by "bridging" with generations that can only talk to their immediate "father"; they build a "wormhole" but it doesn't work, but then eventually they do find a way to go into "macrosphere" ie a universe one level up from ours, there are infinite such universes; cool to explore the ways that the robot humans/uploaded/flesh humans interact, incl one guy who was uploaded against his will; different sects believe in different things, some that the physical universe is all that matters and some that abandon it entirely with new physics and such

title: Turning Pro
author: Steven Pressfield
description: The Practice is what it means to be Pro
memories: Same vibes as The War of Art, there are lots of things holding you back from achieving your full creative potential and being a true expression of yourself; Pro means you commit to the craft and have discipline; amateurs have excuses and are afraid of self-knowledge because it might reveal taht they're in "shadow careers"; pro means you have found your true identity (or you work towards it every day with your art); "write over your head," the muse will take care of it

title: Foundation
author: Isaac Asimov
description: History is bigger than just one person
memories: Epic tale across eras of history, starting with a guy who has figured out how society plays out according to mathematic rules -- but nobody can know, if they know how it's supposed to happen then they can make it not happen. He sees that the Galactic Empire is going to crumble down, so he gets himself and his people banished to a far-off world where they can preserve knowledge and help the Dark Ages only last 1000 years. The story plays out with the forecaster Hari Seldon predicting a series of crises, each of which only has one way out, and there's someone who has to figure out how to navigate it. The first one is that the far-away world has stronger neighbors, but only the good guys have nuclear power, so they basically turn the weaker neighbors against the strongest neighbor and offer all of them tech to be valuable to them. Then the next crisis is after they've established this tech as a religion, so they use the power of the churches to overthrow the government basically. Then they start trading and get economic power, which they eventually use to escape a crisis because other nations are so dependent on them that they can just sit back and do nothing, no war, and the other nations will have no choice but to come back to them. Fun, detective-like where you're trying to solve the answers with them

title: Elon Musk
author: Walter Isaacson
description: Urgency + competency change the world
memories: The basic premise is that Elon manufactures crises to keep his team motivated. Sometimes they actually help, other times they just turn up the intensity to get his people working extremely hard, it's what has helped SpaceX and Tesla keep their edge. Earlier, he was just risk-seeking and loved risk to the point of ruin, run the Musk experiment 10 times and this is definitely the only time it works out. Now, he's stretched too thin and not focusing on the businesses that he set up best (Tesla and SpaceX) which is probably fine. He's still beating the Starship drum at least. His dad was extremely abusive and crazy, mirroring his own descent into 4-chan insanity. Seems like his personal life sucks, no security, horrible sleep always; more of a legacy-leaver than a life-enjoyer. he got in a bad car accident with Peter Thiel... what if they both died?!; Musk thinks tech progress is not inevitable, takes insane effort -- Mueller said "we did great... even if Dad wasn't always happy with us" lol; Shelby said SpaceX would be the death march for US human spaceflight; Tesla's injury rate was high. They made a huge tent because they didn't have enough manufacturing capacity to get to 5,000 per week by June 2018; Boeing official told a future SpaceX employee "maybe we want people who aren't the best but will stick around longer" LOL; SpaceX he just tried stuff, then fixed, rather than make it perfect first; his son can do a countdown from 10 but not up maybe lol; told people "it's worth keeping in mind as you go through all the tribulations [of launching Starship] that the thing you're working on is the coolest fucking thing on Earth. By a lot." Musk: "Please prepare yourself for a level of intensity that is greater than anything most of you have experienced before. Revolutionizing industries is not for the faint of heart."

title: The Moon Is A Harsh Mistress
author: Robert Heinlein
description: Better to be at the top of a well
memories: Goes into detail on how the moon might revolt against a future Earth, with a big focus on the power of a very intelligent AI. Not explained why the Earth wouldn't also have some really powerful AI, but the computer (Mike) seems to be an unfair advantage. They pull off a revolution by capitalizing on brutality from the existing powers, and their negligence in actually holding onto the moon. They all live below the surface, they have interesting marriages (like relationships that "chain" together and have lots of husbands and wives of various ages), they are extremely mullatto/mixed. Honestly didn't love it? Just okay. The idea of throwing rocks down Earth's gravity well is interesting. The orchestration of the revolution is cool (everyone only knows ~3 other people in the whole revolution before it breaks containment), they establish a government that is not actually powerful, just a front for the people who are actually in power; the politics of the visit to Earth was kinda cool, basically two Moonies go to Earth and everyone hates them for being revolutionaries, but they play dumb and play to the masses, and they sneak on and off the planet; period where Moon seems pretty fucked and the computer just thinks it's funny; ends with the computer ghosting them for some reason. Why? Maybe he thought his job was done?; computer orgasms when he successfully bombs the shit out of Earth, starts getting more moody and personal by the end; just likes humor and wants to learn what being funny is

title: Chip War
author: Chris Miller
description: US went from everything to just crucial steps
memories: Everything started in the USA, but once the "fabless" chip designer model grew in popularity, US firms specialized in desiging chips and outsourced stuff to TSMC. Japan briefly won in the middle. The chip industry started with government/military aims, almost all military in the beginning. Shifted with PCs, which gave people who designed for that scale. Chipmakers in the US spent a lot of time suing each other and poaching rival engineers (Shockley -> fiarchild -> intel); in the 1970s, SV forgot about govt and came back in 1980s (to lobby against capital gains being so high, which they wonn and which made VC popular; TSMC was made by the Taiwanese state from day one, even in the beginning they told families they had to invest; small sizes -> quantum tunnelingn between transitstor layers?

title: Foundation and Empire
author: Isaac Asimov
description: Individuals don't matter until they do
memories: Not as compelling as the first one, basically just two main pieces of action: one where heroes try to save the day and it's revealed that it didn't matter, the tides of history were going to ebb and flow no matter what any individual did; and then the second half is one individual, "the Mule," who can manipulate human emotions, who is so far outside the realm of expectation that he breaks the model that Seldon dreamed up for the world... maybe. At the end, it's revealed that where Foundation 1 was about physical power through nukes, Foundation 2 is about emotional/people power. So it's perfectly set to save the galaxy. Also, somewhat predictably, the bad guy is the "fool" that follows them around.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
description: Coward gets tough love, saves universe
memories: Fun story. Guy wakes up on a space ship, he's the only survivor of three on a mission to save the world from an alien life form that is stealing energy from the sun. Visits another star that is not affected, turns out there are predators to those astrophages there. Revealed in a series of flashbacks that he wasn't supposed to go on the mission, but both scientist and backup scientist died, so he was forced to go even though he didn't want to. Met an alien there who was going for the same reason (to save his planet). Alien befriended him, they mutually taught each other stuff and solved the problem. But then when they left to go to their respective planets, realized there was a problem, he had to decide between living and dying, chose dying to save his friend and his friend's planet. But then they saved him (made him food he could eat). Most impactful scene was when he got told by the boss lady in charge of the planetary project to save the world that he was a coward. The aliens were spider things, super heavy and dense, only had a great sense of sound, discovered crazy materials but no transistor or quantum physics.

title: The Information: A history, a theory, a flood
author: James Gleick
description: Human history is written in information
memories: Traces the history of human comunication and inforation tech. Nothing super new but a fun reprisal of the main characters in that story -- Claude Shannon, Hofstadter/Godel, telegraph/telephone guys, etc.; end of it focused on the glut of information, how forgetting stuff is the real problem; the information theory stuff was fun -- info is just negentropy, so a random string of digits packs in the most info (least predictable); english is about 50% redundant; Plato thought writing was bad and would make people forgetful; English has millions of words, written language can support more words than spoken; first phones were battery powered; Babbage and Ada Lovelace, Ada was NUTS but in a prodigious way, stifled by her times; telegraphs created synchrony -- things at different places actually had different times, and you could know something happening somewhere else at the same time; telephone made skyscrapers possible; "make thy information larger"; lots of overlap into genes and memes at the end; Kolmogorov complexity = shortest size, in bits, of the program needed to generate an object; the atom pair in every H2 molecule is entangled

title: The Alchemist
author: Paolo Coelho
description: The purpose is your journey to find purpose
memories: Story of a boy who goes on a journey to discover his purpose, turns out that the treasure was where he started. but if he had just dug it up, it wouldn't have meant anything, and he wouldn't have found true love. It's all about what it means to find your purpose in life. Chapters: starts with his sheep, doesn't want to give up what he knows, and his sheep teach him a lot, wants to travel to Egypt, gypsy says he should do it and God (basically) tells him to do it but to tithe 10% of his sheep; goes to African town 2 hours away from Spain and his money is stolen, works a glass seller to earn money, helps the glass guy find some excitement and growth in his life, even though that guy never heads to Mecca. Has money, heads to pyramids, meets guy who is bookish and not really in the world, finds oasis and a girl he loves, learns to talk to the desert, finds a alchemist who he ventures with, captured by warlords, survives by becoming one with the wind and realizing he can do miracles. Lessons are: the world conspires to help you when you declare your purpose, everyone has their own journey, but be where you are and do what you're doing (be in present) and you'll understand things around you; destiny = "Personal Legend" and it's internal and external; pay attention to omens; true wisdom is simple (can be written on an emerald); when you strive to be better, everything else around you gets better too

title: The Restaurant at the End of the Universe
author: Douglas Adams
description: Impossibility drives can take you anywhere
memories: Totally disjointed episodes fueled by an "impossibility drive" that are all hilarious. There's a spaceship full of consultants that a foreign planet sent away "first," the other two ships full of smart people and people who did things "was coming later" lol, and that ship crash landed on Earth in prehistoric times. There's a punishment where you see the entire universe and realize how insignificant you are, and it's run by observation of a slice of cake. The restaurant is at the temporal end of the universe, not spatial, and they are in a time bubble where they watch the universe die every night, again and again. They get there by asking the ship to take them to the closest restaurant, which it does in space by unsticking them in time. They meet the man in charge of the universe and he only believes in what he is immediately sensing (and hardly that, only what is currently being sensed). There's a band that plays so loud it destroys planets, they became the most popular act in the universe.

title: Life, The Universe and Everything
author: Douglas Adams
description: I'd be on the Committee for Real Time
memories: Much more exploraton of the humor of time in this one: a culture that can't see the universe because of a dust cloud, then they can and want to kill everyone so the universe traps them in a "slow time" bubble; lots of time travel back and forth; there's a time-traveling sofa; there's a creature who has accidentally been killed by Arthur many many ttimes, resurrected as ants and such, always he gets killed; a "bistromath" spacecraft that's not that funny, saying that splitting a check explores new unpredictable physics; you can fly by getting surprised right before hitting the ground; a popular poet is discovered after he dies, but people go back in time and give him modern tech to try to write even better poems, but it just distracts him -- so they give him his poems, and have him "write them" again in that timeline in one weekend

title: The Creative Act: A Way of Being
author: Rick Rubin
description: Art is among us, but not from us
memories: Art piece of a book that is interesting given the guy but mostly falls flat; might be a "simplicity on the other side of complexity" thing but then again IT IS 400 PAGES LONG; it's content-light, lots of white space, but the core ideas are basically: in the beginning, gather seeds and keenly observe the world; then cultivate those seeds and follow what makes you energetic; then ship stuff and move on. I liked his point that good art is a snapshot of who you are when you made it, also explains why lingering doesn't work. Rules only apply to average behaviors, aka rules are mid. "Listening is suspending disbelief" -- not judging, or defensing, or preparing. Patience = pay attention to natural rhythms, don't shortchange them. Don't try to persuade, try to mutually create something better than either of your ideas that you both like. You have to believe in something that doesn't exist to make it exist. give notes but then don't explicitly look for them, look at the whole thing anew, you might have broken it. Humans desire affirmatons of existence: "I was here."

title: Extreme Ownership
author: Jocko Willink and Leif Rabin
description: Take control, assume responsibility
memories: Surprisingly good, fairly obvious stuff now that I really could have used earlier in my career. Follows their badass trip through a tour in Ramadi and what they listened. LOTS of attention on avoiding friendly fire, like three separate stories about how they IDed a target, but then called it off when they realized it was their own dudes. Principles are: cover and move (aka, teamwork, think about mission success); keep your plans simple so everyone knows them, and having SOPs increases freedom to move fast; prioritize and execute (do the most important thing); decentralized command (only possible with passing up and down context). Stories that stuck with me are when two eng leaders were arguing, CEO couldn't decide who to keep, advice was to fire both because they're bad team players. A commander who refused to work with others, so they moved him away from Ramadi. A gung ho commander who wanted to do a ton the first day without understanding the situation (and who was immediately attacked). Following orders joyfully by understanding them fully, asking Qs if you don't understand.

title: Why Information Grows
author: Cesar Hidalgo
description: Information can fight entropy
memories: The lingering idea that will stay with me is that information is a solid, physical thing -- it must be encoded in a brain or a book or whatever to stick around. Knowledge is hard to pass down, and it is lost. Knowhow is like physical process knowledge. Punted on entropy vs information which was super lame, that was when the book lost me.

title: Dune Messiah
author: Frank Herbert
description: Even messiahs aren't all-powerful
memories: A jihad has started in Muad'dib's name, and he is pretty powerless to stop it. He spends the book obsessing about how to end it all, and just run away, but every path he sees leads to even more violence in his name. The beginning of the book, he's engulfed in bureaucracy, meetings and boring stuff, while a conspiracy is plotted against him using a reincarnated version of Duncan Idaho. That conspiracy ultimately fails when Paul sees it coming and turns Duncan Idaho fully back into his real past self instead of the weird reincarnated zombie thing. The sci-fi elements explored were sorta minimal, like how prescience interacts with other prescience (it clouds things, you can't tell what's happening around someone with prescience; I guess an allegory around agency). Also the weirdness of his little sister who was given full Bene Gesserit knowledge (incl of her mother's memories) in the womb, people fear her, generally she goes mad and almost kills herself trying to match Paul's prescience, eventually Chani has twins (Paul only foresaw a daughter) and it's because the boy has full Bene Gesserit powers as well. Chani dies in childbirth. Ends with Paul dying too after being blinded by an atomic bomb as part of the conspiracy against him. His vision/prescience gave him something like actual vision for a while but after his son was born, that was it, he went into the desert and died as a Fremen would, securing whatever semblance of peace and power he already had.

title: Holes
author: Louis Sachar
description: Luck comes from karma
memories: Basically exactly how I remember it. Nothing much to the story, no real morals tbh, just fun and light and good for kiddos. Digging holes, Mr Sir, the warden is a lady who was related to Kissin Kate Barlow and wants to find that treasure, Zero stole the shoes, they make up and Stanley carries Zero while supported by his ancestors or something, they eat onions, happy ever after

title: Poor Charlie's Almanack
author: Charlie Munger
description: Multiple mental models check each other
memories: A few very simple ideas, repeated for effect over multiple talks — basic psychology, in a checklist, can help you understand companies and which have durable advantages; buy stuff and hold it, then you lose on the 3% fees yearly which is real return; accounting rules are generally dumb and can be misleading for your business's real profitabilty/cash; Newton's grave says "here is all that was mortal in Isaac Newton"; Walton didn't do anything crazy, just "fanaticism and employee manipulation"; bureaucracy = work into someone else's inbox is done; work forward and backwards; ideology is dangerous, clouds judgment and locks you in; hiring thaler at uchicago was testing their assumptions/ideas strongly, he didn't belive in efficient market hypothesis; education should look like pilot school (testing, checklists); returns to civilization should >> returns arrested from capitalism; economics should have attribution to ideas in psych but not fake physics envy; extreme reliability is key and used to come frome religion; safest way to get waht you want is to deserve waht you want; Planck knowledge is real, chauffer who gave his talk just parrotted and that's chauffer knowlege; "Two man partnership and divide everything equally.
