And whenever we're behind on our commitments to other people, we will both work 14 hours a day, seven days a week, until we're caught up."; incentives are everything, but that means humans are great at faking compliance or performance; Serpico Syndrome = social proof plus incentives, i.e., being in the mob; academic admin is keeping the people who do no work away from the people who actually do work

title: Open
author: Andre Agassi
description: "Yeah, I did meth and lied about it"
memories: Wild autobiography, dude hates tennis and always did despite being awesome. Dad set him up to be a star, drilled him as a tiny child, turned into a child prodigy but then sent him away to a camp for tennis to basically drop out of school and win. Lots of highs and lows in his career, many tied to personal stuff -- couldn't get his relationships and personal stuff right, which hurt him on the court. Fascinating how those parallels were so clear, tennis being a very mental game makes that make sense. Also battled injuries of course, his version of tennis seems uniquely grindy/whoever gets hurt first loses. Admits he did meth. Had a core crew of dudes who were hugely important to him, incl his trainer, a pastor guy, and his brother.

title: Amp it Up
author: Frank Slootman
description: Pushing people hard can work
memories: Basic idea is that execution > strategy, but he still spends a weird section on strategy? and his execution tips are "focus on execution??" surprisingly little insight for this being a touted book. Get people focused. Ask more of them. Push for speed. Let people cook. Hire career-frustrated people. Articulate exactly why you exist and what you're trying to accomplish. Focus on the problem, not people's proposed solutions.

title: Needful Things
author: Stephen King
description: Greed isn't good
memories: A pawn shop where the owner hypnotizes people and makes people REALLY want the things, pulling from their deepest desires/insecurities/traumas (e.g., two sad housewives buy stuff that lets them hallucinate sex with Elvis, two guys get things that remind them of their fathers, etc.). People buy them for wahtever is in their wallet plus a "favor" but the favors are all pranks that set off other people in town. Eventually, just about everyone in town kills each other, including a bareknuckle brawl between the Catholics and Baptists. Lots of individually sad stories, like a kid who got a baseball card but then pranked a woman who died, so he killed himself. Overall very dark, but very fun to read and a gripping story. Not a great ending, the bad guy goes on to another town to repeat it.

title: Crypto Confidential
author: Nat Eliason
description: Crypto gambling was an inside job
memories: Guy got involved in crypto ponzi scheme/pump and dump stuff, made a bunch of money but almost lost it a bunch of times, including once by having his private key on Github; he was a beginner programmer and that was good enough to copy/paste the code of other people; his friend ended up being the anonymous CEO he worked for

title: The Art of Doing Science and Engineering
author: Richard Hamming
description: You need a vision of the future to go far
memories: Figure out where tech is heading, and go there. Best way is to understand the deep fundamentals, give yourself lots of "hooks" to call upon your knowledge, and just care enough to be ready to seize good luck when it comes. In computers, if the payoff isn't now, it's never. Things change too fast. You can calmly extend things and work within a system, but breakthroughs only come from frustration and emotional involvement. Past missile sims were harder to do with computing power, so you could only do some -- meaning people cared more about fully understanding every run, which was better for intuitive understanding.  Human knowledge is like lifting two 100 pound weights rather than 1 200 pound weight. experts usually say something is impossible right up until it's done. If you ignore all crackpots, you miss the new theory. If you work with your door closed, you might be productive but always on slightly the wrong thing.

title: Where is My Flying Car?
author: J Storrs Hall
description: Capture energy, freely create = progress
memories: Asimov says every tech change has been resisted; major quality of life improvements all came before 1960 when DOE formed and energy flatlined; health care, education, ESR all signal rather than do anything; highway system led to ⅓ of all growth in '50s; 1970 was regulatory explosion under Nixon; four levels of wealth: barefoot (1B), bike and shoes (3B), motorbike (2B), car (1B); more efficient energy use leads to more energy use, and green movement being anti wealth increases kid and therefore bad environmental outcomes; energy poverty kills 28k people per year from cold in US;

title: You Like it Darker
author: Stephen King
description: Being a horror author would be terrible
memories: Two guys save an alien, get gifted a lack of artistic inhibition so they become famous; guy tells his life story to a stranger (he's an alcoholic, it's the fifth step of AA) then kills him; weird kid talks to his grandpa who seems to be making up stuff, turns out the grandpa is a being who never dies and steals lives, so the stories are all real, he steals the kid's life; guy dreams he knows where a body is buried, reports it, they think he did it, crazed cop goes insane thinking it's him, protagonist has a second vision that saves his life from the cop killing him; unlucky kid gets accidentally kidnapped, maybe it was all a dream though; grandpa wants to take a shortcut, family does, they find bad guys there, grandpa saves the day and dad is paralyzed and useless; aliens are on earth and passively in people but activated by a red screen; people terrified of planes prevent them from going down; creepy old lady thinks her dead kids are alive, they sorta are, dude has to burn them to escape; evil scientist unlocks portal to another dimension by pulling up the floor in a dream; guys meets a dude who can answer any question, becomes a hero, that's it

title: Going Infinite
author: Michael Lewis
description: Lewis got there too late
memories: SBF was autistic and unable to feel anything; also truly gave no fucks about where any money was, threw it around without caring which is deeply ironic given the whole EA thing which should imply deliberate behavior with money... it was the whole point in the beginning; the narrative has a huge gap in the time leading up to the SBF vs CZ thing, not enough buildup of that storyline, likely because the author got to the Bahamas too late to really understand what was happening; all of the code was apparently written by one dude; I didn't know China cracked down on CZ; the Jane Street stuff is interesting and it does seem like the arbitrage/market maker stuff for crypto was a genuinely good idea, if sketchy in the details; he paid an insane amount of money to celebrities to have them shill for him; the original loan from Jaan Tallinn to SBF had 50% interest!

title: The Origins of Consciousness in the Breakdown of the Bicameral Mind
author: Julian Jaynes
description: Everybody used to be schizo
memories: Mind-bending. I thought it was terrible in the beginning because the guy spends at least 20% of the book making wild assertions about how this whole thesis could exist and what it would mean for the brain, without evidence. But then he gets into the history and that's actually really interesting, about what shows up in paintings and such. The basic arguments are 1) consciousness is metaphor, you represent the external world through metaphor in your internal world; 2) previously, people were not conscious and everyone simply had auditory hallucinations from "God" that actually came from their right hemispheres; 3) eventually, that broke down, and people became conscious as they had voices in their head. The coolest evidence is the comparison of the Iliad (earlier, pre-conscious) to the Odyssey (later, post-conscious). The former has the Gods saying things and people doing them, the latter has lots more recognizably human drama. Guy named Wallace co-discovered evolution but then was dismissed because he thought hetere were major discontinuities in the evolution of species; abstract words are previously tangible metaphors whose metaphors have worn away (amsi = breathe = am/is); lots of evidence of shrines to dead people, or civilizations with central thing being a statue of an old dead leader; consciousness might start by inferring other people are doing things for reasons; in the Odyssey there are disguises and deceit; prayer is similar to hypnotism;

title: Red Rising
author: Pierce Brown
description: What if classes were permanent?
memories: Genuinely surprising turns at many points in the story — starts with a guy competing and winning, but then the powers that be deny him of his just rewards. He thinks he's helping mine Helium-3 for a future colonization of Mars, but his girlfriend reveals that Mars is already fully colonized and they're being enslaved in the dark. She sings a forbidden song and commits suicide, he gets sad and commits a similar suicide, but then wakes up and is enlisted in a terrorist army that wants to overthrow the society. You learn about all the colors, golds on top, reds on bottom, in a massive specialized pyramid. Darrow gets turned into a gold, and goes to their ruthless version of Hunger Games Hogwarts. Lots of interesting warfare at the school, you start with 1:1 combat to the death and he kills someone from a prominent family, then befriends his brother Cassius (lots of drama later). Many interesting characters introduced: Pax, the friendly giant; Mustang the girlboss (part of a rival powerful family), Sevro (goblin kid who mostly hides away from battle), and the Jackal who was a cannibal to survive, Darrow makes him chop off his hand, etc. Turns out there's another undercover Red there who ends up being too bitter and evil, rapes people. Lots of fun medieval-like combat. Turns out the people in charge of each house are messing with the game, the final move Darrow makes to win is to raid the place where they are watching and kills the most evil one lol. He is the leader of the winning house so he gets the pick of best internship and chooses the house of his girlfriend, Mustang.

title: Holly
author: Stephen King
description: Old people antagonists are not interesting
memories: Pretty lame book. You learn quickly that there are old people who eat young people to try to stay young. Then, predictably, the detective finds them and kills them with her bare hands because they're weak old people. She's still trapped in a cage, though, so there's this whole other plot about a young woman who is related to her friend, and the young woman solves the entire case by herself in like 24 hours to realize where Holly is and save her. Dumb

title: Golden Son
author: Pierce Brown
description: And then... (you'll never guess...) A TWIST!
memories: This sequel greatly annoyed me. It's just twist after twist that is completely unpredictable but also seemingly unnecessary — starts with Darrow losing in The Academy But This Time In Space, then finds out Eo was pregnant and plans to bomb a Gala, instead he challenges Cassius to a duel and is going to win, but then a huge battle breaks out, House Augustus barely survives and has Lysander as a captive, but then Tactus defects and steals their only piece of leverage, so they storm the flagship and take it, there's a memorable scene where he basically forces Lorn au Arcos to join him (and lets Aja escape?), they storm Mars and almost die under the wall, but Darrow storms after the escaping Sovereign and gets captured, would die if not for Ares revealing himself as Fitchner! At a victory feast, Darrow is betrayed because people know he's Red and both Fitchner and Augustus are killed. I had to look a lot of that up. It's not that memorable.

title: Morning Star
author: Pierce Brown
description: Staying true to ideals while conquering is hard
memories: Darrow gets captured by the Jackal and tortured for a whole year, shoved into a tiny box and barely kept alive, turns out inside the board room table; gets saved, Sevro is now Ares and there's drama about him just being a fighter and impostor syndrome basically, needing to be ruthless, assuming he's a terrorist on a suicide mission; they recruit the outer moon lords and the Obsidian fighters on the south pole (those scenes are nuts, they have to kill the mom and it's the daughter that eventually does it, only after raiding the Gold "gods" and stealing their weapons in a show of force; they get fake-captured by Cassius who fake-kills Sevro to get into the inner sanctum, they kill everyone, the Jackal detonates a few nukes, Jackal is killed, Mustang becomes sovereign

title: Second Foundation
author: Isaac Asimov
description: Predicting the future is easier if you rig it
memories: Two halves: one where the Mule searches for the Second Foundation, and one where the Foundation searches for them. The Mule sends two guys (one converted loyalist and one unconverted, suspected 2nd Foundationer) to try to find the Second Foundation. They go to a seemingly random planet, but then the classic "gotcha, no I got you, no I GOT YOU" thing happens: the Mule followed them there, assuming the Second Foundation guy would lead them to the real place, he destroys a big planet near them; but then ultimately the Second Foundation guy gets alone in the room with the Mule and changes his brain, saving everyone; the second half, a band of scientists from the Foundation is searching, they send a guy to the library on Kaldan to see if they can find clues as to where the 2F is, and at the end there are tons of fake reveals: that the 2F doesn't exist (it does), that it's on Kalgan (it's not). The scientists invent a jammer for 2F brain reading basically, identify the mole on their team, and he "confesses" that they're right about 25 people on the Foundation's planet being the entire 2F, but it's not. It was all a plan. Turns out we met the leader of the 2F on Trantor with the little stowaway girl, the 2F had people eevveryywhere

title: Energy and Civilization
author: Vaclav Smil
description: More energy, more civilization
memories: Just finished reading “Energy and Civilization” by Vaclav Smil.It lives up to the hype — it’s a truly comprehensive look at human history through the lens of energy.Here are my (messy) notes from probably the closest thing you’ll find to a “first principles of energy”:‣ The average person consumes 8-11 MJ of food per day, and sustained power provided by a human is capped at around 100 W‣ Lizot (1977) found two nearby Amazonian tribes, one normal and one whose lazy men who took drugs all day. The lazy tribe consumed less than half of the animal protein that its neighbors did.‣ Traditional farming took an insane amount of labor, 94-98% of available labor in China (!) and 120% of available labor in India (!!) during peak summer months‣ An ox-drawn plow reduces the time needed to prep a hectare of land from 100-200 hours to 30 hours‣ Massive projects (e.g., aqueducts) took an objectively irrational amount of labor to complete, meaning no individual person who worked on it could expect to get more energy back than the extra energy they spent to build it.‣ Horses are way better than oxen, generally: in the 1800s, ox-empowered farmers needed 150-170 hours to harvest wheat; in 1900, horses + other advances reduced that to just 9 hours.‣ It was around the 1890s that human muscle power became effectively worthless (1:100 ratio in American farms of that time, human:animal power)‣ Traditional agriculture couldn’t produce enough to eliminate malnutrition, limited by nitrogen recycling, there would be massive famines‣ The original treadmills were literally like stairstepper machines that were installed in English prisons as punishment to power a mill. Tread mill.‣ Different types of fuel (hardwood, charcoal, dung) had different energy content (MJ/kg) depending on their carbon and oxygen (etc.) content‣ The average fuel consumption of an individual in the Roman empire was ~1.8kg of wood per day, or 1.5 GJ, which is about 1 tankful of gasoline (!)‣ The rollout of the steam engine actually increased the deployment of horses, because it made people richer and increased overall shipment — once at the end of the route, you’d still need horses to get stuff from the tracks to your house/store‣ Modern bikes were invented around the same time as cars‣ The total potential energy of the Great Pyramid is 2.5 TJ‣ Coal is goated, dominated Britain for 250 years — >75% of all energy from 1620 to about 1960‣ Chinese percussive drilling managed to create a 1 km deep borehole in 1835‣ Smil loves Edison, thinks his end-to-end innovation of the entire system made him deserve his notoriety‣ First massive deployments of electricity were all for lighting‣ The first-ever small DC electric motor in 1896 was invented by Tesla and intended to be mounted on a stylus to drive a stencil-making pen, to duplicate monuments (!) — shoutout to Monument Labs haha‣ In 1800, the world consumed 20 EJ of energy, by 1900 it had doubled to 43 EJ, mostly from coal‣ The goal of 99.9999% reliability for the US grid is ridiculous, it’s currently at 99.98%‣ We are getting much more efficient in our use of fossil fuels over time, supply up 14x from 1900-2000, but useful energy up 30x‣ Liebig’s law of the minimum: the nutrient in the shortest supply will determine the yield‣ One furnace in South Korea produces 17,000 tons per day‣ America’s GDP doubled from 1880 to 1900, thanks to fossil fuels‣ China laid down 4.9 gigatons of concrete from 2008-2010, as much as America during the entire 20th century‣ Infant morality falls off quickly with increasing per capita energy, up to about 110 GJ/capita, where it levels off‣ The WW2 ramp in production was insane, from 300 planes in April 1917, then a $12 billion appropriation for 22,500 engines‣ One week’s worth of energy use in the US is equivalent to 1 year of avg energy consumption in Nigeria and 2 years in Uganda

title: So Long, And Thanks For All The Fish
author: Douglas Adams
description: More Adams absurdity
memories: Classic Hitchhiker book, except it's almost all on Earth. Arthur finds himself back on Earth, it turns out that the dolphins saved humanity right before the planet got destroyed in book one by retreating into another dimension or something. To the people of Earth, it seemed like the dolphins just disappeared one day. There's a woman who also knows something is awry, they meet and fall in love. There's a Rain God who's pissed about it always raining on him, but he gets rich and famous by the end. They fly on Earth, same way as in previous books. There's a guy who has an inside-out house, he says the rest of the world lives "inside" his "asylum," after being convinced that the rest of the world was insane for having seen a printed set of instructions for using a toothpick. The book is more about a fun way of writing than anything that happened; very hard to capture his absurdist style in a recap. Scientists must see things first, then think later.

title: Structures, or Why Things Don't Fall Down
author: J.E Gordon
description: Key to any structure: load lines
memories: Just finished reading "Structures: or why things don't fall down" by J.E. Gordon.It's a first-principles buildup of how to build things: from archways & bridges, to skyscrapers & cathedralsHere are my (unedited) notes:▸ elasticity is all about deflection: if you climb a tower, the tower becomes shorter▸ recovering fully after a deflection is "elastic"; staying bent out of shape is "plastic" deformation▸ "stress" is how hard the atoms in a material are being pushed together or pulled apart; it's like gas pressure (load per area; sometimes literally psi)▸ "strain" is how far the atoms are being pulled apart, ratio of original length to final length ▸ the strength of a structure is how much load it can take without breaking▸ is is basically impossible to crush normal building materials — you could build a tower into space and the bottom concrete or whatever wouldn't fail by crushing▸ the human body is an insanely awesome structure, human tendons can store 20x more strain energy per unit than modern steel springs▸ simple archery bows are "pre-stressed," meaning bent backwards before being strung — gives you more "stretching distance" and therefore more total available energy to put into the arrow▸ the reason firing a bow without an arrow is bad is because all of that energy has to go through the bow, rather than being released into the arrow▸ it doesn't matter how much stress is in the tip of a crack, it won't propagate (by itself) until it exceeds the "critical Griffith crack length" (which is ~[work of fracture per unit area]/[strain energy stored per unit volume])▸ "plants and animals might be regarded to a considerable extent as so many systems of tubes and bladders whose function is to contain and distribute various liquids and gasses" lmao▸ obviously a sphere is the best way to contain a gas/something with pressure — but this is why sails "billow" out, it more closely approximates a half-sphere and the tension force in the canvas stays mostly constant no matter how hard the wind blows!▸ the surface area of a socket joint doesn't really matter, the stress concentration is usually where the rod enters the socket, no matter how long the rod ▸ rivets stop crack propagation but welds do not — hence, the welded hulls of Liberty Ships splitting in half▸ the reason oil/water tend to separate is that there's an energy advantage: small, well-mixed droplets of oil and water have more surface area to volume, and so there's more surface tension energy; separating into two big sections has less surface area per volume and so has a lower-energy state▸ thrust lines: keep them inside the walls▸ if all else fails, put a statue on top of the wall, it'll help hold up the roof▸ trusses (triangles) also solve this, the whole structure is free-standing and doesn't impart outward force, so it just sits on top of the walls▸ smaller models of big structures can be misleading due to the square-cube law: 2x bigger = 8x heavier▸ the wind in the Nile river blows upstream for most of the year, seriously OP river, makes sense early civilization was there▸

title: Children of Dune
author: Frank Herbert
description: Incredible universe; super lame resolution
memories: Interesting premise, following the kids of Alia who have full knowledge of past lives, the idea of the book being to explore what such voices living in your head can do to you; didn't deliver, really. lots of irrelevant characters introduced just to be killed off in extremely unlikely fashion, totally ignored very interesting parts of the world/story (e.g., Ghanima after she made herself forget about her brother being dead); ending just ended, way too fast; totally ignored everything happening beyond Arrakis, would have been great to learn more about how Spacing Guild or Tlelaxu or other parts of the empire thought about the twins or Alia; the Baron Harkonnen possession of Alia was pretty dumb, should have been more absolute but I guess she needed a foothold to throw herself to her death at the end?; the Laza tigers thing was so silly, training tigers to kill whoever is wearing a certain outfit lol; turning the kid of the former Emperor into a Bene Gesserit ended up not mattering at all? why did they do it?

title: Salems Lot
author: Stephen King
description: Zombies
memories: Really not good, just "vampires" and you know it the whole time. They take over, they win, there's a lot of terrible decisions made that don't make sense and kill major characters, and senseless violence/being evil to babie. My least favorite Stpehn King book

title: Player Piano
author: Kurt Vonnegut
description: We must RETVRN
memories: The world is run by a series of supercomputers, and there's a dude on track to be part of the party leadership. But then he gets sympathetic for the ruled class, and his wife just pressures him to be part of the system (his dad was a big deal), and it escalates into a falling out at this big retreat place where he gets inserted into the revolutionary movement (that he's actually sympathetic to) as a double agent. He gets captured, but there's a huge uprising and he gets freed, before getting surrounded and surrendering.

title: Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?
author: Philip K. Dick
description: What is "humanity" in a world of perfect copies?
memories: Incredible book. One of the best sci-fi books I've ever read. Gripping story about chasing down new humanoids that are basically indistinguishable from humans, the morality of killing them, the meaning of humanity, the will to survive, and so much more. Really interesting characters, from the dummy who shelters the humanoids because he's dumb but loyal (and you get to hear his super dumb thoughts as he can't really process things), to the humanoid who sleeps with bounty hunters to throw them off their game. Lots of huge twists (that humanoid hooker situation; the original thing where they gaslit the main character into almost thinking he got his test wrong...), compelling thoughts, deep deep thinking and implications of robotics getting really good. Loved the thing about people having real animals as pets as a status symbol therefore everyone had to have a pet. The title is very interesting, questions whether robots really want robot pets or real ones. The mirror police station thing was wild too, escaped androids had a little cabal going. The empathy box was a fraud, connecting humanity together in a religion that was just a religion (androids were like HA GOTCHA but humans didn't care). Then the newscaster who was the main nemesis of the religious figure, found evidence to discredit the religion.

title: The Ascent of Man
author: Jacob Bronowski
description: Humanity has come a long way
memories: Here are my (unedited) notes:▸ humanity is unique, because it leaves archaeological evidence not only of what is was, but what it built▸ technology and culture grow hand-in-hand, cultural evolution = widening of collective imagination▸ Emmer wheat is extremely plump/more filling than regular grass, and the predecessor of bread wheat. But it has a huge drawback, its seeds don't naturally spread as well... so it enabled humanity, but also NEEDED humanity to spread itself.▸ You can trace human heritage using blood groups, there were two big migrations over the Siberian land bridge, one of Type O blood folks and one of Type A. There is no prehistoric Type B blood in the Americas.▸ The Gothic arch made different kinds of interior spaces possible, because it takes the load off of the walls, which can then have glass in them▸ Flying buttress was similarly impactful (put the loads outside the building)▸ Bronze is wild — you take soft copper, and add an even softer metal, tin, and get a material harder and more durable than either ingredient it's made of▸ Joseph Priestley was the person who discovered "air" is not a thing, it can decompose into several gasses ("dephlogisticated air"), was the first human to breathe pure oxygen.▸ John Dalton was a good old-fashioned aspie, measured rainfall and temperature every day for 57 years, nothing ever came of that data, but random thinking about it made him the guy that came up with the idea of elementary particles/atomic theory ▸ Pythagoras found that the chords which sound best to the Western ear are those where you divide the length of the string by whole numbers▸ Euclid's "Elements of Geometry" was translated and copied into modern times second-most, only bested by the Bible▸ Lots of Arabic math words: zero, cipher, algebra, almanac, zenith▸ Most bird migration is in the northern, not southern, hemisphere, probably because there's a Pole Star only in the northern sky▸ Copernicus sat on his theory for literally 30 years and only had the courage to publish it on his deathbed▸ Galileo loved telescopes, garnered support for his telescope projects by selling them to merchants who wanted to see ships further in the distance and therefore have advanced commodity pricing data lol▸ wild how much early science was just careful manual observation, e.g., Newton and the prisms, realizing that refraction was pulling stuff out of the initial light rather than creating colors itself▸ okay I'm dumb, every degree of longitude = 4 minutes because days are 24 hours (1440 minutes/360 deg), that's how sailors could know their longitude, compare noon!▸ material decency is the point of growth, just getting everyone coal in an iron range, glass in windows, choice of food, cotton underwear, that's abundance!▸ "The Renaissance established the dignity of man. The Industrial Revolution established the unity of nature." meaning like wind/sun/sea/coal are all "energy"▸ Malthus, objectively pretty lame doomer ideas that have since been disproved, but inspired BOTH of the dudes who independently discovered evolution. Wild. ▸ There are apparently only 7 shapes of crystals in nature?▸ Why are atoms not perpetual motion machines? Quanta!▸ Helium is named after the sun, we discovered it first in a spectrum line during a solar eclipse.▸ Carbon is formed in a star when three Heliums collide at one spot in less than a millionth of a millionth of a second.▸ The smallest atom yet seen is a single atom of Thorium (atomic number 90)▸ Gregor Mendel was a real life Neville Longbottom. He was bad at most other subjects but absolutely loved plants, ended up discovering heredity haha▸ Watson and Crick came up with the idea for a double-chain structure well before they found the double-helix. Solution seeking a problem, they happened to find it.▸ Sexual dimorphism is quite small in humans, our men are very similar to our women.

title: The Technological Republic
author: Alex Karp
description: Tech leaders should engage in politics
memories: I can't tell how much of this book would be new to someone not in tech/not that smart haha. But I knew almost all of the anecdotes/stories that were told here, nothing particularly new. The Palantir bits were shoehorned in, could have done more imo. Fundamental contradiction between having FDEs in the field, direct access to the customer & his thought later that the most important thing is staying a little distant and thinking big broad thoughts. The general idea of a chaotic company that has informal power is interesting, increases the cost of onboarding but maybe keeps people there & is motivational. I wish we actually got Karp's idea of what America is, rather than lots of times he says other people should give their ideas. It seems like his idea is high freedom, copy Silicon Valley at country level? Also America is the only place you don't have to have blood to be a national? We deconstructed and rightfully tore down a bad regime, but had nothing to back it up/replace it with affirmatively.

title: Ubik
author: Philip K. Dick
description: Mind superpowers are unverifiable
memories: Guy runs a telekinesis-cancellation company, communicates with half-dead wife about dilemmas. There's a dude who works there, strug, can't stay un-broke because his apartment charges him microtransactions. Guy gets approached by a lady who can basically go back in time to change the present, he brings her to work with him. Company gets a sus offer to bring all of its people to the moon to stop his enemy... but it's an ambush and it seems like the boss is dead. Eventually you realize that the team is actuallydead and the boss is the only one alive, the team is half-dead and on ice. They are going back in time in the death world, seemingly due to "natural" causes eventually, but they think it's because of the girl being evil; people disintegrate and die from lack of energy basically. Being together keeps you alive. Eventually the boss gets through to them, on one side there's ubik that regenerates things, on the other there's a guy who moves across people in the half-dead ice place to sap on their energy. Battle as old as time apparently. In the end it gets biblical. I like the vibe that you can't really tell whether people are ACTUALLY doing mental prediction powers correctly.

title: Nuclear War
author: Annie Jacobson
description: Deterrence is a religion, not a science
memories: The scenario is simple: North Korea launches two rogue nukes at the US, we fire 100 back but bungle Russia communications, Russia fires 1000 at us and a bunch at NATO, the US fires back at Russia, the world ends. Learned about "Launch on Warning," that we shoot when we get a warning and don't wait for missiles to land. Also the difference between parts of the triad, ICBMs being known in location so important to fire fast. Apparently the US got rid of MIRVs in ICBMs but kept them in the subs because the subs are harder to target... weird. Deterrence doctrine and mythology is crazy. Our missile defense sucks, actually. 50% failure rate for ICBMs!!! And we only have 44 intercept missiles, wtf. We peaked at 31k nukes, down to 3.75k now. There were ~2000 ballistic missiles launched in 2021! Teh highway system was dual use — gave places for helicopter pickup on the highway for nuclear war. "Bolt out of the Blue." Russia apparently will auto-fire their nukes if there's seismic activity and the Russian commanders don't talk to the nukes. We'd lose a lot of ozone + the nuclear winter would make Iowa below freezing for years.

title: After Virtue
author: Alasdair Macintyre
description: Aristotle > analytic philosophy
memories: Analytic philosophy failed, both consequentialism from the utilitarians and Kant's categorical imperative stink; true morality comes from Aristotle's virtue ethics, where there's (1) a practice — meaning like an activity with inherent value you can get IN it, and not just external value like money or fame, he says this would include engineering but not machining basically haha; (2) narrative of a single human life; (3) moral tradition. Says modern moral debates are dumb because there's no meta-framework to judge the claims against each other, so instead we go to post-modern "emotivism" where it's just how you feel about things, nothing actually real, and if emotivism is true then Nietzsche is right and it's just a will to power; current environment is "bureaucratic individualism," either it's individual choice is sovereign or bureaucracy is sovereign, and both sides agree on this; we abandoned the idea of "telos" or the end state of a great man; is DOES imply ought, because who you are sets your role; autonomy means manipulating others to your will, which is a paradox that can't be universalized; corporate leaders don't even succeed in controlling their own companies, they're all asleep at the wheel and have no framework for actually predicting what's going to happen better than anyone else; human behavior is systematically unpredictable (e.g., invention of the wheel thing); in heroic society everyone had their role and their duty to their family; being courageous means people can rely on you; virtues are things that help us achieve the goods inherent within practices; a good life is one in which you pursue the good

title: Iron Gold
author: Pierce Brown
description: War is hell
memories: Feels like Darrow's massive move amounted to nothing except a lot of trauma. War came across as bloody and terrible, not romantic here at all. Governing is harder than overthrowing... and lying is harder than telling the truth. Power and limits of honor; the suicide to try to dissuade from the war was powerful but didn't work. Cassius's sacrifice also didn't really get a chance to land. Lydia's experience in the war was horrific. "If I don't stir the pot, the only opportunities will be the ones they choose to give us."

title: Dark Age
author: Pierce Brown
description: Power over brains is power over all
memories: This book had a cool, totally different theme: about destiny, and roles, and leadership. Mustang has an incredible plan, wins playing politics the right way, and then her evil brother wins by 1) coming back from the dead as a clone, 2) killing a bunch of people indiscriminately, 3) having people he hates around him who worship him, 4) playing to the mob not becauase he believes what they believe, but because he's evil. Evil breaks the rules of good company, which makes it unpredictable to people trying to do things the right way. Lyria got rewarded for being good though, protecting the girl and then sorta rescued, but then correctly calls out her weak-ass parents. The evil of killing Victra's baby is insane, right after she goes Full Gold and gives birth herself, standing up. Shows the uselessness of it, but the grand experiment of Gold which is to show the pinnacle of humanity. Seeing Darrow from Lysander's POV was nuts, Darrow demolished him and didn't think twice about it; Lysander is the other side of virtue though, he's trying his best to be good, like Mustang, but the world is shaped around him (tons of latent supporters for no reason other than blood), which allows him to be magnanimous and self-righteous, his turn dark with the guy at the end will be interesting to track; Ephraim went into one too many missions, matter of time before it bit him; love the Alexandar myth growing in real time, coming to climax, then getting shot in the face for no reason; also Darrow's sons heroism growing. The psychlogical war and counter-techniques are so sick. Fake memories planted, etc.

title: Light Bringer
author: Pierce Brown
description: Don't drink your own kool-aid
memories: Wow, these books are all so different. This one was mostly the Lysander book, tracing his battles to feel and be moral, and then his total collapse into evil (and still trying to justify it to himself). In the beginning he is sick of being useless as Atalantia's husband who runs games, stands up to her because she killed his parents, but is sent into battle to die basically. Lysander is ready to Iron Rain on Mars, but gets poisoned by the Atlas crew. Lyria gives up her Figment power, bummed nothing happened there. She's still a badass though, befriends Cassius and reunites to help Volga come to her senses (even though Volga kills all of those people except her). Lots of very flawed heroes. The final alliance that comes together is insane. Darrow, who destroyed the dockyards; Volga, who enslaved the lowcolors with Fa; the Rim, who were trying to kill Sevro and Darrow in the beginning. Apollonius almost kills Darrow then disappears to Earth? The whole Atlas plot is super cool. Grand scheme, put in motion a long time ago, the attack from within the fleet at Kalyke using the gaunlet and carved arms of the actual pilot, etc. Lysander maintaining the lie just so he has aircover with his allies to destroy the Garter that feeds the Rim is nuts. Cassius went out a great way, fighting for someone he cared about and killing Atlas. The Eidmi biological weapon that Lysander says he's definitely going to use is insane.

title: Bowling Alone
author: Robert Putnam
description: Sociology books have a short half-life
memories: Very weird to see someone talk about the challenges of modernity, when that modernity is the past. Some parallels, mostly its just "they had no idea what was coming." People in the post-war era loved clubs, their kids (boomers) didn't, social downfall. 1964 thought people had a glut of leisure. Greenpeace saw incredibly high membership from sending direct mail, but that was ironic for sustainability so they cut down, and then their membership fell by 85% lol; in 1960, only 41% of American adults graduated from high school and 8% from college; jobless people in the Great Depression became passive and withdrawn; women entering the workforce meant fewer really smart people organized communal events; 80% of men born in the 1920s served, only 10% of men in the 1960s; boomers suck dude — 96% raised religious, 58% abandoned it; "less trustigng, less participatory, more cynical about authorities, more self-centered, more materialistic, less patriotic, money mattered to them more

title: A New Earth
author: Eckhart Tolle
description: Awaken consciousness to get rid of ego
memories: Great start, bad ending, went off the deep end trying to tie in too much science and be too expansive. The core idea is really powerful: that beneath your thoughts and emotions, there is a universal consciousness that is unchanging, peaceful, at ease, always within grasp. He diagnoses lots of modern behaviors as being driven by the ego, aka thoughts and self-identification with thoughts, and those parts of the book were great. He occasionally tried to tie in science stuff which was almost 100% wrong, lost me there; at the end he was going for revolutionary new world order vibes which were also lame. But the core is nice, and I see why people who are less conscientious / conscious will think it's life changing. If humanity was a person, it would be paranoid, murderous, etc.; the most rigid structures (Communism) collapse first; believing you're in sole possession of the truth can make you insane; external forms are unstable; sometimes you gotta flap your wings and let go of excess energy to chill; he calls teh accumulation of past emotional pain the "pain body" which is a quasi-real thing that causes harm in self and others; if small things can disturb you, you are small; let your self be dimished when slighted, live with it, see if it is actually all so bad, rather than justifying why you're right; I am never upset for the reason I think; meditate on your sense of aliveness, not breath; be more concerned with the other person, not what they think of you; awakened doing = "acceptance, enjoyment, enthusiasm" where the last is just goal-directed enjoyment

title: Player of Games
author: Iain M. Banks
description: Absent scarcity, there aren't any motives left
memories: Fantastic story and world; it's a post-scarcity society where anyone can have anyhting they want, basically; you have glands in your brain that let you control your mental and emotional state, you can bypass your stomach with alcohol, you can order any clothes or object you want and it's given for free, there are supercomputer Minds that order society (sometimes in very intelligent ways), and life is carefree and people just follow their interests; the story is about the "Contact" section of the society who has to manage the Culture's relationship with the outside world, other galaxies of intelligent people, e.g., the Empire that the book centers around. The Empire is run by harsh, evil leaders who take lots of joy in the pain of others, e.g., secret gore porn channels and such. They select their leaders by playing a game, there's an awesome game player in the Culture, and Special Circumstances (Contact but for the hardest circumstances) sends him there to win the game and undermine the society, and it works. Lots of commentary on current life through the simple but super compelling story. My favorite one was that the Empire has outlawed slavery (which they're "really proud of") but still you have a job, which to a Culture person sounds like slavery haha, you buy people for money. Crazy climax where the Game Player Gurgeh gets all the way to the reigning leader in the finals of the game, and he's gonna win before the leader kills everyone and tries to assume power with force, but fails when the Contact drone reflects his laser shot back through his head. Turns out SC manipulated Gurgey into wnating to go on the mission, he was a pawn in their grand game.

title: The Great Stagnation
author: Tyler Cowen
description: Best form factor of a book ever
memories: Very solid argument that we ate all the low-hanging fruit and now won't get crazy easy growth anymore; he thinks the internet is small revenue and small employment lol, amazing that smart people thought that in 2011; a home equity loan is not real GDP growth, we are drawing down more on our homes these days

title: The Arsenal of Democracy
author: AJ Baime
description: If only Edsel ran the company
memories: Henry Ford sucked so hard, anti-semitic, almost had it right by appointing Edsel early, but then gummed up the works for a long time, incl. embarrassing his son by not letting him fight in the war and then stopping them from helping in WW2; in 1941 Detroit had a bigger economy than every nation but Britain, France, Germany, and the Soviet Union; in 1940, only 48k out of 132M taxpayers made >$40k in today's dollars; Ford doubled salaries and gave everyone at least $5 per day, had people traveling the country to try to work for him; integrated the production line and hired black workers; Ford was super against WW1 involvement, failed obviously, embarrassed; of the first 40 Air Mail pilots, 31 died in crashes; Nazis absolutely loved Henry for a book he wrote about Nazi production, gave him a medal; hitler: "What is America but millionaires, beauty queens, stupid records, and Hollywood?"; Ypsi grew from 330 to 100,000 people; when we dropped leaflets bragging about Willow Run, it still hadn't produced a single plane; what unlocked it was stopping requirements from shifting; 20x more people injured/killed working on the war effort at home vs soliders overseas; an Army training film for a bombing run started with a picture of a naked girl lol; Germany was completely unable to cope with reality when things shifted against them, "it's the official policy that planes did not reach" a place that was bombed. 325k planes produced!

title: A Scanner Darkly
author: Philip K. Dick
description: Drugs are bad
memories: Guy does drugs as an undercover cop, ends up dissociating, and not realizing that he's investigating himself. Spends a lot of time talking about drugs among burnouts. Scramble suit idea is cool. Heartbreak and longing.

title: A Time to Build
author: Yuval Levin
description: People need to stop playing "outsider" card
memories: Americans don't love institutional mediation, we go around institions not through them, everyone plays "outsider" card; instituitons are places for performance, not formative of us as people; Congress isn't actually doing governance anymore; social media is trial without jury, no rules on the social media elite like for the press informal rules; left thinks in terms of "oppressor, oppressed," and right thinks of "order, disorder"; celebrity combines power and faux intimacy; the secret is devotion

title: The Philosopher in the Valley
author: Michael Steinberger
description: Much is downstream of this man
memories: Karp was apparently woke in college, revolutionary kid that thought the deans needed to do more to fight bigotry; he was 34 when he started as CEO; Colin learned the Palantir recruiting style to hire people who actually care about the mission; they somehow never gave up a board seat; getting out of sales mode and into problem-solving mode is how they made sales; they got accused of discriminating against asians; dunking on Google got him pretty far; in the late 1990s, there were 500-600 IPOs per year, they maintained F shares, 49.999999% of the total vote lol; we killed a lot of people using their phones

title: Atlas Shrugged
author: Ayn Rand
description: Agency is morally good
memories: I just read “Atlas Shrugged” for the first time.It’s a hell of a morality tale, and although the characters are caricatures, you can squint at the villains and see some real-world people alive today.Here are my notes, in case you don’t want to read all 1,168 pages (MASSIVE SPOILERS FOLLOW) —The Unspoken Backstory:‣ a young inventor, John Galt, invents a infinite energy machine‣ but then his bosses become Communists (“from each according to their ability, to each according to their need”), and he sees communistic rot pervading society, so he decides the only moral choice is to quit society and let it burn itself to the ground‣ he teams up with his two other college buddies (Francisco d’Anconia [heir to a copper fortune] and Ragnar Danneskjöld [philosopher turned pirate]) to try to convince all of the world’s top businesspeople, musicians, and smart people to quit society along with them‣ and he builds a Atlantean paradise in the middle of the desert, for all of the defecting businesspeople to live in capitalistic harmony‣ everything above is not explicitly known to the reader for the vast majority of the book, you just hear hints/rumors/legends of these people for about 800 pagesThe Plot:‣ Dagny Taggart is a woman who wants to own a railroad (“Taggart Transcontinental”), but her idiot brother has been put in charge instead; she’s still running it in reality.‣ A new hotshot founder, Ellis Wyatt, has basically invented fracking and is about to become the world’s best oil producer — but chose Taggart’s main competitor over them, thanks to Dagny’s brother’s mismanagement of the company‣ and then, the government strikes. Thanks in part to Dagny’s brothers lobbying, the government passes a rule that effectively regulates Taggart’s competitor railroad out of existence (it’s called the “anti dog-eat-dog rule” and basically says “incumbents win”)‣ Dagny now MUST repair the Rio Norte line, but it’s almost impossible to do so. She only has one lifeline — a steel magnate, Henry Rearden, has just invented a new metal alloy (“Rearden Metal”) that is way stronger/lighter, and can be produced fast enough, to make the Rio Norte line work‣ and then, the government strikes. Rearden Metal is too good, his competitors get scared and lobby against him, and his own lobbyist sells him out — a massive PR campaign drops, with complicity from the “State Science Institute” that baselessly calls Rearden Metal unsafe‣ this makes the Taggart board and Dagny’s brother cower in fear, but she knows the metal is safe from first principles, so eventually she spins off her own company from Taggart to take on all of the PR risk herself‣ the first third of the book ends in a triumphant W for Dagny and Rearden. Dagny finishes the railroad on time, Rearden’s metal works perfectly, the two of them take the first ride out and begin a (raunchy, far-too-descriptively-described) sexual relationship‣ predictably, though, the government strikes a fury of blows. The middle of the book is just a string of increasingly oppressive regulations‣ They now love Rearden Metal, and always loved it of course!! But to be true to their lame, lobbying, communistic principles…‣ they impose selling quotas because “everybody deserves an equal amount of the metal,” which screws over the railroads that need a ton of it, and basically people can only use it for small consumer goods‣ they pass the “Equalization of Opportunity” bill that basically makes vertical integration illegal‣ and then, eventually, they pass the biggest bill of all — Directive 10-289, which says “nothing shall happen ever.” it’s now illegal to fire (or hire) anyone, illegal to produce more (or less) than you did last year, invent anything new, etc.‣ Rearden is eventually blackmailed by the government (using his secret infidelity with Dagny) to force him to “gift” his patent for Rearden Metal to the country / his competitors‣ in response to each of these rules, more and more inventors/businesspeople start to leave society and defect. Wyatt defects and burns his oil fields to the ground when the Equalization of Opportunity bill is passed, for instance.‣ Dagny really, really doesn’t want to quit, and is searching for a sliver of hope. She tours the country and eventually finds the ruins of (basically) Ford, where she finds what looks like an infinite energy machine. She desperately tries to find the creator (and the person who is disappearing her friends), but can’t — even going as far as hijacking a plane and chasing them… but then her plane crashes.‣ she wakes up in Atlantis, aka “Galt’s Gulch,” aka California Forever‣ she spends time there, it’s a paradise where even her favorite composer still lives; she falls in love with Galt, reconciles with d’Anconia and Rearden, and wants to stay… but can’t bear to leave her railroad.‣ she goes back to the world, watches it fully burn down after a 3-hour-long (and ~80 page long) speech by Galt, almost loses Galt to the bad guys and their final Superweapon, but lives. ‣ the world crumbles without its Great Men (gender-neutral), and at the end of the book, the Men are ready to go back into the world and rebuild it from the ground up, without the dumb communists trying to ruin everythingThe Major Themes:‣ the book is unabashedly pro-America, pro-business, pro-Great Men, pro-self interest‣ the morality of the book centers on Reason, Purpose, and Self Esteem as the most important things in life — you have to use your mind, you can’t be an NPC, you have to take control and responsibility for your own life, and if everyone does that, nobody ever has to ask someone else for a handout. To get admitted into the Atlantis-like Gulch, you have to say this oath: "I swear by my life and my love of it that I will never live for the sake of another man, nor ask another man to live for mine." ‣ the heroes of the book are all absurdly heroic. They are hot, strong, smarter than everyone else, and capable. They’re all selfish, but that’s kinda the point. They have various bumbling idiots (e.g., Rearden’s family) passive-aggressively leeching their life force away at all times.‣ and the villains are even more of a caricature. It’s actually hilarious how inept and evil they are. The book is often criticized for this, but it’s a great device for Rand’s point, which is to tell a morality tale. The non-heroes are little, one-dimensional, parable-like demons who have One Huge Fatal Flaw in the way they see the world — the best parts of the book are when you get to meet a lot of them in succession, e.g., on Dagny’s quest to find the motor’s creator‣ Ayn Rand also pulls no punches describing the government. The President is a bumbling idiot who only knows how to speak in soothing generalities. He is variously captured by a few main people — the incompetent but ruthless lobbyist, the sell-out scientist, the business/union representative who’s scared of his own shadow, Dagny’s idiot brother, etc., who are all zero-agency moochers who just want to benefit themselves by leeching from the Men who build things‣ overall, the book spends the vast majority of the time establishes some “rules” by which the evil people live, and then finally inverts them in one (long, too long) speech at the end by Galt. The basic rules are:1) You must feel guilty for any and all human suffering, regardless of your personal involvement in it.2) You must never take responsibility for anything. <- climactically, a guard refuses to even decide whether to let Dagny into Galt’s prison cell thing, she counts down from three then shoots him before he decides3) The needs of society outweigh any financial considerations.4) Making money is bad and selfish.5) There is no virtue in doing things you enjoy, or giving people what they deserve. You must suffer or sacrifice to do good.6) Appearances are all that matter, even if everyone knows the truth behind them; you must never speak those deeper truths.7) Nobody is any good. Everything sucks.8) Unhappiness is a virtue.Ultimately, all of the creators and inventors have their virtues (productivity, morality, etc.) turned against them — until they realize this, and leave society to try to survive without them. Which is what that society claims to want!Overall, I liked it. The story is a fun wrapper to a philosophy text. I would have liked it a lot more if it was 400-800 pages shorter. I heard someone say about the book that “the heroes are fake, but the villains are real,” which seems accurate… It doesn't take a lot of creativity to find examples of the “sell-out scientist” or “‘socially-responsible’ business-owning communist” from our modern world. Only read the whole thing if you’re sadistic, or also on paternity leave. :)

title: Consider Phlebas
author: Iain M. Banks
description: Decent intro to an incredible world
memories: Story was a slog mostly; episodic a la Hitchhiker's Guide, but the individual stories weren't that interesting/suspenseful, and the whole thing was way too long. Make it half as long and it still does its job: introducing the Culture and some of the cool futuristic science. Horza is a Changer, a dude who can change his physical appearance, has poison fingernails, spits venom, etc who is just fighting to stay alive as a mercenary. He starts getting drowned in shit by a Gerontocracy, is rescued by the three legged warrior "bad guy" race, abandoned in space, recovered by some pirates who get into hijinks (failed robbery from a neutral planet that was nevertheless armed to the teeth; failed looting of a megaboat that runs into an iceberg; then they go to a "Damage" card game where the stakes are lives of desperate people and you can send other people emotions they need to deal with), then he kills hm, steals the ship, and tries to steal a Mind (AI brain of a ship) from a dead, icy planet, but gets killed by literally two big baddie tripedal guys. The core story is about allegiance I guess, where he has people he loves and will fight to save/see, and intellectually agrees with one side of the war (bad guys) over the Culture (who he sees as anti-human because they turn their lives over to machines), but ends up being saved by a machine, loving a half-Culture woman, being friends with a Culture agent, and having a ship (the Mind) named after him.

title: Use of Weapons
author: Iain M. Banks
description: Anything's a weapon if you're at war
memories: Cool structure to the book: diverging chapter pairs that start around the same time, then one moves forward while the other hops backwards through prologue; follows (who you believe to be) Zalakwe, a culture special circumstances agent, who is dropped into random wars, the last time to lose for his side and mostly just do a ton of traumatic stuff, maybe it's punishment after all (!), because it's not Zakalwe, it's his cousin, who killed Zakalwe's sister and turned her chair into bones, making the other Zalakwe kill himself; advice unacted upon might always have been right; smaller the ship, weirder the crew lol, they gave each other colds; spaceship that manifests as a cute animal rather than a robot; bad gods have the beaiful faces and soft voices; birds are better at flying, but people still do it; no purpose in building stronger or smarter humans when machines are way better at both, but pleasure is uniquely human
